OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of human immunodeficiency virus and hepatitis C virus coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones. Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation, while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Nail–patella syndrome, Coffin–Siris syndrome, Meier–Gorlin syndrome, Coxopodopatellar syndrome, Genitopatellar syndrome
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Bilateral osseous processes from posterior iliac bones.  
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia with patellar luxation on knee radiographs.  
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature on elbow radiographs.  
- Hypoplastic fingernails on physical examination.  
- No significant mention of other systemic anomalies except HIV and hepatitis C coinfection, which are likely incidental.

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome:  
   - Pros: Classic triad includes hypoplastic or absent nails, patellar hypoplasia or aplasia, and iliac horns (characteristic osseous processes projecting from posterior iliac bones). Elbow anomalies such as radial head hypoplasia and joint dislocations can also be seen.  
   - Cons: None significant - fits all described findings.  

2) Coffin–Siris syndrome:  
   - Pros: Can have nail hypoplasia/aplasia and other congenital anomalies.  
   - Cons: Typically shows hypoplasia/aplasia of the distal phalanges and nail of the fifth digit, with other systemic features such as growth retardation and characteristic facial dysmorphism. No mention of patellar or iliac abnormality described here.  

3) Meier–Gorlin syndrome:  
   - Pros: Features include patellar hypoplasia, microtia, and short stature.  
   - Cons: Nail hypoplasia and iliac horns are not characteristic; no iliac osseous processes typical on imaging; radial anomalies less described.

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:  
   - Pros: Includes patellar and iliac ossification defects.  
   - Cons: Usually also associated with genital and facial anomalies. Iliac anomalies are typically dysplasia rather than classic horns; radial head hypoplasia less characteristic; no mention of nail changes.

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:  
   - Pros: Can have patellar aplasia/hypoplasia and some limb anomalies.  
   - Cons: Usually more severe with multiple systemic involvement, including genitourinary and central nervous system anomalies; iliac horns not typical; nail hypoplasia not prominent.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome (perfect match with nail hypoplasia, patellar hypoplasia and luxation, iliac horns, radial head/radio-humeral anomalies).  
2) Coxopodopatellar syndrome (iliac anomalies and patellar hypoplasia present but radial and nail findings less typical).  
3) Genitopatellar syndrome (patellar involvement but less iliac process and nail hypoplasia).  
4) Meier–Gorlin syndrome (patellar hypoplasia but no nail or iliac findings).  
5) Coffin–Siris syndrome (nail involvement but no iliac or patellar/anterior iliac osseous processes).  

<final> Nail–patella syndrome </final><|return|>
options: Nail–patella syndrome | Coffin–Siris syndrome | Meier–Gorlin syndrome | Coxopodopatellar syndrome | Genitopatellar syndrome

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. DifferentialDiagnosisList: Angiosarcoma, Ig-G4 related disease, Primary cardiac lymphoma, Erdheim–Chester disease, Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Elderly male with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presenting with orthopnoea, exertional dyspnoea.  
- Elevated troponins and third-degree AV block on ECG.  
- Echocardiogram shows right ventricular (RV) thickening and pericardial effusion.  
- PET-CT reveals a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue restricted to the right heart chambers and perivascular spaces, sparing the left ventricle, with right coronary artery cuffing.  
- CT confirms infiltrative hypodense myocardial mass involving right atrium (RA), RV, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove, causing mural thickening and moderate pericardial effusion.  
- Hypermetabolic nodular image at left lung base confirmed as solid nodule via CT chest.  
- The presence of a cardiac mass with infiltration of right heart structures and systemic manifestation (lung nodule) points to a primary cardiac tumor with possible metastasis.  
- Histology from nodular lung lesion pending/not detailed here.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:   
1) Angiosarcoma:  
- Pros: Common primary cardiac malignancy; usually involves right atrium and right ventricle; often causes pericardial effusion; can be hypermetabolic on PET; often associated with lung metastases/nodules; right atrial involvement consistent with site.  
- Cons: Usually, angiosarcomas are highly aggressive and can cause pericardial effusions and conduction disturbances as seen. No major cons identified.  

2) Ig-G4 related disease:  
- Pros: Can infiltrate cardiac tissues; can cause pericardial effusion.  
- Cons: Typically causes more diffuse fibrosis and less focal mass effect; not usually hypermetabolic on PET; usually not associated with nodular lung lesions; mass is more inflammatory/fibrotic, not a discrete hypermetabolic mass; rarely causes AV block directly.  

3) Primary cardiac lymphoma:  
- Pros: Can present as right atrial/right ventricular mass; hypermetabolic on PET; can cause pericardial effusion and AV block due to infiltration.  
- Cons: Typically presents with systemic B symptoms; mass is often bulky but can be large; lymphoma can involve lung; nodular lung lesion can be metastasis. However, primary cardiac lymphomas usually present as a single mass rather than extensive myocardial infiltration involving interatrial septum and AV groove with perivascular cuffing. The clinical context of older patient with atrial fibrillation and conduction block supports it, but less typical pattern than angiosarcoma.  

4) Erdheim–Chester disease:  
- Pros: Rarely involves myocardium; can cause pericardial effusion.  
- Cons: Usually presents with symmetric periaortic and perirenal fibrosis, not focal myocardial masses; not typically hypermetabolic mass in right heart; typically not associated with lung nodules or AV block in this pattern.  

5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma:  
- Pros: Can be primary cardiac sarcoma and cause infiltrative mass; hypermetabolic on PET.  
- Cons: Rarely involves right heart chambers preferentially; location can be anywhere but less common than angiosarcoma; clinical presentation less typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Angiosarcoma: Most consistent with right atrial and ventricular involvement, pericardial effusion, hypermetabolism, and lung nodules.  
2) Primary cardiac lymphoma: Very possible, given hypermetabolic myocardial mass and lung nodule, but less typical infiltration pattern.  
3) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma: Possible but less typical site involvement.  
4) Ig-G4 related disease: Unlikely pattern for focal hypermetabolic mass and lung nodules.  
5) Erdheim–Chester disease: Least likely due to typical disease characteristics.  

Final diagnosis is Angiosarcoma.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Angiosarcoma  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 37-year-old man with high-grade fever, chills, vomiting, epigastric pain.  
- Lab: leukocytosis with neutrophilia, mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase.  
- USG: hepatomegaly, large left lobe hepatic abscess (350 cc), impending rupture.  
- Doppler USG: echogenic content with absent color flow at porto-splenic confluence (suggesting thrombosis), intrahepatic venous collaterals, portal cavernoma/cavernous transformation.  
- CECT: confirms hepatic abscess, thrombosis at porta hepatis, pinpoint IVC narrowing with small thrombus segment.  
- MRCP: prominent intrahepatic bile ducts left lobe, non-visualisation of biliary distal to common hepatic duct = biliary obstruction.  
- Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices noted draining into splenic vein.  
B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
   - Pros: Portal vein thrombosis and cavernous transformation plus varices could suggest cirrhosis.  
   - Cons: No mention of cirrhotic features (e.g., nodular liver surface), liver abscess primary cause, young patient, and pancreatic parenchymal varices are unusual.  
2) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
   - Pros: Intrahepatic venous collaterals could be AVM.  
   - Cons: AVM typically not associated with abscess or pancreatic varices; no dilated feeding arteries described.  
3) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: Portal vein thrombosis (thrombosis at porta hepatis), hepatic abscess, cavernous transformation, and pancreatic parenchymal varices consistent with retrograde collateral flow due to portal hypertension from pylephlebitis.  
   - Cons: Pancreatic varices rare but possible in portal hypertension; fits well with infectious thrombus (pylephlebitis) caused by liver abscess.  
4) Cystic lesions of pancreas  
   - Pros: None mentioned.  
   - Cons: No pancreatic cyst on imaging; pancreatic varices not cystic lesion.  
5) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (IPMN)  
   - Pros: No mention of pancreatic duct abnormality or cystic lesion.  
   - Cons: Pancreatic parenchymal varices not typical feature; no imaging evidence.  
  
C) Ranking:  
1) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
2) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
3) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms  
5) Cystic lesions of pancreas  
<final> Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 31-year-old male with large heterogeneous right hemiscrotal mass, no palpable normal testis or epididymis on that side.  
- Ultrasound shows solid and cystic/necrotic components with vascularisation; left testis normal.  
- Serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) >53,000 µg/L (extremely high).  
- CT shows no metastases or lymphadenopathy.  
- Clinical features consistent with testicular tumor.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
- Mixed germ cell tumour: 
  - Pros: Common testicular tumor, often heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic areas.  
  - Cons: Typically mixed tumors may have varied tumor markers; pure yolk sac tumor is a component but mixed tumors may have other subtypes and patterns. AFP can be elevated but not always this dramatically high.  
- Pure yolk sac tumour:  
  - Pros: Highly elevated AFP >53,000 µg/L is classic for yolk sac tumor; large heterogeneous mass with necrotic/cystic areas typical. Young adult male fits age.  
  - Cons: Pure yolk sac tumors less common than mixed tumors in testis; however, AFP level is a strong clue.  
- Seminoma:  
  - Pros: Common testicular tumor, solid homogeneous mass often vascularized.  
  - Cons: Seminomas rarely elevate AFP; AFP usually normal, or mildly elevated with mixed features, but not extremely high; homogeneous, not heterogeneous cystic necrotic.  
- Choriocarcinoma:  
  - Pros: Can have high tumor markers but typically beta-hCG, not AFP.  
  - Cons: AFP not elevated; choriocarcinoma usually small, hemorrhagic.  
- Teratoma:  
  - Pros: Can be heterogeneous with cystic and solid components.  
  - Cons: AFP not typically markedly elevated unless there is a yolk sac component.  
- Lymphoma:  
  - Pros: Can present as testicular mass.  
  - Cons: Usually homogeneous, no cystic necrosis; AFP not elevated; age and sex less typical.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- Pure yolk sac tumour: AFP >53,000 is pathognomonic; solid and cystic heterogeneous mass typical; no metastasis yet, but large mass fits classic presentation.  
- Mixed germ cell tumour: possible, but AFP level especially high favors pure yolk sac; also no mention of other tumor markers or elements.  
- Other tumors less likely due to AFP level and imaging features.  

Thus, the strongest fit is Pure yolk sac tumour.  
</analysis><final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left
 
 hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI
 
 revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 1d). Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted (Figure 1d).
 
 CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left (Figure 2).
 
 CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid
 
 internal carotid arteries (
 
 ICAs) (Figure 3). The left
 
 middle
 
 cerebral artery (
 
 MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left
 
 anterior cerebral artery (
 
 ACA) was perfused through the
 
 anterior communicating (
 
 ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left
 
 posterior cerebral artery (
 
 PCA) was identified.
 
 Digital subtraction angiography (
 
 DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance (Figures 4a and 4b) and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale. Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted. CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left. CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries (ICAs). The left middle cerebral artery (MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left anterior cerebral artery (ACA) was perfused through the anterior communicating (ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left posterior cerebral artery (PCA) was identified. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events, Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement, Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia, ICA dissection causing ischemia, Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- MRI shows acute watershed infarcts in right centrum semiovale.  
- Ivy sign (sulcal hyperintensity) in right temporal region on MRI, which indicates leptomeningeal collateral circulation.  
- CT shows hypoplastic left internal carotid artery canal.  
- CTA and DSA confirm left ICA hypoplasia and bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenosis.  
- Left MCA supplied via posterior circulation, left ACA via ACom artery.  
- Fetal origin of left PCA noted.  
- No "puff of smoke" collateral vessels on DSA, indicating absence of classic Moyamoya collaterals.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events  
   - Pros: Bilateral ICA stenosis present, watershed infarcts compatible with hypoperfusion; ivy sign can be seen in chronic hypoperfusion states like atherosclerotic disease.  
   - Cons: ICA hypoplasia is a congenital anomaly, not atherosclerotic. Young age (39) less typical for severe atherosclerosis. Absence of typical atherosclerotic plaque noted on imaging. Bilateral symmetrical stenosis with hypoplasia is less typical. Fetal origin PCA also more commonly seen in congenital anomalies.  

2) Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement  
   - Pros: Bilateral ICA stenosis with abnormal collateral circulation (ivy sign), watershed infarcts, Moyamoya syndrome can be 'primary' when no known cause.  
   - Cons: No "puff of smoke" collateral vessels on DSA, which is a hallmark of Moyamoya disease/syndrome. Ivy sign present but no extensive basal collaterals; ICA hypoplasia described rather than progressive stenosis with collaterals.  

3) Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia  
   - Pros: ICA hypoplasia plus bilateral ICA stenosis; Moyamoya disease typically involves progressive stenosis of distal ICA and its branches; presence of alternate collaterals (left MCA via posterior circulation, left ACA via ACom) support collateralization. Ivy sign supports collateral flow.  
   - Cons: Absence of classic Moyamoya collateral networks ("puff of smoke") on DSA argues against full-blown Moyamoya disease (which requires presence of these collaterals as diagnostic criterion). However, early/atypical stage or variant might show hypoplasia with limited collaterals.  

4) ICA dissection causing ischemia  
   - Pros: ICA stenosis could be due to dissection, leading to ischemic events.  
   - Cons: Hypoplastic ICA canal on CT suggests congenital hypoplasia rather than acute dissection. No imaging signs of dissection (e.g., intimal flap, double lumen). Clinical presentation bilateral and chronic in nature, not acute dissection picture.

   - Pros: Bilateral ICA stenosis could occur in vasculitis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

1) Extragonadal germ cell tumour
   - Pros: Could occur in pelvis; can enhance and be cystic.
   - Cons: Usually not a fat attenuation mass; typically solid tumors with calcification and heterogeneous enhancement; no mention of typical germ cell tumor features; location unusual (ischiorectal fossa is not a common site); fat attenuation mass unlikely.

2) Ischiorectal liposarcoma
   - Pros: Fat attenuation mass location matches; can develop soft tissue enhancing components over time; thick irregular rim enhancement and septations possible with progression; location is right ischiorectal fossa.
   - Cons: Typically, liposarcoma shows solid nodular enhancing components from the start; cystic areas can develop but usually mixed solid-cystic; presence of well-defined cystic compartments less typical; however, size increase and enhancement over 5 years suggest malignant transformation.

3) Dermoid cyst
   - Pros: Usually cystic with fat-fluid levels; common in areas with embryological remnants; can have fat attenuation mass; can enlarge and develop complex internal contents.
   - Cons: Usually benign, slow-growing; not commonly found in ischiorectal fossa; typically shows fat-fluid levels and calcified components; MR findings of mixed fluid and soft tissue with thick irregular rim enhancement less typical.

4) Tailgut cyst
   - Pros: Classic location in presacral/retrorectal/posterior rectal region; can be multiloculated, cystic; can contain fluid and soft tissue; may enhance irregularly; can enlarge and become complex over time.
   - Cons: Usually does not have fat attenuation mass; typically not fat density but cystic with proteinaceous content; but initial fat attenuation here might be due to inclusion of fat within cyst; more commonly hypodense with fluid or proteinaceous material, not true fat attenuation.

5) Perineal epidermal cyst
   - Pros: Can be subcutaneous, cystic; usually well-defined.
   - Cons: Usually a simple cystic lesion without fat attenuation; does not typically enlarge dramatically or develop enhancing soft tissue rim; location is unusual for perineal epidermal cyst; no mention of midline location.

6) Anterior sacral meningocele
   - Pros: Occurs in presacral region; cystic mass;
   - Cons: Typically does not have fat attenuation; usually cerebrospinal fluid density (water attenuation, ~0 HU), no thick irregular rim enhancement; patient aged 40; typically congenital; CT and MR findings not typical.

2) Tailgut cyst: Location matches classic presacral area; complex cystic nature supports this, but initial fat attenuation less typical.
3) Dermoid cyst: Fat attenuation common but location atypical and enhancement features less typical.
4) Extragonadal germ cell tumour: Location and fat density less typical.
5) Perineal epidermal cyst: Unlikely given fat attenuation and size progression with enhancement.
6) Anterior sacral meningocele: Unlikely due to CT density and enhancement pattern.

Thus, the best diagnosis is Ischiorectal liposarcoma.

</analysis><final>Ischiorectal liposarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

- MRI shows a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall:

  -- No diffusion restriction and no significant enhancement of soft tissue components.  
- Blood work mildly elevated CRP and CK, no other abnormalities.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb)  
   - Pros: Complex cystic lesion with daughter cysts (small cysts within the lesion), characteristic septation pattern with radiating septa, thin wall. T2 hypointense septa can be seen due to calcifications or fibrous tissue. Lack of significant enhancement and no diffusion restriction also fit.  
   - Cons: No mention of classic "hydatid sand" or calcifications, no serology confirmatory test described. However, MRI appearance is quite typical.  

2) Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis  
   - Pros: Patient has malignancy (follicular lymphoma), which can metastasize to liver. Necrotic metastases can appear heterogeneous, cystic.  
   - Cons: Follicular lymphoma rarely metastasizes to liver as cystic lesions; usually more solid lesions. Lack of diffusion restriction and lack of enhancement argue against necrotic metastasis. The lesion is not described as solid or infiltrative, more cystic with daughter cysts.  

3) Hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: Fever of unknown origin, weight loss, elevated CRP, large fluid density lesion, complex cystic structure with septations.  
   - Cons: Typically abscesses show restricted diffusion, wall enhancement, and often clinical signs of infection. Here, no diffusion restriction or significant enhancement, and fibro-hyalinised nodule in biopsy suggests chronic or inactive process rather than active suppurative infection.  

4) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma  
   - Pros: Biliary cystic neoplasms often shows multiloculated cystic lesion with septations and mural nodularity.  
   - Cons: Usually shows enhancement of septa or mural nodules; here, no significant enhancement is noted. Also, the imaging feature of radiating septa outlining smaller cysts is more characteristic of hydatid disease than cystadenoma.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb) appears most consistent with a multiloculated cystic lesion with daughter cysts and characteristic T1/T2 signal of septa (hypointense).  
2) Cystic/necrotic metastasis is less likely due to lack of enhancement/diffusion restriction and atypical for follicular lymphoma.  
3) Hepatic abscess less likely due to lack of restricted diffusion and absence of typical signs of infection.  
4) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma less consistent due to lack of enhancement.  

Thus, the diagnosis that best matches the imaging features and clinical context is hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb).

- Patient is a 55-year-old female with follicular lymphoma, weight loss, fever of unknown origin.  
- Imaging revealed a large (7.5 x 5 cm), heterogeneous lesion in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe, with fluid densities and lobulated margins.  
- Ultrasound-guided biopsy was unrepresentative, showing fibro-hyalinised nodule.  
- MRI shows a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall:  
  -- T2 hypointense lesion with radiating septa that are also hypointense on T2 and hyperintense on T1, delineating smaller cysts that are T2 hyperintense and T1 hypointense.  
  -- No diffusion restriction and no significant enhancement of soft tissue components.  
- Blood work mildly elevated CRP and CK, no other abnormalities.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb)  
   - Pros: Complex cystic lesion with daughter cysts (small cysts within the lesion), characteristic septation pattern with radiating septa, thin wall. T2 hypointense septa can be seen due to calcifications or fibrous tissue. Lack of significant enhancement and no diffusion restriction also fit.  
   - Cons: No mention of classic "hydatid sand" or calcifications, no serology confirmatory test described. However, MRI appearance is quite typical.  

2) Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis  
   - Pros: Patient has malignancy (follicular lymphoma), which can metastasize to liver. Necrotic metastases can appear heterogeneous, cystic.  
   - Cons: Follicular lymphoma rarely metastasizes to liver as cystic lesions; usually more solid lesions. Lack of diffusion restriction and lack of enhancement argue against necrotic metastasis. The lesion is not described as solid or infiltrative, more cystic with daughter cysts.  

3) Hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: Fever of unknown origin, weight loss, elevated CRP, large fluid density lesion, complex cystic structure with septations.  
   - Cons: Typically abscesses show restricted diffusion, wall enhancement, and often clinical signs of infection. Here, no diffusion restriction or significant enhancement, and fibro-hyalinised nodule in biopsy suggests chronic or inactive process rather than active suppurative infection.  

4) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma  
   - Pros: Biliary cystic neoplasms often shows multiloculated cystic lesion with septations and mural nodularity.  
   - Cons: Usually shows enhancement of septa or mural nodules; here, no significant enhancement is noted. Also, the imaging feature of radiating septa outlining smaller cysts is more characteristic of hydatid disease than cystadenoma.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb) appears most consistent with a multiloculated cystic lesion with daughter cysts and characteristic T1/T2 signal of septa (hypointense).  
2) Cystic/necrotic metastasis is less likely due to lack of enhancement/diffusion restriction and atypical for follicular lymphoma.  
3) Hepatic abscess less likely due to lack of restricted diffusion and absence of typical signs of infection.  
4) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma less consistent due to lack of enhancement.  

Thus, the diagnosis that best matches the imaging features and clinical context is hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb).
options: Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb) | Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis | Hepatic abscess | Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

A) Key findings:  
- One-year-old male with thoracic deformity  
- Chest AP radiograph: absent ribs in right mid-zone with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs

- Dextroscoliosis of thoracic spine

1) Spondylocostal dysostosis (Jarcho–Levin syndrome)  
   - Pros: Presence of vertebral segmentation anomalies (posterior vertebral element fusion defects), rib anomalies (bifid ribs, segmental fusion)  
   - Cons: Typically shows anterior rib defects or absent ribs rather than posterior rib defects or bifid ribs; volume loss may be mild but is described; fusion defects may be milder  

2) Spondylothoracic dysostosis (Lavy–Moseley syndrome)  
   - Pros: Characterized by multiple vertebral segmentation defects, prominent "fan-like" or "crab-like" rib anomalies (costal anomalies with bifid ribs and segmental fusion), severe thoracic deformity, posterior vertebral anomalies, and volume loss of hemithorax  
   - Cons: The presence of bifid ribs fits well with this diagnosis; the posterior vertebral arch defects are consistent  
   - This diagnosis better accounts for the described bifid and segmentally fused ribs, posterior vertebral arch defects, and mild hemithorax volume loss  

C) Ranking:  
1) Spondylothoracic dysostosis (Lavy–Moseley syndrome) - fits best with posterior vertebral arch defects, bifid and segmentally fused ribs bilaterally, and hemithorax volume loss  
2) Spondylocostal dysostosis (Jarcho–Levin syndrome) - less consistent with bifid ribs and posterior vertebral arch defects  

Based on the presence of bifid ribs, posterior vertebral arch fusion defects, and associated thoracic deformities with hemithorax volume loss, the diagnosis of spondylothoracic dysostosis (Lavy–Moseley syndrome) is most compatible.

</analysis><final>  
Spondylothoracic dysostosis (Lavy–Moseley syndrome)  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An otherwise healthy 22-year-old man presents with acute respiratory symptoms and has an emergency CT scan performed, which reveals an incidental finding.
 Imaging Findings: No abnormalities are evident on a PA radiograph of the chest (Figure 1). The CT reveals no thoracic abnormalities except for two bilateral cortical defects of the undersurface of the sternal extremity of each clavicle, being slightly larger on the left side (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). An MRI is additionally performed, revealing no evidence of a pathological fracture, soft tissue mass or bone marrow oedema (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: No abnormalities are evident on a PA radiograph of the chest. DifferentialDiagnosisList: Osteolytic metastasis, Osteomyelitis, Plasmacytoma, Giant cell tumour, Rhomboid fossa variant
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 22-year-old healthy male with acute respiratory symptoms.  
- Chest PA radiograph normal.  
- CT shows bilateral cortical defects (erosions) undersurface of the sternal extremity of each clavicle, slightly larger on left.  
- MRI shows no pathological fracture, soft tissue mass, or bone marrow edema (i.e., no active lesion or inflammation).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Osteolytic metastasis:  
  Pro: Cortical defects and bone changes can be metastatic lesions.  
  Con: Patient is young (22), no primary malignancy reported, and MRI shows no soft tissue mass or marrow edema. Metastasis usually presents with marrow involvement or soft tissue mass.  
- Osteomyelitis:  
  Pro: Cortical defects could be infection.  
  Con: No inflammatory signs on MRI, no bone marrow edema, no clinical signs or lab markers mentioned.  
- Plasmacytoma:  
  Pro: Lytic cortical lesions possible.  
  Con: Usually seen in older patients; MRI would show marrow involvement; no soft tissue mass; no systemic myeloma signs.  
- Giant cell tumour:  
  Pro: Typically lytic lesion, often epiphyseal or metaphyseal.  
  Con: Usually seen in post-pubertal patients but with aggressive, expansile lesions and often soft tissue components; MRI would show marrow changes and possible soft tissue mass; no such findings here.  
- Rhomboid fossa variant:  
  Pro: Known bilateral symmetrical cortical defect of the undersurface of the sternal end of the clavicle; benign anatomical variant.  
  Con: None; fits perfectly with imaging and lack of pathological signs.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Rhomboid fossa variant - pathognomonic bilateral symmetrical cortical defects undersurface of sternal clavicle, no marrow or soft tissue changes.  
2) Osteolytic metastasis - less likely due to no malignant features or marrow changes.  
3) Osteomyelitis - unlikely without inflammation or marrow edema.  
4) Plasmacytoma - less likely due to absence of marrow signal changes.  
5) Giant cell tumour - unlikely given lack of aggressive features and clinical context.  
The rhomboid fossa variant is a classic benign anatomical variant that fits imaging and clinical context precisely.  
</analysis><final>Rhomboid fossa variant</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

A) Key findings:  
- Omphalocele with liver and bowel herniation through a membrane-covered defect.  
- Bladder exstrophy.  
- Imperforate anus.  
- Spinal anomalies: fusion defects in lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with cystic mass containing neural elements (likely myelomeningocele).  
- Ectopic kidneys (one thoracic, one pelvic).  
- Midgut malrotation.  
- Rib synostosis, sacral agenesis, and pubic symphysis diastasis.  
- Ambiguous genitalia.  
- Cranial anomalies: low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, foramen magnum fullness, possible aqueductal stenosis.  
- Musculoskeletal abnormalities.  
- Neonatal presentation with respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.  

B) Candidates:  
1) VACTERL association  
Pros: Includes vertebral anomalies, anal atresia, cardiac defects (not mentioned here), tracheoesophageal fistula (not mentioned), renal anomalies, and limb defects.  
Cons: No omphalocele or bladder exstrophy; omphalocele is more typical of other syndromes; bladder exstrophy is not a VACTERL defining feature; spinal dysraphism more extensive here than typical VACTERL.  

2) Caudal regression syndrome  
Pros: Lower spinal/sacral agenesis and limb anomalies are hallmark.  
Cons: Does not include omphalocele, bladder exstrophy, or imperforate anus; spinal defects are more caudal with potential for more severe lower body anomalies but omphalocele is not typical; cranial anomalies rarely prominent.  

3) Pentalogy of Cantrell  
Pros: Includes omphalocele, ectopia cordis, anterior diaphragmatic defects, sternal defects, and intracardiac anomalies.  
Cons: No mention of thoracic wall defects, ectopia cordis, or diaphragmatic defects; bladder exstrophy and spinal dysraphism not typical; imperforate anus not characteristic.  

4) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome  
Pros: Omphalocele and organomegaly common.  
Cons: Includes macroglossia, hemihypertrophy, neonatal hypoglycemia; bladder exstrophy, spinal fusion defects, imperforate anus, ectopic kidneys not typical; ambiguous genitalia less commonly described.  

5) OEIS complex (Omphalocele, Cloacal exstrophy, Imperforate anus, and Spinal dysraphism)  
Pros: Classic features include omphalocele, bladder exstrophy (variant: cloacal exstrophy), imperforate anus, spinal dysraphism with meningomyelocele, renal anomalies, ambiguous genitalia; fits the description perfectly. Consistent with extensive multi-system involvement.  
Cons: None significant, matches findings well.  

C) Ranking:  
1) OEIS complex - matches all major features (omphalocele, bladder exstrophy/cloacal exstrophy, imperforate anus, spinal dysraphism).  
2) VACTERL association - shares many features but lacks bladder exstrophy/omphalocele and spinal dysraphism is less extensive.  
3) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome - omphalocele present but key features missing; exstrophy rare.  
4) Caudal regression syndrome - spinal and sacral anomalies present but not the full syndrome features.  
5) Pentalogy of Cantrell - less likely given missing characteristic thoracic and cardiac defects.  

Conclusion: The imaging and clinical features best fit OEIS complex.  
</analysis>  
<final>OEIS (omphalocele, cloacal exstrophy, imperforate anus, and spinal dysraphism) complex</final><|return|>

- Omphalocele with liver and bowel herniation through a membrane-covered defect.  
- Bladder exstrophy.  
- Imperforate anus.  
- Spinal anomalies: fusion defects in lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with cystic mass containing neural elements (likely myelomeningocele).  
- Ectopic kidneys (one thoracic, one pelvic).  
- Midgut malrotation.  
- Rib synostosis, sacral agenesis, and pubic symphysis diastasis.  
- Ambiguous genitalia.  
- Cranial anomalies: low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, foramen magnum fullness, possible aqueductal stenosis.  
- Musculoskeletal abnormalities.  
- Neonatal presentation with respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.  

B) Candidates:  
1) VACTERL association  
Pros: Includes vertebral anomalies, anal atresia, cardiac defects (not mentioned here), tracheoesophageal fistula (not mentioned), renal anomalies, and limb defects.  
Cons: No omphalocele or bladder exstrophy; omphalocele is more typical of other syndromes; bladder exstrophy is not a VACTERL defining feature; spinal dysraphism more extensive here than typical VACTERL.  

2) Caudal regression syndrome  
Pros: Lower spinal/sacral agenesis and limb anomalies are hallmark.  
Cons: Does not include omphalocele, bladder exstrophy, or imperforate anus; spinal defects are more caudal with potential for more severe lower body anomalies but omphalocele is not typical; cranial anomalies rarely prominent.  

3) Pentalogy of Cantrell  
Pros: Includes omphalocele, ectopia cordis, anterior diaphragmatic defects, sternal defects, and intracardiac anomalies.  
Cons: No mention of thoracic wall defects, ectopia cordis, or diaphragmatic defects; bladder exstrophy and spinal dysraphism not typical; imperforate anus not characteristic.  

4) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome  
Pros: Omphalocele and organomegaly common.  
Cons: Includes macroglossia, hemihypertrophy, neonatal hypoglycemia; bladder exstrophy, spinal fusion defects, imperforate anus, ectopic kidneys not typical; ambiguous genitalia less commonly described.  

5) OEIS complex (Omphalocele, Cloacal exstrophy, Imperforate anus, and Spinal dysraphism)  
Pros: Classic features include omphalocele, bladder exstrophy (variant: cloacal exstrophy), imperforate anus, spinal dysraphism with meningomyelocele, renal anomalies, ambiguous genitalia; fits the description perfectly. Consistent with extensive multi-system involvement.  
Cons: None significant, matches findings well.  

C) Ranking:  
1) OEIS complex - matches all major features (omphalocele, bladder exstrophy/cloacal exstrophy, imperforate anus, spinal dysraphism).  
2) VACTERL association - shares many features but lacks bladder exstrophy/omphalocele and spinal dysraphism is less extensive.  
3) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome - omphalocele present but key features missing; exstrophy rare.  
4) Caudal regression syndrome - spinal and sacral anomalies present but not the full syndrome features.  
5) Pentalogy of Cantrell - less likely given missing characteristic thoracic and cardiac defects.  

Conclusion: The imaging and clinical features best fit OEIS complex.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ.
 
 On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

   - Cons: None significant; findings are pathognomonic for this entity.

2) Acromioclavicular cyst (close but less specific due to absence of rotator cuff tear explanation)  
3) Degenerative acromioclavicular joint changes (explains joint degeneration but not mass or fluid collection)  
4) Acromioclavicular joint traumatic injury (history and imaging argue against trauma)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp (Figure 1).
 
 Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing
 
 a “filarial dance”
 
 sign (Figures 2 and 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp. Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing a “filarial dance” sign.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dirofilariasis, Epidermal inclusion cyst, Cysticercosis, Foreign body granuloma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 71-year-old woman with fall and history of recurrent swelling and itching over left temporal scalp for 6 months.  
- CT brain normal for trauma, but shows a thick-walled cystic lesion in the left temporal subcutaneous plane.  
- Ultrasound shows a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures with echogenic walls.  
- Ultrasound shows "filarial dance" sign, which is a classic ultrasound finding for live filarial worms (Wuchereria bancrofti typically) in a cystic lesion, often in lymphatics or subcutaneous tissues.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Dirofilariasis:  
  - Pros: Also a filarial infection that can cause subcutaneous nodules.  
  - Cons: Less common in humans compared to lymphatic filariasis; typically presents as a subcutaneous nodule with a single worm; ultrasound mobility and "filarial dance" also seen but more classically with bancroftian filariasis.  
- Epidermal inclusion cyst:  
  - Pros: Common subcutaneous cystic lesion; can cause swelling.  
  - Cons: No mobile tubular structures; no "filarial dance" sign; would show keratin debris on US, not mobile echogenic tubular structures.  
- Cysticercosis:  
  - Pros: Can cause cystic lesions with mobile scolex visible on US or MRI.  
  - Cons: Usually found in brain, muscle, subcutaneous tissues; scolex shows an eccentric echogenic focus with central hypoechogenic area; tubular structures are not typical; no "filarial dance" sign.  
- Foreign body granuloma:  
  - Pros: Can present as subcutaneous mass.  
  - Cons: No mobile tubular structures; no "filarial dance" sign; no cystic lesion with active movement.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Dirofilariasis: Has subcutaneous cystic lesion with tubular mobile structures, but "filarial dance" sign classically described with lymphatic filariasis (Wuchereria bancrofti). However, dirofilariasis can present similarly, especially in soft tissues.  
2) Cysticercosis: Has cystic lesions with scolex, but scolex is different from tubular structures and no "filarial dance" sign.  
3) Epidermal inclusion cyst: No mobile structures.  
4) Foreign body granuloma: No mobile structures.  

Considering the "filarial dance" sign is classic for active mobile filarial worms (lymphatic filariasis), but the lesion is subcutaneous and the clinical scenario fits with subcutaneous filarial infection, the best diagnosis is Dirofilariasis.  

</analysis>  
<final>  
Dirofilariasis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An
 
 orthopantomogram
 
 showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion (Figure 1). A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a
 
 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An orthopantomogram showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion. A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus, compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis. During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table, also consistent with osteomas.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cemento-osseous dysplasia, Osteomatosis, Odontomas, Gardner’s syndrome, Hypercementosis, Hereditary multiple exostoses, Fibrous dysplasia
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- An 18-year-old female with persistent primary teeth, mainly in mandible.  
- Palpable left pre-auricular mass and forehead mass.  
- Imaging (orthopantomogram and CT) shows large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandibular ramus compatible with osteoma.  
- Multiple well-delineated mature bone lesions between roots (hypercementosis).  
- Multiple small sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from outer table of skull bones (also osteomas).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- **Cemento-osseous dysplasia:** This typically occurs in the jaw, involving fibro-osseous tissue, and often presents as periapical radiolucent to radiopaque lesions near tooth apices. It does not cause osteoma-like exophytic lesions on the skull or mandibular ramus, nor multiple osteomas on the skull. Hypercementosis may be present but not osteomas. PARTIAL MATCH, but osteomas on skull are not typical.  
- **Osteomatosis:** General term for increased bone density, not specific. Osteomatosis would show diffuse increased bone density but not characteristic multiple separate exophytic osteomas.  
- **Odontomas:** These are odontogenic tumors, appearing as radiopaque bodies resembling teeth. Not consistent with osteomas on skull nor hypercementosis.  
- **Gardner’s syndrome:** A variant of familial adenomatous polyposis with characteristic multiple osteomas of craniofacial bones (including mandible and skull), hypercementosis, and multiple odontogenic keratocysts. Associated with persistent primary teeth, multiple osteomas on mandible and skull, hypercementosis, and soft tissue masses. This matches the findings well.  
- **Hypercementosis:** Overgrowth of cementum on tooth roots, noted in imaging but does not explain multiple osteomas or skull lesions. Not sufficient alone.  
- **Hereditary multiple exostoses:** Multiple osteochondromas predominantly in long bones, rarely in craniofacial bones. Cranial osteomas are not typical.  
- **Fibrous dysplasia:** Usually presents as ground-glass or radiolucent lesions in craniofacial bones, not exophytic osteomas. Does not explain hypercementosis or multiple osteomas.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Gardner’s syndrome (matches osteomas on mandible and skull, hypercementosis, persistent primary teeth).  
2) Campton-osseous dysplasia (partial features but does not explain skull osteomas).  
3) Hereditary multiple exostoses (less consistent with craniofacial involvement).  
4) Fibrous dysplasia (different lesion appearance).  
5) Odontoma (different lesion type).  
6) Hypercementosis alone (explains one feature but not osteomas).  
7) Osteomatosis (nonspecific). 

</analysis><final>Gardner’s syndrome</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm
 
 (ap/axial/cc) (
 
 rrows in Figures 1
 
 and
 
 2). Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level (
 
 small arrow in Figure 2
 
 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-
 
 enhancing
 
 fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe (arrows on Figure 3).
PostDescription:

4) Suppurative thyroiditis:  
   - Pros: Fistulous tract connecting to thyroid pole could indicate infection of thyroid.  
   - Cons: Primary lesion is retropharyngeal, not thyroid; thyroid involvement is secondary via fistula, making this less likely primary diagnosis.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst - fits best given fistulous tract from retropharynx to thyroid lobe and abscess formation.  
2) Retropharyngeal abscess - also present but the fistulous tract points to branchial origin.  
3) Suppurative thyroiditis - possible but lesion origin is retropharynx, making it secondary.  
4) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy - less likely due to absence of nodal abnormality description.  
</analysis>

Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

- MRI with hepatobiliary contrast shows lesion 1 (34 mm) infiltrative, rectangular, capsular retraction, T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, no enhancement, no fat, no diffusion restriction.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Hepatoblastoma  
- Pros: Common pediatric liver tumor, typically T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, can show capsular retraction; usually enhancing but not always early.  
- Cons: Usually show arterial enhancement and washout on hepatobiliary MRI; this case has no enhancement, which is unusual. The age (2 days) is early but possible. Infiltrative, capsular retraction is classic. The presence of multifocal lesions also fits with hepatic mets, but no enhancement less typical.  

2) Lymphoma  
- Pros: Can be infiltrative and hypovascular; rare in neonates.  
- Cons: Diffusion restriction typically seen, not present here. Unusual in neonates. No clinical history suggestive of lymphoma.  

3) Infantile myofibromatosis  
- Pros: Can present as soft tissue and visceral tumors in neonates; cutaneous lesion present; can involve liver.  
- Cons: Usually T1 isointense or hypointense; T2 hyperintense may be variable. Usually shows enhancement due to vascularity. Infiltrative with capsular retraction less typical. Imaging often shows solid vascular masses.

- Pros: Can metastasize to liver, causing hypovascular lesions in neonates; can be hyperintense T2.  
- Cons: Primary neuroblastoma sites not liver; liver metastases usually hypervascular with enhancement; no mention of primary site; diffuse lack of enhancement and no diffusion restriction less typical; age is very young but possible.  

5) Haemangiomata (infantile hepatic hemangioma)  
- Pros: Common neonatal liver tumor, T2 hyperintense, T1 hypointense or isointense; multiple lesions seen.  
- Cons: Typically hyperenhancing in arterial phase; however, large lesion may undergo changes and stop enhancing (involution), but usually enhancement persists early. Also capsular retraction less common. Infiltrative appearance less typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Hepatoblastoma: Most likely given age, infiltrative appearance, capsular retraction, T1 hypointensity, T2 hyperintensity; lack of enhancement is unusual but may represent early stage or technical factor.  
2) Infantile myofibromatosis: Could explain cutaneous lesion and liver involvement, but imaging findings less classic, especially lack of enhancement.  
3) Haemangiomata: Multiple lesions, typical signal, but absence of enhancement arguing against classic haemangioma unless involuted phase.  
4) Lymphoma: No diffusion restriction, unusual in neonate.  
5) Neuroblastoma: No primary site or typical metastasis pattern.  

Final choice: Hepatoblastoma  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Hepatoblastoma  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman with a known history of retroperitoneal sarcoma, who was receiving radiotherapy, was referred for evaluation of newly palpable lumps in her left breast and left axillary region.
 Imaging Findings: A diagnostic bilateral mammogram (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d; previous mammogram is not available for comparison) shows two circumscribed masses in the left breast upper-outer quadrant.
 
 On the ultrasound of the left breast and axilla (Figures 2a and 2b), there are two adjacent hypo-echoic masses with circumscribed borders in the left breast at 2
 
 o’clock; m
 
 oreover, there is a similar mass in the left axillary region.
 
 The abdominal CT scan with contrast (Figures 3a and 3b) shows a large heterogeneously enhancing mass in the retroperitoneal space with left kidney invasion and aortic wall encasement.
 
 The histopathologic exam reveals highly atypical spindle cells with large pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei and high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio (Figure 4).
PostDescription:

The abdominal CT scan with contrast shows a large heterogeneously enhancing mass in the retroperitoneal space with left kidney invasion and aortic wall encasement. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an inferior glenohumeral ligament (IGHL) lesion. Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion. However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast. Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation. This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema. Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences, indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function. MRA also identified a superior labrum from anterior to posterior (SLAP) lesion, which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligament (HALG injury) with superior labrum from anterior to posterior (SLAP) lesion, Bony humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligament (HALG injury), Bony Bankart lesion, Rotator-cuff tear/tendinosis
FinalDiagnosis: Humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligament (HALG injury) with superior labrum from anterior to posterior (SLAP) lesion

- MRI arthrography: Full-thickness intrasubstance tear of anterior IGHL band with contrast extravasation; axillary pouch shape changed from U to J (indicative of IGHL laxity); superior labrum from anterior to posterior (SLAP) lesion identified.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 67-year-old female with known malignant renal mass undergoing cancer staging.  
- Contrast-enhanced CT of thorax, abdomen, pelvis performed.  
- Multiple peripheral lung lesions with tissular densities ranging from a few millimeters up to 22x15 mm.  
- Lesions enhance during arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures.  
- Lesions have feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hypervascular lung metastases  
   - Pros: Patient with known malignancy (renal cancer); presence of multiple enhancing lung nodules compatible with hypervascular metastases.  
   - Cons: The lesions show well-defined feeding arteries and venous drainage; lesions enhance like vascular structures (suggesting vascular malformations); typical metastatic lesions do not usually have feeding lobar arteries or venous drainage and are not fed by lobar arteries directly.
  
2) Pulmonary sequestration  
   - Pros: Hypervascular lesions with systemic arterial supply may show feeding vessels; congenital malformation with abnormal vascular connections.  
   - Cons: Classic sequestration is a single or few cystic mass with systemic arterial supply (often from aorta) and no connection to pulmonary arteries; lesions located peripherally but usually unilateral; here multiple lesions bilateral; feeding arteries from lobar arteries (pulmonary arteries) rather than systemic; no mention of systemic arterial supply or abnormal venous drainage.  
  
3) Bronchocoele  
   - Pros: None; bronchocoeles are dilated bronchi, usually air-filled.  
   - Cons: Lesions described as tissular densities enhancing with arterial phase similar to vascular structures, not air-filled structures; no enhancement, no vascular feeding vessels. 
  
4) Pulmonary varix  
   - Pros: Dilated pulmonary vein; vascular structure, can be peripheral.  
   - Cons: Typically a single dilated vein; multiple lesions described; feeding arteries identified imply arterial component; varices are venous dilations, no feeding arteries from lobar arteries.  
  
5) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm  
   - Pros: Arterial feeding vessels; dilated arterial structures enhancing in arterial phase; lesions may be multiple and peripheral.  
   - Cons: Aneurysms usually focal dilations of arteries, not masses; typically involve a single artery; multiple lesions possible especially in some vasculitides or infections, but less common; no mention of aneurysm morphology or clinical context of vasculitis.  
  
6) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations (PAVMs)  
   - Pros: Direct connection (shunt) between pulmonary arteries and veins; feeding lobar arteries and draining lobar veins; lesions enhance in arterial phase similar to vascular structures; multiple peripheral lesions; seen in patients with known conditions (like Osler-Weber-Rendu) but can be sporadic.  
   - Cons: No clinical mention of hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, but not necessary; multiple lesions fit; size range compatible.  
  
7) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications  
   - Pros: Abnormal vascular communications; systemic to pulmonary supply typical of some congenital malformations.  
   - Cons: Feeder arteries are pulmonary lobar arteries, not systemic arteries; hence, less likely.

C) Ranking:  
1) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations - best fits the description of multiple peripheral lesions with feeding lobar arteries and drainage in lobar veins, enhancing like pulmonary vessels; typical imaging features.  
2) Hypervascular lung metastases - possible but less likely given feeding vessel pattern and enhancement similar to vessels; metastases typically do not have feeding lobar arteries.  
3) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm - less likely as no aneurysmal morphology described; multiple lesions possible but less common.  
4) Pulmonary sequestration - wrong vascular supply; usually systemic artery supply.  
5) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications - no systemic arterial supply described.  
6) Pulmonary varix - usually single venous dilation without feeding artery.  
7) Bronchocoele - not vascular lesion.

Overall, the imaging findings are classic for pulmonary arteriovenous malformations.  
</analysis>

<final>Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
