OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old female patient presented with exertional dyspnoea, weakness, abdominal pain, and lower limb swelling persisting for a month. There was no history of fever. Initial ultrasound investigation revealed mild ascites, multiple small abdominal lymphadenopathies and minimal pericardial effusion. Laboratory results showed haemoglobin of 12.2g/dL, platelet count of 105×109/L and white blood cell count of 3.32×10
 
 9
 
 /L. A 12-lead ECG revealed prolonged P waves, abnormal Q waves, poor R wave progression, and T wave abnormalities (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography revealed mild pericardial effusion, grade II diastolic dysfunction, right ventricle with segmental aneurysmal dilation and apical retraction, mild mitral regurgitation, and hyperechoic mass at the left ventricular apex, indicative of fibrofatty tissue deposition. While the right ventricle (RV) showed localised enlargement due to aneurysm formation, the overall restrictive physiology remained evident (Figures 2a and 2b). No thrombus was detected on echocardiography and cardiac MRI. Chest X-ray showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette and elevated diaphragms.
 
 The patient was referred for a cardiac MRI in our department. Cardiac MRI, done with cine imaging, perfusion, and early and late gadolinium-enhanced (LGE) PSIR sequences on MAGNETOM Vida 3T scanner revealed ascites, pericardial effusion, enlarged right atrium, retraction of the RV apex (RV apical notch), RV aneurysm, obliteration of the left ventricular (LV) apex on True FISP (true fast imaging with steady-state free precession) cine imaging (Figure 3). The LV end-diastolic volume was 69 mL, and the end-systolic volume was 28 mL. Similarly, the RV end-diastolic volume measured 70 mL, with an end-systolic volume of 39 mL, indicating relatively small chamber volumes. LGE imaging demonstrated subendocardial fibrosis with fat infiltration at both the LV and RV apices, resulting in a V-shaped enhancement pattern in each ventricle (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 12-year-old Malagasy boy consulted for a fever that had been evolving for two months, with a notion of trauma and pain in the right upper quadrant as well as chronic diarrhoea. A parasitological examination of the stool found eggs of
 
 Schistosoma mansoni
 
 . An ultrasound and a CT scan were then ordered.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (Figure 1), there were multiple confluent hypoechoic nodular formations, some of which have peripheral hyperechoic walls in segment VII of the liver. On CT scan (Figures 2 and 3), the formation corresponds to multiple hypodense fluid collections with thick peripheral enhancing walls with peri-lesional oedema in segment VII of the liver. There was an extracapsular extension into the hepato-renal space. The liver was enlarged, measuring 187 mm in the midclavicular line. The spleen was also increased in size, measuring 160 mm in height. The rest of the examination was considered normal.

- Additional findings: Hepatomegaly (liver 187 mm), splenomegaly (spleen 160 mm), otherwise normal exam.

1) Hydatid cyst  
   - Pros: Liver infection with cystic lesions, patient from endemic area (Malagasy), peripheral enhancing walls compatible with hydatid cyst membranes.  
   - Cons: Multiple cysts with extracapsular extension into hepato-renal space is less typical; no mention of daughter cysts, calcifications or "water lily" sign; more focal lesions rather than multiple confluent nodules.  

2) Liver pyogenic abscess  
   - Pros: Multiple hypodense fluid collections with thick peripheral enhancing walls, peri-lesional edema, extracapsular extension, hepatomegaly and clinical fever and pain are classic for pyogenic abscess.  
   - Cons: Chronicity (2 months fever), history of schistosomiasis, and multiple confluent nodules with hyperechoic walls more suggestive of granulomatous lesions rather than bacterial abscesses.

3) Hepatic adenoma  
   - Pros: Typically hyperechoic mass on US, can enhance peripherally.  
   - Cons: Age and gender less typical, usually solitary lesion; multiple confluent lesions unusual; no mention of characteristic arterial phase hyperenhancement; also no symptoms of infection.  

4) Hepatic malignant mass  
   - Pros: Could present as multiple lesions with necrosis and thick enhancing walls.  
   - Cons: Age (12 years) untypical, no other signs of malignancy, clinical picture and parasitology point towards infectious etiology rather than malignancy.  

5) Hepatic haemangioma  
   - Pros: Common benign liver lesion, typically hyperechoic on US, may show peripheral nodular enhancement.  
   - Cons: Usually solitary, well-defined lesion; no fluid collections, edema, or extracapsular extension; clinical picture and findings not supportive.

There were discrete and confluent areas of T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in periventricular and deep white matter of bilateral frontoparietal lobes, showing no restriction of diffusion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old male patient with no significant past medical background is referred for left knee pain since the age of 10, associated with knee swelling. Since then, he has always maintained complaints but lived a normal life. Plain radiograph, CT scan and MR were ordered for further investigation.
 Imaging Findings: On plain film (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d), there are periosteal and endosteal cortical thickening associated with extra-cortical bone formation along the anterior aspect of the left tibia and the medial aspect of the left femur. Areas of central medullary hyperostosis can be observed in the inferior third of the femoral diaphysis and in the femoral head. The lesions have an appearance that resembles “flowing candle wax”.
 
 CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, and 2e) allows for further acknowledgement of the changes described above.
 
 MR performed on a 1.5T MR scanner (T1 and STIR sequences in axial and coronal planes; Figures 3a and 3b) depict extensive changes with very low signal on all imaging sequences, mainly involving the left femur and tibia, with diffuse thickening of the bone cortex giving the bone surface a wavy appearance, as well as multiple intramedullary foci showing marked hypointensity, indicating a strong component of bone sclerosis. The lesions show eccentric growth, extending from the diaphysis to the epiphysis, and exceeding the cortical surface.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old woman (P1L1) presented with a two‐day history of acute pelvic pain and vomiting. There was no history of fever or vaginal bleeding. Her menstrual cycles were regular, but often accompanied by mild dysmenorrhea. No history of tubal ligation. The patient’s medical history was unremarkable, and a urinary pregnancy test was negative.
 Imaging Findings: Transvaginal sonography (TVS) revealed a convoluted, heterogeneously echogenic mass in the pouch of Douglas (POD), predominantly towards the right, showing significant surrounding inflammation. At its distal end was a well-defined, hypoechoic cystic lesion measuring approximately 3×4 cm, exhibiting peripheral mucosal infoldings with a classic cog-wheel pattern, strongly indicating tubal origin. The cyst contained homogeneous low-level internal echoes, raising suspicion for a haematosalpinx.
 
 Doppler imaging demonstrated a twisted vascular pedicle with a near-classic whirlpool pattern, along with pronounced adnexal hyperaemia. The right ovary maintained its normal anatomical position, morphology and good stromal vascularity, however, with mild periovarian hyperaemia. A small volume of echogenic free fluid was present in the POD.
 
 The left ovary, adnexa, uterus and cervix appeared unremarkable, while the endometrium exhibited features of hyperplasia.

Lack of free intraperitoneal fluid or haemoperitoneum described other than small amount of echogenic fluid, and cystic lesion shows hematic content inside a tubal mass. Cons: No fever, no leukocytosis mentioned, mass shows twisted pedicle with whirlpool pattern indicating torsion rather than abscess. Cystic lesion with haematosalpinx does not fit abscess; absence of fever or systemic signs makes PID less likely.  
4) Isolated right tubal torsion with haematosalpinx  
Pros: Exact match with imaging findings: twisted vascular pedicle with whirlpool, adjacent convoluted mass in POD, tubal origin indicated by cog-wheel sign in cyst, haematosalpinx within cystic lesion, normal ovary morphology, location right side predominantly, acute pain without pregnancy.  
Cons: None prominent; fits exactly.

Pros: Acute pain and haemorrhagic cyst on ultrasound, echogenic free fluid.  
Cons: Ovary appears normal in morphology and position, cystic lesion with tubal origin (cog-wheel sign), adnexal mass is convoluted in POD not directly ovarian, twisted pedicle consistent with torsion of tube rather than rupture of cyst. No mention of ovarian wall cystic lesion or hemorrhagic degeneration within ovary itself.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Isolated right tubal torsion with haematosalpinx (best fit; classic imaging signs of torsion including whirlpool and hemoperitoneum within a tubal cyst; tubal origin confirmed).  
2) Ruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy (negative pregnancy test argues against).  
3) Ovarian torsion (contradicted by normal ovary).  
4) PID with tubo-ovarian abscess (absence of systemic signs).  
5) Haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with rupture (ovary normal; mass tubal in origin).  
Thus, choice #4 (Isolated right tubal torsion with haematosalpinx) is the correct diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old non-obese female with a history of cholecystectomy in the last four months presents to the emergency department with clinical signs including right upper quadrant pain, generalised jaundice, and a fever of 38.5°C. Liver function tests reveal hepatic cytolysis and cholestasis (elevated AST/ALT and ALP). An abdominal ultrasound was requested.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound with a low-frequency probe reveals the presence of multiple hyperechoic formations within the intrahepatic bile ducts, causing posterior acoustic shadowing (Figure 1) and resulting in dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 2 and 3). The common bile duct was clear.
 
 The presence of jaundice and fever raised concerns about an associated cholangitis, prompting the indication for hepatobiliary MRI. The MRI reveals more pronounced dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts, particularly in the left lobe of the liver, containing multiple signal-void formations consistent with the previously described biliary calculi (Figures 4 and 5). Additionally, T1-weighted sequences post-gadolinium demonstrate subtle thickening and enhancement of the intrahepatic bile duct walls along with peribiliary hepatic infiltration, secondary to the inflammation of the liver adjacent to biliary ducts caused by potential cholangitis (Figure 6).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Young (36yo), non-obese female with history of cholecystectomy 4 months ago.  
- Presents with RUQ pain, jaundice, fever (38.5°C).  
- LFTs show hepatic cytolysis and cholestasis (elevated AST/ALT, ALP).  
- Ultrasound: multiple hyperechoic formations within intrahepatic bile ducts causing posterior acoustic shadowing and leading to intrahepatic biliary dilatation; common bile duct normal.  
- MRI: pronounced intrahepatic bile duct dilatation, especially in left lobe; multiple signal-void formations on MRI consistent with calculi; T1 post-gadoxetate gadolinium shows subtle thickening/enhancement of intrahepatic bile duct walls with peribiliary hepatic infiltration, compatible with inflammation from cholangitis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Classical gallbladder lithiasis  
- Pros: Presence of biliary stones (calculi) is compatible.  
- Cons: Patient is post-cholecystectomy, so classical gallbladder stones are unlikely to be present. Also, stones here are intrahepatic bile ducts, not gallbladder. Thus, classical biliary stones unlikely.  

2. Recurrent pyogenic cholangiohepatitis  
- Pros: Inflammation of intrahepatic bile ducts, presence of intrahepatic biliary stones, cholangitis suspicion, patient is symptomatic with fever and elevated LFTs. MRI shows peribiliary infiltration and segmental intrahepatic duct dilatation. Typically seen in non-obese women with intrahepatic cholestasis and intrahepatic stone disease, often post-cholecystectomy.  
- Cons: None significant; patient fits well with known risk profile and imaging findings.  

3. Low phospholipid-associated cholelithiasis (LPAC) syndrome  
- Pros: Intrahepatic biliary stones, young non-obese adult female typically affected, often recurrent cholestasis after cholecystectomy.  
- Cons: LPAC syndrome is characterized by intrahepatic stones and biliary sludge, typically with chronic episodes, and MRCP findings may show stone disease and ductal dilatation. However, the MRI findings of peribiliary enhancement and acute cholangitis can be seen in LPAC as well. LPAC often presents with recurrent non-cholestatic biliary symptoms; cholangitis can occur but is less typical as primary diagnosis.  

4. Caroli disease  
- Pros: Intrahepatic bile duct dilatation with biliary stones possible.  
- Cons: Caroli disease typically presents with saccular dilatations of the intrahepatic ducts, sometimes with segmental/diffuse involvement and stone formation, but the ducts are more fusiform or saccular than simple dilatation; imaging would show characteristic central dot sign (fibrous portal venule within dilated duct); no mention here of such finding. Also, Caroli disease is congenital and usually presents earlier in life.  

5. Primary sclerosing cholangitis  
- Pros: Multifocal bile duct stricturing and dilatation, inflammatory changes.  
- Cons: PSC usually involves extrahepatic and intrahepatic arteries and causes concentric strictures and beading, not solitary intrahepatic stones; no mention of ulcerative colitis or typical PSC imaging. Absence of typical beaded appearance and stones in PSC. Also, PSC is rare in females and usually older.

1. Recurrent pyogenic cholangiohepatitis - matches most closely with intrahepatic stones on imaging, recent cholecystectomy, female non-obese with cholangitis, segmental intrahepatic duct dilatation, and peribiliary enhancement.

3. Caroli disease - less likely due to lack of characteristic imaging findings (saccular ductal dilation, central dot sign).  
4. Primary sclerosing cholangitis - imaging and demographics do not fit.  
5. Classical gallbladder lithiasis - excluded due to post-cholecystectomy status.  

Tie-break: LPAC syndrome vs. Recurrent pyogenic cholangiohepatitis.  
- LPAC is a syndrome characterized by intrahepatic stones and recurrent cholestasis, often in young women, and predisposes to recurrent pyogenic cholangitis due to intrahepatic stones.  
- The acute presentation with fever and imaging showing inflammation suggests active cholangitis superimposed on underlying stones.  
- Given the acute inflammatory changes and cholangitis, "Recurrent pyogenic cholangiohepatitis" best captures the acute presentation and pathology.  

<final>  
Recurrent pyogenic cholangiohepatitis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 53-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with an episode of dysarthria followed by a decrease in the level of consciousness, without sphincter involvement. Physical examination upon admission was unremarkable, with no signs of neurological focal deficits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced CT shows two hypodense frontal lesions involving the cortex and the subcortical white matter with minimal surrounding oedema (Figure 1). The right lesion presents with a gyriform coarse calcification, and there is no enhancement after intravenous contrast administration (Figure 2). The left lesion produces light bone remodelling.
 
 On MRI, the lesions exhibit heterogeneous signal intensity and ill-defined margins, predominantly showing hyperintensity on T2/FLAIR sequences without communication between them through the corpus callosum (Figures 3a and 3b), and areas of hypointensity on susceptibility-weighted imaging consistent with coarse calcifications on the right side and bleeding on the left side (Figures 4a and 4b). There is no significant diffusion restriction (Figures 5a and 5b). Following contrast administration, only minimal linear enhancement is observed on both lesions (Figure 6).
 
 A perfusion study was carried out showing an increase in the cerebral blood volume (Figures 7a and 7b), and spectroscopy reveals a decrease in the N-acetylaspartate (N-Ace) peak, no elevation of lactate (LL) and an increased choline (Ch) level (Figures 8a and 8b).

   - Pros: Typically bilateral frontal lesions; calcifications common; increased choline and decreased NAA typical.  
   - Cons: Usually well-circumscribed rather than ill-defined margins; bone remodeling less common; bilateral symmetric lesions often but this case shows ill-defined and asymmetric (bleeding on left). Minimal enhancement fits grade 2.  

2) Diffuse astrocytoma  
   - Pros: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, ill-defined margins, no enhancement.  
   - Cons: Calcifications uncommon, bilateral presentation rare, no cortical involvement often; less prone to calcification and bone remodeling.  

3) Glioblastoma  
   - Pros: Increased CBV, heterogeneous mass, susceptibility changes (bleeding), calcifications can be present.  
   - Cons: Usually strong enhancement; infiltrative with central necrosis; bilateral symmetrical lesions rare; cortical involvement possible but typically in older patients (mean 65), but patient is 53, acceptable. However, lesions are relatively hypodense and with minimal edema, not typical for glioblastoma. Ill-defined border could fit, but minimal enhancement and no diffusion restriction argue against classic GBM.  

4) Ganglioglioma  
   - Pros: Cortical lesion with T2 hyperintensity; can have calcifications; occasionally may present in young adults.  
   - Cons: Usually unilateral and well-circumscribed; bilateral lesions rare; not typically bilateral and ill-defined.

   - Pros: Frontoparietal location, cortical and subcortical involvement, T2 hyperintensity, no or minimal enhancement.  
   - Cons: Typically occurs in children or young adults, not usually bilateral; calcifications are uncommon; bone remodeling unlikely; bilateral lesions uncommon.  

6) Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma  
   - Pros: Usually superficial cortical or subcortical location, T2 hyperintensity, can have calcifications, mild enhancement.  
   - Cons: Typically unilateral, well-demarcated, occurs in younger patients (<30), bilateral lesions rare, no multiple lesions typically.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) WHO grade 2 bilateral frontal oligodendroglioma - fits bilateral frontal location, calcifications, increased choline, decreased NAA, minimal enhancement, compatible age.  
2) Diffuse astrocytoma - fits ill-defined T2 hyperintensity without enhancement but lacks calcifications and bilateral symmetry.  
3) Glioblastoma - lacks strong enhancement and typical necrosis, bilateral lesions unusual, minimal edema; less likely.  
4) Ganglioglioma - typically unilateral and well-defined, less likely bilateral and ill-defined.  
5) DNET - favors children/young adults, unilateral, no calcifications.  
6) Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma - unilateral superficial tumor, less calcification, rare bilateral.  

Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is WHO grade 2 bilateral frontal oligodendroglioma.  
</analysis>  
<final>WHO grade 2 bilateral frontal oligodendroglioma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old woman presented with epigastric pain and uneasiness since six months. She had no difficulty in swallowing or regurgitation of food. She had no respiratory complaints. An ultrasonography of the abdomen was unremarkable. The patient was subjected to an erect chest radiograph for routine evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Erect frontal chest radiograph showed a well-defined, lobulated, homogeneous, soft tissue opacity (green arrow) in the left lower hemithorax, obscuring the medial aspect of the left dome of the diaphragm. It was seen as continuous with an ill-defined, left paravertebral soft tissue opacity (pink arrow). There was no silhouetting of the left heart border (Figure 1).
 
 A barium (95% w/v) oesophagogram showed unobstructed passage of barium through the thoracic soft tissue opacity. The retrocardiac oesophagus was tortuous and deviated to the left with a normal gastro-oesophageal junction (Figure 2).
 
 An unenhanced CT of the chest was performed with oral contrast, followed by intravenous contrast-enhanced CT with oral contrast in the same sitting. Axial unenhanced images showed circumferential, homogeneous thickening of the lower oesophageal wall with patency of the lumen showing orally administered water-soluble contrast. There were a few tiny foci of calcification within the wall (pink arrow on Figure 3). Sagittal venous phase images showed homogeneous enhancement of the thickened oesophageal wall without areas of necrosis (blue arrow) or evidence of invasion into surrounding structures (Figure 4). Coronal unenhanced images in the lung window showed surrounding atelectasis without signs of lung parenchymal involvement (Figure 5).

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Oesophageal leiomyoma  
   - Pros: Common benign esophageal tumor, usually well-circumscribed, can have calcifications. Typically presents as a lobulated mass in the esophageal wall. Usually causes extrinsic compression on esophagus but lumen patency is common.  
   - Cons: Leiomyomas are usually solitary and do not typically cause esophageal wall thickening circumferentially. Calcifications can be present but are small foci. Imaging shows a discrete mass rather than circumferential wall thickening.  

2) Oesophageal lymphoma  
   - Pros: Can cause diffuse wall thickening circumferentially and homogeneous enhancement. Typically shows no necrosis early. May have calcifications rarely.  
   - Cons: Very rare in esophagus. Usually presents with systemic symptoms or dysphagia. This patient has no respiratory or systemic signs. Calcifications in lymphoma are uncommon. No lymphadenopathy or invasion was described.  

3) Oesophageal gastrointestinal stromal tumour (GIST)  
   - Pros: Can arise in esophagus (though less common than stomach/intestine). Typically appear as soft tissue masses arising from muscularis propria, show homogeneous enhancement, may show wall thickening. Calcifications can be present but are uncommon. Usually lobulated and can cause circumferential thickening in advanced cases.  
   - Cons: Calcifications less common than in leiomyoma. Usually presents as localized mass rather than diffuse thickening, but can be circumferential in large tumors. No necrosis or invasion noted supports benign or low-grade pathology. The unobstructed lumen and tortuosity with no dysphagia is consistent with a slowly growing lesion.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Oesophageal leiomyoma - most common benign esophageal tumor, matches lobulated soft tissue opacity, homogeneous enhancement, presence of calcifications, and lumen patency. Diffuse thickening less typical, but can be misleading due to imaging plane. No invasion or necrosis supports benign lesion.  
2) Oesophageal GIST - possible but less likely because GISTs are rarer in the esophagus and calcifications less common.  
3) Oesophageal lymphoma - very unlikely since esophageal lymphoma is rare and patient lacks systemic or respiratory signs.  

Thus, the best fit is oesophageal leiomyoma.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old male patient complains of long-lasting chest pain, which worsens in the prone position and interferes with sleep. He denies any dyspnoea, palpitations, or a history of trauma. His medical history includes anxiety and a surgical procedure to remove multiple bone tumours from his lower limbs six years ago.
 Imaging Findings: A computed tomography (CT) scan was performed on the chest, revealing two lesions on the left scapula: one on the ventral surface in the subscapular fossa and another on the dorsal surface in the infraspinatus fossa (Figures 1 and 2). Both lesions presented calcium density and were found to have continuity with the adjacent bone, assuming a pedunculated conformation with anterior and posterior protrusion. These well-defined lesions did not show destruction of the cortical bone, irregular calcifications, or periosteal reaction of the underlying bone.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 66-year-old man with no significant medical history develops back pain after a running session. After starting physiotherapy, the complaints became acutely worse and did not disappear during the night. No other B-symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: Computer tomography (CT) of the thoracic spine showed a moth-eaten osteolytic aspect of the 7th thoracic vertebral body with a pathological wedge-shaped compression fracture. There is an associated homogeneously enhancing extensive paravertebral and epidural soft tissue component (approximately 56 x 65 x 47 mm) with encasement of the azygos vein.
 
 MRI demonstrated diffuse T1 hypointense, STIR hyperintense (compared to the disci) bone marrow infiltration of the 7th vertebral body with paravertebral and epidural mass extension. The mass showed homogeneous contrast enhancement with marked diffusion restriction (ADC value of 710 μm²/sec). A compressive myelopathy developed based on the epidural component. Encasement of the azygos vein is seen, as well as intercostal arteries traversing through the mass without invasion. There were no signs of invasion of the disci or other vertebrae.
 
 Additional CT thorax and abdomen, PET-CT, and whole body MRI showed no other lesions or adenopathies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old lady presented with complaints of pain and swelling in the left-hand middle finger following minor trauma. She has been having on-and-off dull aching pain. She sought treatment in a local hospital with buddy strapping. The blood investigation showed mildly elevated CRP (5.8 mg/dl) and ESR (50 mm/hr) but normal serum creatinine, calcium, phosphorus and alkaline phosphatase.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray showed a well-defined central expansile lytic lesion with scalloped margins involving the base of the 3rd proximal phalanx of the left hand with few specks of calcifications, and pathological fractures involving medial and lateral walls of the 3rd proximal phalanx (Figure 1a). Computed tomography showed similar findings to X-ray (Figure 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the hand showed a well-defined expansile lytic lesion which was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and showed no diffusion restriction with scalloping and fracture involving medial and lateral walls, causing splaying of sagittal bands on both sides (Figures 1c, 1d, 1e, 1f, and 1g).

- Pros: Expansile lytic lesion with scalloped margins and cortical erosion; chondrosarcoma can involve phalanges with endosteal scalloping; older age.

- Cons: Pathological fractures and expanding lesion with scalloping can mimic aggressive lesion; no cortical breach or soft tissue mass.

- Pros: Can present as expansile lytic lesion with scalloped margins; in setting of hyperparathyroidism could be considered.  
- Cons: Normal calcium, phosphorus, alkaline phosphatase argue against hyperparathyroidism; no systemic endocrine signs.  
5) Giant cell tumour  
- Pros: Expansile lytic lesion often found in bones; aggressive features possible.  
- Cons: Usually occurs in epiphyses of long bones; phalanges rare; presence of specks of calcifications not typical.

- Brown tumour ruled out due to normal biochemical values.  
- Giant cell tumour less likely due to site and imaging features.  

Considering imaging findings and clinical context, the lesion appears to be a benign cartilaginous tumour with characteristic specks of calcifications, expansion, scalloping without aggressive soft tissue features, and mild inflammatory markers consistent with minor trauma or local irritation. Therefore, enchondroma is most fitting.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old female patient, with no relevant previous history, presented to the outpatient clinic complaining of a progressively growing swelling at the left popliteal fossa, with associated limited knee flexion and local pain. At clinical examination, a firm and non-mobile swelling was evident at the popliteal region on the left.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a subfascial soft tissue mass mainly composed of tissue isointense to the muscle on T1-weighted (T1) sequences, hyperintense on T2-weighted fat-suppressed (T2FS) sequences and which, after contrast with fat suppression (T1 Gd), showed intense and fast enhancement, as well as “feathery” margins (flame sign). These areas were mixed with areas of very low signal on T1 and T2FS, which did not enhance after contrast (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 2). Some diffusion restriction was seen in the more enhancing areas (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 The extra-articular mass extended into the subcutis, as well as into the muscle bellies of the biceps femoris and medial gastrocnemius. The mass also encased the peroneal nerve (Figure 4), following its trajectory until the level of the fibular head (Figures 5a and 5b). Reactive oedema along the deep fascia of the lower leg was also present.

3. Fibrosarcoma: Possible, but less consistent with "flame sign" and typical enhancement pattern.  
4. Desmoid fibromatosis: Less likely due to marked diffusion restriction and intense vascular enhancement not typical for this entity.  

Overall, the imaging features (feathery margins, intense contrast enhancement with low signal collagenous areas, diffusion restriction, nerve encasement) point most strongly toward solitary fibrous tumour.

Clinical History: A 65-year-old female presented with a 3-day history of upper abdominal pain and nausea without vomiting, bowel changes, or systemic symptoms. Examination revealed epigastric tenderness without peritoneal signs. Laboratory results showed mild elevated CRP (16 mg/L) and WCC (11.9 x 10⁹/L), with normal liver functions and amylase levels.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed ill-defined fat stranding in the lesser omentum, located near the lesser curvature of the stomach. This fat stranding was not associated with a well-defined mass. The stomach and pancreas appeared normal on imaging, except for a small hiatus hernia identified incidentally. There was no evidence of free intraperitoneal gas, bowel wall thickening, or signs of bowel obstruction. No other abnormalities were observed in the abdominal and pelvic structures, and no additional masses or lesions were noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old male presented with pain in the right hypochondrium and loss of appetite for 1 month. There was no history of fever. The patient has a history of a successfully treated abdominal tuberculosis, for which he had taken anti-tubercular treatment for 1 year.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound was performed, which showed a well-defined round to oval isoechoic lesion in segment IV of the liver, without any significant internal vascularity.
 
 A contrast CT scan was further requested, which demonstrated a well-defined round to oval lesion, measuring ~6 x 4.2 x 5.1 cm, well demarcated from the rest of the liver with the same attenuation as that of the normal liver tissue. It was located inferior to segment IV (Figures 1a and 1b) with arterial supply from a branch of the left hepatic artery (Figure 2) and venous drainage into a branch of the middle hepatic vein. It was seen to cause mild dilatation of central and peripheral intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe due to compression (Figure 3). The liver was otherwise normal in size with maintained segmental morphology and normal hepatic veins. The left branch of the portal vein was attenuated in calibre; however, it showed normal contrast opacification (Figure 4). Physiological areas of altered attenuation were also noted in the right lobe of the liver.

- Contrast-enhanced CT reveals well-defined round to oval lesion ~6 x 4.2 x 5.1 cm in segment IV, same attenuation as normal liver tissue.  
- Lesion supplied by branch of left hepatic artery and drains via branch of middle hepatic vein.  
- Causes mild dilatation of left hepatic ductal system due to compression, left portal vein attenuated in calibre but opacified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Patient, 2 days old, born at 39 weeks of gestation from a controlled and uneventful pregnancy. At birth, notable observations included the violaceous discolouration of the scrotal skin, bilateral scrotal swelling, and difficulty in locating the testes in the scrotal sacs and inguinal canals (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: In the postnatal testicular ultrasound (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d), both scrotal sacs were occupied by heterogeneously echogenic lesions with poorly defined borders and multiple internal calcifications. These were not considered masses due to the unclear borders. Moderate increased vascularisation was noted on Doppler study.
 
 The abdominal ultrasound showed no calcifications, cysts, or free fluid. The testes and epididymides were difficult to visualise but appeared morphologically normal in the upper poles of both scrotal sacs.
 
 To complete the study, a simple X-ray of the abdomen and scrotum was performed, revealing multiple coarse, diffuse, bilateral intrascrotal calcifications, with no intra-abdominal abnormalities observed (Figure 2).
 
 MRI (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c) identified solid lesions occupying both scrotal sacs, hyperintense on T2 sequences with hypointense microcalcifications on T2. Both testes were of normal size and morphology and located within the scrotal sacs. No inguinal hernias were identified.

  - Cons: no trauma history; bilateral findings; patient neonate; no specific trauma signs.

  - Pros: swelling and increased vascularity fit infection.  
  - Cons: internal calcifications and bilateral symmetrical lesions unusual; normal testicular morphology; no fluid collection noted.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Meconium periorchitis  
2) Acute orchitis  
3) Testicular cancer  
4) Inguinal hernia  
5) Abscess or local infection  
6) Trauma or testicular injury

The absence of intra-abdominal abnormalities, no signs of hernia, and no focal neoplastic mass further support this diagnosis.

</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Incidental finding in the right iliac bone of a 68-year-old male patient with lung cancer who was undergoing a routine follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had lung cancer, and during a follow-up oncology CT scan, we incidentally discovered a painless mass in his right pelvic wing. CT of the pelvis shows a well-defined lesion in the right pelvis protruding into the iliac muscle with dense calcifications and a rim of fatty tissue that confirms its lipomatous nature (Figures 1a and 1b). On bone window, the calcified nature of the lesion can be clearly seen as an excrescence from the right iliac wing, causing a mild periosteal reaction on the adjacent bone cortex (Figure 2). The scout view of the CT is also helpful in observing the lesion, its close relation to the right iliac wing, with clumped calcifications and fatty tissue in the periphery (Figure 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: Incidental finding in the right iliac bone of a 68-year-old male patient with lung cancer who was undergoing a routine follow-up.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient is a 68-year-old male with lung cancer undergoing routine follow-up CT.  
- Incidental finding of a painless, well-defined mass in the right iliac wing protruding into the iliac muscle.  
- CT shows dense calcifications and a fatty rim, confirming its lipomatous nature.  
- On bone window, there is an calcified lesion as an excrescence from the right iliac wing with a mild periosteal reaction on adjacent bone cortex.  
- The lesion has clumped calcifications and fatty tissue peripherally.  
- Scout view confirms lesion's location related to the iliac wing.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Parosteal lipoma:  
  - Pros: The lesion is well-defined, protruding into the iliac muscle, with fatty rim confirming lipomatous nature, and dense calcifications on the bone surface. Parosteal lipoma is a lipoma attached to periosteum, often with associated calcifications.  
  - Cons: Parosteal lipomas usually arise from bone surface but may not always have a prominent calcified excrescence.  
- Osteochondroma:  
  - Pros: Osteochondromas arise from the bone surface as bony excrescences; they commonly present with cortical continuity.  
  - Cons: Osteochondromas typically do not have a fatty rim; they are purely bony/cartilaginous tumors. The presence of a fatty rim and lipomatous nature disfavors this diagnosis.  
- Liposarcoma:  
  - Pros: Fat-containing lesion could be malignant.  
  - Cons: Liposarcomas tend to be more aggressive, poorly defined, and may show soft tissue infiltration; they rarely present with a well-defined fatty rim and clumped calcifications adjacent to bone as described. Also, periosteal reaction and fatty rim suggest benign process.  
- Nora lesion / Bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation (BPOP):  
  - Pros: BPOP arises from bone surface with mixed bone, cartilage, and fibroblastic proliferation, can have calcifications and cause periosteal reaction. Typically affects small bones of the hands/feet but can involve pelvis rarely. Does not have a fatty rim.  
  - Cons: Lack of fatty rim and lipomatous nature argue against BPOP.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Parosteal lipoma - best matches due to lipomatous nature with peripheral fatty rim and calcifications on bone surface.  
2) Osteochondroma - less likely due to lack of fatty rim and lipomatous features.  
3) Nora lesion / BPOP - typically no fatty rim and occurs infrequently in iliac wing; less likely.  
4) Liposarcoma - unlikely due to benign features, well-defined lesion and presence of fatty rim rather than infiltrative mass.  
Thus, the most plausible diagnosis is parosteal lipoma.  
</analysis>

<final>Parosteal lipoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A young male presents with ataxic gait, numbness in lower limbs, and difficulty in standing and walking. To evaluate further, MR brain and MR spine screening was done, which suggests compensated hydrocephalus. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunting was done to treat this. After VP shunting, he presents with headache, with symmetrical weakness in the upper limbs and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: Pre-shunt
 
 There is prominence of the midline cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) space in between the lower parts of the bilateral cerebellar hemispheres inferior to the vermis, displacing the vermis mildly upward and the communication to the 4th ventricle—this may represent a Blake’s pouch cyst, an incidental finding as seen in Figure 1. There is moderate dilatation of both supra and infratentorial ventricular systems without obvious periventricular ooze, i.e., the possibility of compensated hydrocephalus as seen in Figure 3. There is evidence of engorgement of the epidural venous plexus causing mass effect over the cord, as seen in Figures 4a and 4b.
 
 Post-shunting
 
 There is evidence of enlargement of the ventral epidural space showing enhancement in the cervical spine and proximal dorsal spine up to the D2 level, with extension cranially till the foramen magnum with prominent flow voids, likely engorged epidural veins within it, causing cord compression as seen in Figure 5. There is evidence of an enlarged pituitary gland (mid sagittal height of 12.7 mm) as seen in Figure 6. There is evidence of a round dural venous sinus as seen in Figure 7. Given these findings, the possibility of excessive CSF diversion needs consideration.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:

  * Pros: Presents after VP shunt with cord compression from engorged epidural veins; symptoms of weakness and headache post-shunt consistent with overshunting causing low CSF pressure, venous hypertension, and spinal cord engorgement; enlarged dural venous sinus and pituitary suggest CSF hypovolemia consequences.

  * Pros: None; no CNS lesions, plaques, or white matter changes reported.

- Infection  
  * Pros: None; no signs of inflammation, infection, or systemic infection reported.  
  * Cons: Imaging shows structural changes consistent with venous engorgement rather than infection.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Overshunting-associated myelopathy – most consistent with post-shunt venous engorgement, cord compression, clinical symptoms, and imaging findings.  
2) Degenerative disorders – unlikely given age and acute postoperative course.  
3) Demyelination – no imaging evidence; symptoms acute, no lesions.  
4) Infection – no clinical or imaging signs.  

</analysis>  
<final>Overshunting-associated myelopathy</final>

<|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old premenopausal woman presented with dysmenorrhea. Ultrasound (USG) revealed a bulky uterus with multiple fibroids. She subsequently underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy combined with a left salpingo-oophorectomy and right salpingectomy. Six months post-surgery, she presented with abdominal fullness and difficulty breathing.
 Imaging Findings: A chest CT scan revealed numerous parenchymal and subpleural nodules of varying sizes, randomly distributed in both lungs (Figures 1a and 1b). No intralesional calcifications noted.
 
 A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen identified a large, heterogeneously enhancing mass with both solid and cystic components in the left para-aortic region, the aortocaval and left external iliac regions, exerting a mass effect on nearby visceral structures without signs of direct invasion (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 A USG-guided Tru-cut biopsy was performed on the left para-aortic solid-cystic mass and sent for histopathological examination (Figure 3).
 
 On follow-up FDG-PET/CT, the retroperitoneal masses showed no significant fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (Figures 4a and 4b).
PostDescription:

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 44-year-old premenopausal woman with history of multiple uterine fibroids and recent total abdominal hysterectomy with salpingo-oophorectomy.  
- Six months post-surgery: abdominal fullness, dyspnea.  
- Chest CT: numerous randomly distributed parenchymal and subpleural nodules of varying sizes in both lungs, no intralesional calcifications.  
- Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT: large heterogeneously enhancing mass with solid and cystic components in left para-aortic, aortocaval, and left external iliac regions, causing mass effect but no direct invasion.  
- Biopsy of the para-aortic mass performed.  
- Follow-up FDG-PET/CT: retroperitoneal masses show no significant FDG uptake.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Lymphoma: Could cause retroperitoneal masses and pulmonary nodules. However, lymphoma often FDG avid on PET, and nodules often show lymphomatous infiltration but would expect a more cohesive nodal distribution rather than random pulmonary nodules without calcification. Retroperitoneal masses here are cystic-solid, which is unusual for lymphoma. Moreover, biopsy results are pending and no FDG uptake argues against lymphoma. FAIL.  
- Retroperitoneal sarcoma: Can produce large heterogeneous retroperitoneal masses but typically solid, invading local structures. Pulmonary metastases can occur but usually show FDG avidity and may be more nodular consolidations. The mass here is cystic-solid and no FDG uptake on PET, less common for sarcoma. FAIL.  
- Metastasis: From what primary? Likely uterine leiomyoma (since she had hysterectomy for fibroids). Metastases from leiomyoma (benign metastasizing leiomyoma) can produce retroperitoneal masses and multiple lung nodules, often with no FDG uptake. PASS.  
- Germ cell tumour: Retroperitoneal germ cell tumors possible, but less likely without elevated markers; masses usually solid, may have calcifications; pulmonary metastases less random, often nodular. No FDG uptake argues against active germ cell tumor. FAIL.  
- Lipoma: Retroperitoneal fat-containing lesions, usually no solid/cystic heterogeneity and no significant enhancement. Also, not associated with pulmonary nodules. FAIL.  
- Lymphangioleiomyomatosis: Affects women of childbearing age; characterized by cystic lung changes, not solid nodules. Also, no retroperitoneal masses. FAIL.  
- Benign metastasising leiomyoma: Known to cause multiple lung nodules and retroperitoneal masses in women after hysterectomy for fibroids; typically no or minimal FDG uptake (benign), mass can be cystic-solid. PASS.  
- Peripheral nerve sheath tumour: Can cause retroperitoneal masses, but these are usually well-circumscribed, solid, and FDG avid. Pulmonary metastases possible but less likely with these characteristics. FAIL.  
- Teratoma: Usually contains fat and calcifications; retroperitoneal teratomas possible but rare in women after hysterectomy. Nodules in lung without fat/calcifications less likely. FAIL.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Benign metastasising leiomyoma (PASS) — fits history (uterine fibroids), findings (retroperitoneal solid-cystic mass), multiple lung nodules without FDG avidity, typical behavior.  
2) Metastasis (general) — less specific without knowing primary; the history and lack FDG uptake point to BM leiomyoma.  
3) Lymphoma and sarcoma lower due to FDG PET findings and imaging characteristics.  

</analysis><final> Benign metastasising leiomyoma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented with complaints of right knee pain for one month and hemi-hypertrophy of the right lower limb since birth. No history of trauma. The patient was evaluated with X-ray of both lower limbs, computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the knee.
 Imaging Findings: X-rays of knee, leg and foot (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c), CT (Figures 2a and 2b) and MRI (Figures 3a and 3b) show variable bone sclerosing spectrum with shared features of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata and melorheostosis seen in right lower limb bones involving the right femur and tibia, right fibula, multiple tarsal bones, metatarsal and phalanges.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented with complaints of right knee pain for one month and hemi-hypertrophy of the right lower limb since birth. No history of trauma. The patient was evaluated with X-ray of both lower limbs, computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the knee.
 Imaging Findings: X-rays of knee, leg and foot, CT and MRI show variable bone sclerosing spectrum with shared features of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata and melorheostosis seen in right lower limb bones involving the right femur and tibia, right fibula, multiple tarsal bones, metatarsal and phalanges.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia, Paget’s disease of bone, Osteoblastic metastases, Erdheim–Chester disease, Myelofibrosis, Sickle cell disease
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 43-year-old female with right knee pain and hemi-hypertrophy of the right lower limb since birth  
- Imaging shows variable bone sclerosing spectrum involving right femur, tibia, fibula, tarsal bones, metatarsals, and phalanges  
- Imaging findings describe mixed features of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata, and melorheostosis  
- No trauma history  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia  
   - Pros: The combination of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata, and melorheostosis is a recognized constellation termed "Mixed Sclerosing Bone Dysplasia"  
   - Cons: None; this diagnosis directly matches the described mixed sclerosing bone findings with hemi-hypertrophy is consistent with melorheostosis  
2) Paget’s disease of bone  
   - Pros: Paget’s disease can cause bone thickening and sclerosis  
   - Cons: Paget’s generally affects older adults, asymmetric and usually limited to fewer bones; the mixed sclerosing pattern and multiple lesions including osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis are not typical  
3) Osteoblastic metastases  
   - Pros: Osteoblastic lesions can cause focal sclerosis on imaging  
   - Cons: Clinical history lacks malignancy; widespread multifocal lesions with osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis pattern are inconsistent with metastases; also melorheostosis and osteopathia striata are not metastatic patterns  
4) Erdheim–Chester disease  
   - Pros: Can cause symmetric sclerotic bone lesions, especially in long bones and epiphyses  
   - Cons: This disease typically involves diaphyses symmetry and epiphyses; the unilateral hemi-hypertrophy and mixed sclerosing pattern with osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis are not characteristic  
5) Myelofibrosis  
   - Pros: Can cause bone remodeling and sclerosis in chronic phases  
   - Cons: Myelofibrosis is a systemic bone marrow disease, usually older patients, no hemi-hypertrophy or mixed sclerosing patterns; imaging in myelofibrosis shows diffuse changes rather than focal sclerosing patterns  
6) Sickle cell disease  
   - Pros: Can cause bone infarcts, sclerosis, and deformities  
   - Cons: Usually involves multiple infarcts and patchy sclerosis, no hemi-hypertrophy; osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis not typical  

C) Ranking:  
1) Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia - best fits the clinical and imaging findings, including coexistence of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata, melorheostosis, and unilateral hemi-hypertrophy.  
2) Paget’s disease - less likely due to age, lack of typical radiologic features of Paget’s, and unusual mixed sclerosing patterns with osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis.  
3) Erdheim-Chester disease - less likely due to unilateral involvement and mixed sclerosing patterns not classic for this disease.  
4) Osteoblastic metastases - excluded due to clinical and imaging mismatch.  
5) Myelofibrosis and 6) Sickle cell disease - excluded as per cons.

</analysis>  
<final>  
Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia  
</final><|return|>

   - Pros: The combination of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata, and melorheostosis is a recognized constellation termed "Mixed Sclerosing Bone Dysplasia"  
   - Cons: None; this diagnosis directly matches the described mixed sclerosing bone findings with hemi-hypertrophy is consistent with melorheostosis  
2) Paget’s disease of bone  
   - Pros: Paget’s disease can cause bone thickening and sclerosis  
   - Cons: Paget’s generally affects older adults, asymmetric and usually limited to fewer bones; the mixed sclerosing pattern and multiple lesions including osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis are not typical  
3) Osteoblastic metastases  
   - Pros: Osteoblastic lesions can cause focal sclerosis on imaging  
   - Cons: Clinical history lacks malignancy; widespread multifocal lesions with osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis pattern are inconsistent with metastases; also melorheostosis and osteopathia striata are not metastatic patterns  
4) Erdheim–Chester disease  
   - Pros: Can cause symmetric sclerotic bone lesions, especially in long bones and epiphyses  
   - Cons: This disease typically involves diaphyses symmetry and epiphyses; the unilateral hemi-hypertrophy and mixed sclerosing pattern with osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis are not characteristic  
5) Myelofibrosis  
   - Pros: Can cause bone remodeling and sclerosis in chronic phases  
   - Cons: Myelofibrosis is a systemic bone marrow disease, usually older patients, no hemi-hypertrophy or mixed sclerosing patterns; imaging in myelofibrosis shows diffuse changes rather than focal sclerosing patterns  
6) Sickle cell disease  
   - Pros: Can cause bone infarcts, sclerosis, and deformities

3) Erdheim-Chester disease - less likely due to unilateral involvement and mixed sclerosing patterns not classic for this disease.  
4) Osteoblastic metastases - excluded due to clinical and imaging mismatch.  
5) Myelofibrosis and 6) Sickle cell disease - excluded as per cons.

Clinical History: A 45-year-old female patient was referred to the radiology department in view of a history of recurrent patellar dislocation and knee joint pain aggravated for the last 20 days. No history of trauma.

Grade IV chondromalacic changes (Outerbridge staging) are seen in the lateral articular surface of the patella at the patellofemoral joint space (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 17-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of markedly elevated beta-hCG levels, brown vaginal discharge, and back pain. The patient reports an uterus growing in size and can now be palpated at the level of the umbilicus. The patient has a history of irregular menses.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal sagittal (Figure 1a) and coronal (Figure 2b) uterine ultrasound shows a diffusely enlarged uterus containing a heterogeneous, avascular, solid and cystic mass with multiple small cystic spaces (“bunch of grapes” appearance). Contrast-enhanced pelvic CT in sagittal (Figure 2a) and coronal (Figure 2b) projections shows a diffusely enlarged uterus containing cystic and solid mass-like components.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: 17-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of markedly elevated beta-hCG levels, brown vaginal discharge, and back pain. The patient reports an uterus growing in size and can now be palpated at the level of the umbilicus. The patient has a history of irregular menses.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal sagittal and coronal uterine ultrasound shows a diffusely enlarged uterus containing a heterogeneous, avascular, solid and cystic mass with multiple small cystic spaces (“bunch of grapes” appearance). Contrast-enhanced pelvic CT in sagittal and coronal projections shows a diffusely enlarged uterus containing cystic and solid mass-like components.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Retained products of conception, Placental mesenchymal dysplasia, Triploidy (partial mole), Complete hydatidiform mole, Ectopic pregnancy, Choriocarcinoma, Endometrial carcinoma, Leiomyoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 17-year-old female with a 2-week history of markedly elevated beta-hCG, brown vaginal discharge, and back pain.  
- Uterus enlarged and palpable at the level of the umbilicus.  
- History of irregular menses.  
- Ultrasound: diffusely enlarged uterus with a heterogeneous, avascular, solid and cystic mass with multiple small cystic spaces described as a "bunch of grapes" appearance.  
- Pelvic CT: diffusely enlarged uterus containing cystic and solid mass-like components.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Retained products of conception  
   - Pros: Elevated beta-hCG can be seen; uterine enlargement possible.  
   - Cons: Usually avascular solid/cystic mass with "bunch of grapes" appearance is less typical; usually history of recent pregnancy, not mentioned here; uterus enlarged to umbilicus level less likely.  

2) Placental mesenchymal dysplasia  
   - Pros: Cystic placental changes on imaging, resembles "bunch of grapes."  
   - Cons: Usually fetal placenta lesions, not uterine mass; would expect a fetus or gestational sac; no mention of fetus; elevated beta-hCG possible but less typical for uterine mass.  

3) Triploidy (partial mole)  
   - Pros: Elevated beta-hCG possible; can cause uterine enlargement.  
   - Cons: Partial moles usually have a fetus or fetal parts; mass not typically "bunch of grapes" appearance; cystic vesicles usually irregular and mixed; no mention of fetal parts.  

4) Complete hydatidiform mole  
   - Pros: Markedly elevated beta-hCG; uterus enlarged far beyond gestational age; "bunch of grapes" cystic vesicles characteristic; heterogeneous avascular mass typical.  
   - Cons: Usually history of a pregnancy; irregular menses could include bleeding after missed period; no fetal parts typically.  

5) Ectopic pregnancy  
   - Pros: Elevated beta-hCG.  
   - Cons: No mass outside uterus described; uterus enlarged to umbilicus unlikely from ectopic; imaging shows uterine mass; no mention of adnexal mass.  

6) Choriocarcinoma  
   - Pros: Elevated beta-hCG; uterine mass possible.  
   - Cons: Typically patients have history of prior mole, pregnancy, or abortion; mass is often vascular; imaging shows solid mass but not "bunch of grapes"; avascular cysts less typical.  

7) Endometrial carcinoma  
   - Pros: Uterine mass possible.  
   - Cons: Elevated beta-hCG unlikely; patient too young (17 years); no cancer risk factors; cystic "bunch of grapes" appearance not typical.  

8) Leiomyoma  
   - Pros: Uterine mass; solid and cystic components possible.  
   - Cons: Leiomyomas are vascular; beta-hCG usually not elevated; "bunch of grapes" appearance not typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Complete hydatidiform mole – best fits elevated beta-hCG, markedly enlarged uterus, and classic "bunch of grapes" cystic appearance.  
2) Placental mesenchymal dysplasia – plausible but usually involves placenta rather than uterine mass.  
3) Partial mole (Triploidy) – possible but typically has fetal parts.  
Others less likely due to imaging, clinical, or lab inconsistencies.

</analysis>  
<final>Complete hydatidiform mole</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with persistent dull pain in the right upper abdomen. Physical examination revealed a flat and soft abdomen with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had complete imaging data, including CT, MR, and PET-CT. All imaging examinations showed a mass in segment VII/VIII of the liver.
 
 Unenhanced CT scan showed that the lesion was low-density, with a CT value of about 23 HU, linear calcification at the edge, and mild shrinkage of the liver capsule. After enhancement, the lesion did not enhance, and the surrounding liver parenchyma was mildly enhanced in the arterial phase (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The mass contains mixed low signal on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images and T2-weighted fat-suppressed images (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Coronal T2-weighted fat-suppressed images and coronal venous-phase T1-weighted fat-suppressed images showed that the intrahepatic bile duct beside the mass is dilated and connected to the lesion; the edge of the lesion and the adjacent bile duct showed mild enhancement (Figures 3a and 3b). Diffusion-weighted image (DWI, b = 800 mm2/s) showed a slightly higher signal (Figure 4a). The ADC map does not show restricted diffusion (Figure 4b). Similar to the CT-enhanced images, on T1-weighted enhanced arterial phase images, it can also be seen that the liver parenchyma around the lesion is slightly enhanced (Figure 4c).
 
 PET-CT showed a slight increase in FDG metabolism (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d).
PostDescription:

Unenhanced CT scan showed that the lesion was low-density, with a CT value of about 23 HU, linear calcification at the edge, and mild shrinkage of the liver capsule. After enhancement, the lesion did not enhance, and the surrounding liver parenchyma was mildly enhanced in the arterial phase. The mass contains mixed low signal on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images and T2-weighted fat-suppressed images. Coronal T2-weighted fat-suppressed images and coronal venous-phase T1-weighted fat-suppressed images showed that the intrahepatic bile duct beside the mass is dilated and connected to the lesion; the edge of the lesion and the adjacent bile duct showed mild enhancement. Diffusion-weighted image (DWI, b = 800 mm2/s) showed a slightly higher signal. The ADC map does not show restricted diffusion. Similar to the CT-enhanced images, on T1-weighted enhanced arterial phase images, it can also be seen that the liver parenchyma around the lesion is slightly enhanced. PET-CT showed a slight increase in FDG metabolism.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Primary hepatic adenosarcoma, Undifferentiated carcinoma (also known as sarcomatoid carcinoma), Hepatic fibroadenoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient: 53-year-old male with persistent dull pain in right upper abdomen.
- Imaging: CT (unenhanced and enhanced), MR (T1, T2, fat-suppressed, DWI), PET-CT.
- Location: Mass in segment VII/VIII of the liver.
- Unenhanced CT: Low-density lesion (~23 HU), linear calcification at edge, mild liver capsule shrinkage.
- Enhanced CT: No enhancement of lesion; surrounding liver parenchyma shows mild arterial phase enhancement.
- MRI: Mixed low T1 signal; hyperintense T2 signal (including fat-suppressed images); intrahepatic bile duct beside and connected to mass is dilated; mild enhancement at lesion edge and adjacent bile duct; slight DWI hyperintensity but no restricted diffusion on ADC.
- PET-CT: Slight increase in FDG metabolism.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma
   - Pros: Mass located in right liver; bile duct dilation connecting to lesion; mild peripheral enhancement (typical of cholangiocarcinoma showing peripheral enhancement and delayed progressive enhancement); low CT density; linear calcification at edge could be seen; slight FDG uptake.
   - Cons: No significant arterial phase enhancement; no restricted diffusion; lesion did not enhance centrally; cholangiocarcinoma often shows progressive peripheral enhancement and central fibrosis; calcifications and capsule retraction can be seen in cholangiocarcinoma. Slight FDG uptake fits but is not strong.

2) Primary hepatic adenosarcoma
   - Pros: Rare tumor may present as a hepatic mass.
   - Cons: No specific imaging features documented for adenosarcoma; enhancement pattern is typically heterogeneous; rare; not strongly supported by imaging findings; no typical features.

3) Undifferentiated carcinoma (sarcomatoid carcinoma)
   - Pros: Can present as a liver mass with heterogeneous signals.
   - Cons: Usually shows more aggressive imaging features; more enhancement expected; no specific enhancement described; no restricted diffusion or high FDG uptake often seen; no mention of typical imaging patterns.

4) Hepatic fibroadenoma
   - Pros: Contains fibrous stroma which could explain low density and T2 hyperintensity; may have calcifications.
   - Cons: Rare in the liver (more common in breast); MRI features often include well-defined lesions with mixed solid and cystic components; no bile duct dilatation or connection; rare to have bile duct connection; imaging shows dilated bile duct and lesion connecting to duct, not typical for fibroadenoma. FDG uptake is not typical.

C) Ranking:
1) Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma: Best fits location, bile duct dilatation and connection, peripheral mild enhancement, calcification, and slight FDG uptake.
2) Undifferentiated carcinoma: Less likely due to lack of strong enhancement or aggressive features.
3) Primary hepatic adenosarcoma: Very rare, no supportive imaging features.
4) Hepatic fibroadenoma: Least likely due to location, duct involvement and imaging features.

Conclusion: The combination of a mass in segment VII/VIII with adjacent bile duct dilation and connection, mild peripheral enhancement, linear calcifications, and slight FDG uptake best matches hepatic cholangiocarcinoma.

<final>Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma</final><|return|>
options: Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma | Primary hepatic adenosarcoma | Undifferentiated carcinoma (also known as sarcomatoid carcinoma) | Hepatic fibroadenoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male with anal swelling and bleeding of years of evolution. Physical examination revealed a mass of approximately 20 cm in perianal region, with several fistulous tracts covering anal and perianal area with purulent secretion.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT showed a voluminous low-density gluteal mass with areas of internal enhancement (Figures 1a and 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a multiloculated, cauliflower-like 21 x 16 x 11cm gluteal mass. It extended through both ischioanal fossae and fistulised to the anal canal with two posterior tracts (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lesion was hypointense with respect to the muscle in T1-weighted sequences and showed high signal intensity in T2, compatible with abundant myxoid-mucinous content. Contrast-enhanced fat-suppressed T1-weighted MR imaging showed linear and papillary mesh-like enhancing areas (Figure 2d).
PostDescription:

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a multiloculated, cauliflower-like 21 x 16 x 11 cm gluteal mass. It extended through both ischioanal fossae and fistulised to the anal canal with two posterior tracts. The lesion was hypointense with respect to the muscle in T1-weighted sequences and showed high signal intensity in T2, compatible with abundant myxoid-mucinous content. A) Key findings:  
- 62-year-old male with a long-standing perianal mass (20-21 cm), with multiple fistulous tracts and purulent discharge.  
- CT: voluminous low-density gluteal mass with internal enhancement.  
- MRI: multiloculated, cauliflower-like gluteal mass extending through both ischioanal fossae, fistulising to anal canal with posterior tracts.  
- Lesion hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2 (suggests myxoid/mucinous content).  
- Contrast-enhanced fat-suppressed T1 showed linear and papillary mesh-like enhancing areas.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Perianal abscess  
   - Pros: Mass with fistulous tracts and purulent secretion; abscesses can be multiloculated with enhancement.  
   - Cons: Abscesses are usually smaller, acute or subacute; large multiloculated mass with myxoid/mucinous content and cauliflower-like morphology less typical; chronicity and size argue against simple abscess.  

2) Teratoma  
   - Pros: Large mass can have multiloculated appearance; can be in presacral/gluteal area.  
   - Cons: Teratomas often show fat, calcifications on imaging; clinical history of fistulous tracts and fistulisation to anal canal less typical; myxoid/mucinous contents less characteristic; cauliflower-like morphology unclear.  

3) Carcinoma arising from anal fistula  
   - Pros: Chronic fistulous disease with mass and multiloculated enhancement; fistulising to anal canal with multiple tracts; large mass with enhancing solid components; mucinous/necrotic areas can show T2 hyperintensity; clinical history matches risk (long-standing fistula).  
   - Cons: Imaging can also mimic abscesses or cystic masses; lack of explicit description of solid nodular mass or invasion, but mesh-like papillary enhancement and multiloculated "cauliflower-like" pattern can be suspicious of malignancy.  

4) Tailgut cyst  
   - Pros: Typically presacral location; multiloculated cystic lesion; can have mucinous/myxoid content and T2 hyperintensity.  
   - Cons: Usually asymptomatic or with mild symptoms; fistulisation to anal canal and large size unusual; Tailgut cysts are usually midline and presacral, here mass is gluteal extending through ischioanal fossae with perianal fistulas, more extensive than typical Tailgut cyst.  

5) Dermoid cyst  
   - Pros: Can have low density on CT with enhancement; multiloculated cystic lesions.  
   - Cons: Usually contain fat, calcifications; no mention of fat or calcifications; fistulisation and large size less typical; T1 hyperintensity expected due to fat (not described).  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Carcinoma arising from anal fistula  
2) Tailgut cyst  
3) Teratoma  
4) Dermoid cyst  
5) Perianal abscess  

The lesion's morphology, chronic fistulous disease with multifocal sinuses and fistulisation, large multiloculated mass with internal enhancement and T2 hyperintensity (myxoid/mucinous content), plus mesh-like papillary enhancement are highly suspicious for a malignancy arising in a chronic anal fistula.  

<final>  
Carcinoma arising from anal fistula  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with abdominal distension and pain of 10 days’ duration. No vomiting or obstipation was seen.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT (computed tomography) scan of the abdomen was performed. Small bowel loops appeared dilated with multiple air-fluid levels (Figures 1, 2a, and 2b). A transition zone was found on the right side of the urinary bladder. A closed-loop obstruction was found with distal ileal loops as content, causing indentation of the right superolateral wall of the urinary bladder (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). A few dilated loops show a small bowel faeces sign (luminal contents resembling colonic faecal matter with gas bubbles). The hernial sac was filled with a minimal amount of fluid. There was no abnormal enhancement of the small bowel loops within the hernial sac. No thickening of the bowel walls was noted. Large bowel loops were collapsed. The cecum appeared normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old presented with dysphagia and post-prandial vomiting since one month. She had a history of accidental corrosive ingestion 20 years back, for which she was managed surgically (an elective retrosternal oesophageal bypass surgery using a gastric pull-through procedure was performed). She underwent bougie dilatation of the esophagogastric anastomosis for similar presenting symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Frontal chest radiograph showed a fairly well-defined, round soft tissue opacity in the retrocardiac region with a broad base towards the mediastinum (Figure 1). There were no calcifications or cavitation within the opacity. The lateral border of the descending thoracic aorta was obscured by the lesion. On esophagogram, the neo-oesophagus (pulled up stomach) was seen in the right paramedian retrosternal region (Figures 2a and 2b). There was no opacification of the native oesophagus. Unenhanced chest CT showed passage of oral contrast through the neo-oesophagus into the stomach. There was a well-defined fluid-filled elongated cystic lesion (max. diameter of 6.8 cm) in the posterior mediastinum, posterior to the heart and anterior to the descending thoracic aorta. It was communicating with the narrowed proximal and mid native oesophagus (Figures 3a and 3b).

3) Post-operative collection - Less likely given well-defined cyst and lack of signs of infection or complexity.
4) Pancreatic pseudocyst - Anatomically impossible here.

Therefore, the best diagnosis is "Oesophageal mucocele post corrosive stricture surgery".

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We report a case of a 16-year-old patient living in a rural environment. The symptomatology goes back to 3 months with diffuse abdominal pain, distension, and with vomiting and fever 3 days before his admission to the ER. Clinical examination revealed a slight dullness of the flanks and hypogastrium.
 Imaging Findings: An abdominal ultrasound was performed and revealed multiple hepatic, splenic and peritoneal cystic lesions with large intraperitoneal effusion (Figure 1). Abdominal and pelvic CT scan in axial, sagittal and coronal sections showed multiple cystic lesions adherent to the peritoneal leaflets, falciform ligament, great omentum and mesentery (Figures 2 and 4). The largest cyst is located in the left iliac fossa, measuring 55.5 x 42 mm, containing some calcifications (Figure 3), and with a large intraperitoneal effusion. The CT scan also revealed hepatic and splenic cystic lesions.
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 16-year-old patient from rural environment with 3-month history of diffuse abdominal pain, distension, vomiting, fever.
- Ultrasound shows multiple hepatic, splenic and peritoneal cystic lesions with large intraperitoneal effusion.
- CT shows multiple cystic lesions adherent to peritoneal leaflets, falciform ligament, great omentum, and mesentery.
- Largest cyst is in left iliac fossa, 55.5 x 42 mm, with calcifications and large intraperitoneal effusion.
- Presence of hepatic and splenic cystic lesions.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Mesenteric lymphangioma
- Pros: Mesenteric lymphangiomas can present as cystic abdominal masses, often multilocular, can cause abdominal distension and pain.
- Cons: Typically lymphangiomas are not associated with multiple cystic lesions elsewhere in the abdomen like hepatic and splenic involvement; calcifications are uncommon in lymphangiomas; presence of multiple organ cystic involvement (liver, spleen) is unusual.

2) Peritoneal hydatidosis
- Pros: Hydatid disease caused by Echinococcus granulosus commonly presents with multiple cystic lesions in liver and spleen; peritoneal hydatidosis represents secondary spread with peritoneal cysts adherent to peritoneal leaflets, omentum, mesentery; calcifications within cysts are characteristic of hydatid cysts; large intraperitoneal effusion can be due to cyst rupture or infection; patient is from rural environment, which is endemic for hydatid disease.
- Cons: None significant; findings are classic for peritoneal hydatidosis.

3) Mesenteric cyst
- Pros: Can be multiple and large; can cause abdominal distension.
- Cons: Typically single cystic lesion, rarely multiple; calcifications uncommon; hepatic and splenic involvement is unusual; peritoneal and organ involvement (multiple organ cysts) less typical.

C) Ranking:

1) Peritoneal hydatidosis - best fits all findings: multiple cystic lesions with calcifications in liver, spleen, peritoneum; large cyst in left iliac fossa adherent to peritoneal structures; rural environment is classic for hydatid disease; large intraperitoneal effusion possibly due to cyst rupture or infection.

2) Mesenteric lymphangioma - less likely due to multiple organ involvement and calcifications.

3) Mesenteric cyst - least likely due to typical presentation being a solitary cyst without calcifications or systemic involvement.

Thus, Peritoneal hydatidosis is the most probable diagnosis.

</analysis> <final>Peritoneal hydatidosis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old female presented with progressively worsening holocranial headache over 3 years, occasionally accompanied by photophobia and phonophobia, sometimes waking her at night, and refractory to mild analgesics. She denied visual symptoms, sinus surgery, or head trauma. Ear, nose, and throat (ENT) examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: CT of the paranasal sinuses revealed complete opacification of the left sphenoid sinus by a hyperattenuating lesion, with moderate expansion and smooth thinning and remodelling of its osseous walls (Figure 1a). Calcifications were present within the lesion (Figures 1b and 3). A focal dehiscence of the sinus roof in close proximity to the anterior cranial fossa floor and sclerosis of the posterior and lateral walls of the sinus were seen (Figure 1b and 1c).
 
 On MRI, the lesion was iso to mildly hypointense on T1-W (Figure 2a) and markedly hypointense on T2-W (Figure 2b). Restricted diffusion was observed in the right sphenoid sinus on DWI/ADC (Figure 4a). Post-contrast T1-W imaging revealed a smooth peripheral rim of enhancement (Figure 5). Additionally, a thin dural enhancement was noted contiguous to the erosion of the sinus roof. The adjacent brain parenchyma appeared normal, with no signs of oedema or infiltration.
PostDescription:
