OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 21-year-old Caucasian man, an amateur drummer, presents with a two-year history of progressive weakness of the left distal hand. There is no associated pain or sensory loss. Past medical and family history are unremarkable. There is no history of preceding trauma.
 Imaging Findings: On MRI performed with the neck in the neutral position, T2 and T1-weighted sagittal images (Figures 1 and 2, respectively) reveal mild spinal cord atrophy at the C5–C7 level. On T2 axial images (Figure 3), atrophy of the left anterior horn is clearly visible.
 
 In the flexed position (Figure 4), there is an anterior shift of the posterior wall of the cervical dural sac at the C3–Th3 level. A crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus appears in the posterior epidural space, showing high signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences and is accompanied by flow voids.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 21-year-old Caucasian man, an amateur drummer, presents with a two-year history of progressive weakness of the left distal hand. There is no associated pain or sensory loss. Past medical and family history are unremarkable. There is no history of preceding trauma.

On T2 axial images, atrophy of the left anterior horn is clearly visible. In the flexed position, there is an anterior shift of the posterior wall of the cervical dural sac at the C3–Th3 level. A crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus appears in the posterior epidural space, showing high signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences and is accompanied by flow voids.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Young-onset amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, Spinal muscular atrophy, Anterior horn syndrome, Hirayama disease
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 21-year-old man with progressive weakness of left distal hand, no pain or sensory loss.  
- MRI in neutral neck position shows mild spinal cord atrophy at C5âC7 level, specifically T2 and T1 sagittal images.  
- T2 axial images show atrophy of the left anterior horn cells.  
- MRI in flexed neck position shows anterior displacement of the posterior dural wall from C3âTh3, with a crescent-shaped high signal intensity epidural venous plexus on T1 and T2 (with flow voids).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Young-onset amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)  
   - Pros: Young age, progressive weakness, selective involvement of anterior horn cells can be seen in ALS.  
   - Cons: ALS is a relentlessly progressive neurodegenerative disease, typically more generalized and symmetric onset with upper and lower motor neuron signs and no characteristic MRI findings like dural shift or epidural venous plexus; also, the age is very young for ALS.  

2) Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA)  
   - Pros: Anterior horn cell disorder causing atrophy and weakness; can be early onset.  
   - Cons: Usually presents diffusely with proximal muscle weakness; no specific cervical MRI findings with dural shift or venous plexus; SMA is a genetic disorder typically diagnosed clinically and genetically, not by imaging.  

3) Anterior horn syndrome  
   - Pros: General term that could explain anterior horn cell atrophy and motor weakness.  
   - Cons: Too nonspecific and does not explain the specific imaging findings, including flexion-induced dural shift and epidural venous plexus; a catch-all term lacking pathognomonic imaging.  

4) Hirayama disease  
   - Pros: Classic imaging findings include focal cervical spinal cord atrophy (often C5âC7), selective anterior horn cell loss, anterior dural displacement during neck flexion, and crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus with high T1/T2 signal and flow voids.  
   - Cons: Clinical presentation matches: young male, unilateral or asymmetrical distal arm/hand weakness without sensory loss or upper motor neuron signs.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Hirayama disease (best match on clinical and imaging grounds, especially the flexion-related dural shift and venous plexus).  
2) Anterior horn syndrome (too nonspecific, lacks imaging correlates).  
3) Young-onset ALS (less likely due to localized and slowly progressive unilateral weakness without UMN signs and characteristic MRI findings).  
4) Spinal muscular atrophy (does not match imaging findings or typical clinical distribution).  
</analysis>

<final>Hirayama disease</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old man was referred with a four-month history of persistent right upper quadrant pain radiating to the back. Liver function tests were normal, and he denied fever or pruritus. His medical history included pacemaker implantation, malaria, appendicectomy, arterial hypertension, and orthopaedic surgeries. No alcohol abuse or smoking was reported.
 Imaging Findings: The patient first underwent abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b), which revealed consistent findings: gallbladder hydrops with thin walls, moderate dilatation of the intrahepatic right bile ducts, and an ill-defined 1.5 cm soft-tissue lesion at the junction of the cystic duct and the common bile duct.
 
 Gadoxetic acid-enhanced MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 3a and 3b) confirmed tissue infiltration at the junction of the right anterior segmental duct and common bile duct, explaining the dilatation of the right anterior bile ducts.
 
 Endoscopic ultrasound and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) (Figure 4) were subsequently performed to perform biopsies and to place a plastic biliary stent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 A 28-year-old patient with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presented with chronic fatigue, diffuse abdominal pain, and hip joint discomfort exacerbated by walking.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 34-year-old patient with a known history of sickle cell disease underwent CT for left upper quadrant abdominal pain and chronic fatigue.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed a markedly reduced spleen size, with the parenchyma completely replaced by dense, coarse calcifications. The spleen had irregular but preserved contours. Post-contrast CT images did not show any enhancement within the splenic parenchyma. There was no perisplenic collection or lymphadenopathy.
 
 Bone window CT images through the pelvis revealed bilateral femoral head avascular necrosis (AVN), with subchondral collapse and cortical irregularities (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Case 2
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) demonstrated a severely atrophied spleen appearing as a densely calcified structure in the left upper quadrant. The calcifications are confluent and coarse, with no visible soft tissue component. Post-contrast images showed no contrast enhancement, and no other abdominal or bony abnormalities were identified.

The spleen had irregular but preserved contours. Post-contrast CT images did not show any enhancement within the splenic parenchyma. There was no perisplenic collection or lymphadenopathy. Bone window CT images through the pelvis revealed bilateral femoral head avascular necrosis (AVN), with subchondral collapse and cortical irregularities. Case 2: Abdominal CT scan demonstrated a severely atrophied spleen appearing as a densely calcified structure in the left upper quadrant. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 12-year-old boy without significant previous medical history presented in the ultrasound department for evaluation of a palpable lump noticed since the last two weeks in the left breast. The lump was non-tender and showed no changes in the overlying skin. There was no history of medication use. The hormonal profile and routine CBC profile were normal.
 Imaging Findings: Focused high-resolution ultrasound of both breasts and each axilla was performed using a high-frequency linear probe. The ultrasound identified a well-defined, well-encapsulated, hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins (Figure 1). It measured 3.8 x 2 x 3 cm in size and involved the left breast at the 12 o’clock periareolar region. The lesion was oriented parallel to the tissue planes (Figure 3). A single gentle lobulation was seen in this lesion. No significant vascularity was displayed on the colour Doppler study (Figure 2). The right breast was normal, and both axillae were normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male child from a tuberculosis (TB) endemic region presented with complaints of poor feeding, loss of weight for a month, and a low-grade, evening fever without any associated rash or chills for 15 days. The patient also had jerky movements of the right upper and lower limbs without any other signs of seizure disorder.
 
 The patient showed positive Kernig’s (pain and resistance to knee extension on flexing the patient’s hip and knee to 90 degrees and then attempting to extend the knee) [1] and Brudziński’s signs (in a patient with supine position, positive Brudziński’s is involuntary flexion of the hips and knees when the patient’s neck is passively flexed) [2].
 Imaging Findings: As the patient showed positive Kernig’s and Brudziński’s signs, meningeal TB was suspected. However, the chest radiograph was normal. On MRI, subtle T2 FLAIR (fluid attenuation inversion recovery) hyperintense minimally enhancing foci were seen in the bilateral cerebral cortex, caudate nucleus, thalami, and right hemipons with diffusion restriction and without blooming on gradient echo (GRE) sequences (Figures 1a and 1b). Subtle areas of leptomeningeal enhancement along the bilateral frontoparietal region were also seen. Mild communicating hydrocephalus was seen.
 
 A follow-up scan was advised after 4 months of antituberculosis treatment (ATT), as the patient had reduced activity, drowsiness, and episodes of seizure. On follow-up MRI, multiple conglomerated T2 hyperintense T1 fat-sat ring-enhancing lesions were seen in bilateral gangliocapsular and periventricular regions with leptomeningeal enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 2a and 2b). Mild surrounding cerebral oedema was also noted.

B) Candidate pass/fade with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Cerebral abscess  
- Pros: Ring-enhancing lesions can be seen in abscesses; diffusion restriction is typical for abscesses.  
- Cons: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions in typical TB sites (gangliocapsular, periventricular) after ATT is less typical; leptomeningeal enhancement and hydrocephalus are more typical of TB meningitis; clinical context supports TB rather than bacterial abscess.  
2) Neurocysticercosis  
- Pros: Ring-enhancing lesions are classic in NCC; T1 fat-sat images may show ring enhancement in later stages.  
- Cons: Diffuse involvement (bilateral gangliocapsular and periventricular) less common; leptomeningeal enhancement and initial meningitis signs are uncommon; patient is from TB endemic area with signs pointing to TB meningitis.  
3) Space-occupying lesion  
- Pros: Ring-enhancing lesions can be space-occupying tumors.  
- Cons: Clinical history and initial MRI with leptomeningeal involvement and multiple lesions argue against neoplasm; space-occupying lesions usually do not show diffuse minimal enhancement and diffusion restriction as in this case.  
4) Subacute infarct/haemorrhage  
- Pros: Diffusion restriction can be seen in acute infarcts.  
- Cons: Multiple bilateral ring-enhancing lesions, leptomeningeal enhancement, clinical signs, and progression after ATT do not fit infarct or hemorrhage; infarcts rarely cause ring enhancement with edema in multiple sites.  
5) Disease progression/Drug-resistant tuberculosis  
- Pros: Child clinically worsened before follow-up despite ATT, with progression on MRI (increase in size and number of lesions), fitting drug resistance or disease progression.  
- Cons: Leptomeningeal enhancement and multiple ring-enhancing lesions fit TB pathology; no direct evidence of drug resistance discussed, however clinical worsening supports disease progression.  
6) Paradoxical tuberculomas post-antituberculosis treatment  
- Pros: Paradoxical reaction is known to cause development or enlargement of tuberculomas during ATT, even with clinical worsening; multiple ring-enhancing lesions, leptomeningeal enhancement, and hydrocephalus fit this pattern; patient worsened despite treatment.  
- Cons: Sometimes paradoxical reactions occur early or late during treatment; no other complications described, but findings match paradoxical exacerbation.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Paradoxical tuberculomas post-antituberculosis treatment  
2) Disease progression/Drug-resistant tuberculosis  
3) Cerebral abscess  
4) Neurocysticercosis  
5) Space-occupying lesion  
6) Subacute infarct/haemorrhage  

The paradoxical reaction best fits the timing (4 months post ATT initiation), worsening clinical symptoms despite treatment, and imaging findings of multiple ring-enhancing lesions in classic TB sites with leptomeningeal enhancement and hydrocephalus. Disease progression/drug resistance is a close alternative but paradoxical reaction is a well-known specific entity in TB meningitis with lesion progression after ATT in the absence of treatment failure signs.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An eight-month-old female child presented with jaundice and abdominal distension. Clinical examination revealed icterus, a distended stomach, and prominent superficial veins. Laboratory reports showed elevated serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase and serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase levels with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (USG) showed grossly coarsened echotexture of liver with multiple tiny communicating and non-communicating cystic lesions (Figures 1a and 1c). A slightly irregular liver outline was also noted (Figure 1c), indicating liver cirrhosis. Figure 1b shows a large intrahepatic cyst near the porta hepatis containing echogenic debris in dependent location, measuring 2.2 x 1.1 cm (indicating Caroli disease). Many sections also showed ill-defined hypoechoic nodules in the liver, indicative of dysplastic nodules (Figure 1d). The gallbladder was distended, showing an elongated gallbladder neck with a thickened wall (2.4 mm) and increased pericholecystic echogenicity (Figure 2a). Mild dilatation of the cystic duct was also noted (Figure 2b). Another section showed both liver and right kidney in profile with increased renal parenchymal echotexture (Figure 3). Colour Doppler assessment showed absent colour flow in cystic structures, suggesting cystic lesions to be biliary dilatations (Figures 4a and 4b). A splenomegaly of 8 cm with a dilated (10 mm) splenic vein was noted (Figure 6).

  - Pros: Combines liver fibrosis/cirrhosis (irregular liver outline, coarsened echotexture, portal hypertension signs like splenomegaly), intrahepatic bile duct dilatations with large cystic structures near porta hepatis, dysplastic nodules, distended gallbladder with thickened wall and ductal dilation; fits clinical and imaging picture perfectly.  
  - Cons: Renal changes (increased echotexture) can be associated with Caroli syndrome (sometimes polycystic kidneys).  

C) Ranking:  
1) Caroli syndrome (congenital hepatic fibrosis with Caroli disease) - best explains all imaging findings including liver fibrosis, intrahepatic cystic dilations, dysplastic nodules, portal hypertension signs, and possible renal involvement.  
2) Choledochal cyst/Caroli disease - important component but doesn't cover fibrosis or portal hypertension.  
3) Cirrhosis (viral/alcoholic) - part of picture but insufficient to explain bile duct dilation.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old girl with normal hearing and cognition had severe dysarthria and language delay since birth. Neurological exam showed no motor deficits but significant expressive impairment. Given the persistent speech delay, a brain MRI was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI images demonstrated the eye-of-the-tiger sign. There is marked hypointensity of the globus pallidus on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences secondary to iron deposition, containing minimal dot-like hyperintensities (vacuolisation), associated with comma-shaped high-signal intensity in the antero-medial part of the globus pallidus, likely due to gliosis (Figures 1 and 2). There is also marked hypointensity on both T2*EG and DWI (diffusion-weighted images) sequences due to susceptibility effects (Figures 3 and 4).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- The patient is an 11-year-old girl with severe dysarthria and language delay since birth, but otherwise normal motor function.  
- MRI shows the eye-of-the-tiger sign: marked T2 hypointensity in the globus pallidus due to iron deposition, with central dot-like hyperintensities (vacuolation) and comma-shaped high signal (gliosis).  
- Significant susceptibility artifacts on T2* and DWI sequences.  
- The presence of the eye-of-the-tiger sign is a hallmark imaging feature.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Wilson’s disease  
- Pros: Wilson’s disease also causes basal ganglia abnormalities and can present with neurological symptoms.  
- Cons: Typically shows mixed signal changes including hyperintensity in the putamen and midbrain; the eye-of-the-tiger sign is not characteristic. Also Wilson's presents with liver dysfunction which is not mentioned here.  

2) Aceruloplasminemia  
- Pros: Causes iron deposition in basal ganglia, particularly globus pallidus.  
- Cons: Usually presents in adulthood or late adolescence, and the eye-of-the-tiger sign is not typical.  

3) Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) disorder  
- Pros: NBIA group disorders typically involve iron deposition in basal ganglia; the eye-of-the-tiger sign is a hallmark of NBIA.  
- Cons: NBIA is an umbrella term; further subtyping needed.  

4) Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN)  
- Pros: PKAN is the most common NBIA disorder; it is characterized by the eye-of-the-tiger sign on MRI (T2 hypointensity with central hyperintensity in globus pallidus). Typically presents in childhood with motor or speech abnormalities.  
- Cons: None significant; fits well with age and symptoms.  

5) Infantile bilateral striatal necrosis  
- Pros: Can present with basal ganglia abnormalities.  
- Cons: Usually shows necrosis and cystic changes, different from iron deposition; eye-of-the-tiger sign not typical.  

6) Organophosphate poisoning  
- Pros: Acute poisoning may show basal ganglia changes.  
- Cons: Eye-of-the-tiger sign is not typical; more diffuse or acute changes expected. Not applicable here due to chronic presentation.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN) - matches eye-of-the-tiger sign precisely, age and clinical features fit.  
2) Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) disorder - umbrella term includes PKAN but less specific.  
3) Wilson’s disease - less specific imaging, other clinical features absent.  
4) Infantile bilateral striatal necrosis - imaging and clinical less fitting.  
5) Organophosphate poisoning - clinical history and imaging inconsistent.  
6) Aceruloplasminemia - usually adult presentation, less typical imaging and clinical features.  

Conclusion: The imaging hallmark, clinical presentation, and age strongly favor PKAN.  
</analysis>

<final>Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old boy, previously healthy, presented with fever, cough, dyspnoea, vomiting and diarrhoea. Eighteen days after hospital admission, he had the first spontaneous bilateral pneumothorax, needing drainage, intensive care unit admission, and mechanical ventilation. After 3 months, even with remaining lung lesions and pneumothorax, he could be discharged for outpatient follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The initial imaging exam was a chest radiograph (X-ray) showing a bilateral diffuse micronodular acinar infiltrate (Figures 1a and 1b). A computed tomography (CT) was performed, which revealed a diffuse micronodular pattern and random distribution throughout the lung parenchyma, suggestive of an inflammatory/infectious process (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The patient experienced several pneumothoraces, all of which were drained. Up to three chest tubes were placed simultaneously (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 3e, and 3f). A second contrast-enhanced chest CT revealed confluent cystic lesions, prevailing on the upper lobes and posterior regions (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f).
 
 A few weeks later, another contrast-enhanced chest CT showed the same findings, but with larger pneumothoraces on both sides, despite the presence of three thoracic drains (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, and 5e). Pneumomediastinum was noted.
 
 Before hospital discharge, the patient underwent a new contrast-enhanced chest CT. The lung abnormalities persisted, but the patient was able to tolerate them even after the chest tubes were removed (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c).
 
 Eight months later, a follow-up chest CT revealed almost complete recovery of the pulmonary abnormalities, with sequelae changes and discreet cystic lesions, and with favourable evolution of the imaging findings over the eleven-month follow-up period (Figures 7a, 7b, and 7c).
PostDescription:

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient is a 9-year-old boy with fever, cough, dyspnea, vomiting, and diarrhea.  
- Initial chest x-ray: bilateral diffuse micronodular acinar infiltrate.  
- Initial CT: diffuse micronodular pattern with random distribution, suggestive of inflammatory/infectious process.  
- Developed multiple bilateral pneumothoraces, requiring drainage and ICU care.  
- Follow-up CTs showed confluent cystic lesions, mainly in upper lobes and posterior regions, increasing pneumothorax extension and pneumomediastinum.  
- Over time, with management (chest tubes), pulmonary abnormalities improved and eventually showed near-complete recovery with residual small cystic lesions and sequelae.  
- Over the follow-up period, there was resolution of most pulmonary lesions, suggestive of a reversible or treatable process.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP):  
- Pros: Can show diffuse micronodular infiltrates and cystic lesions; can cause spontaneous pneumothorax; immunocompromised patients or severe infections often have this.  
- Cons: Typically seen in immunocompromised hosts (HIV, immunosuppression), whereas the patient was previously healthy; cystic changes are more common in long-standing or severe cases; no mention of immunosuppression or HIV; random micronodular pattern is unusual; PJP does not usually result in confluent cystic areas evolving to large pneumothoraces.  

2) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (LIP):  
- Pros: Can cause diffuse ground-glass opacities and cystic lesions, usually bilateral and diffuse.  
- Cons: More common in older patients, associated with autoimmune disease or HIV; not typically seen in healthy children; no mention of relevant systemic disease.  

3) Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH):  
- Pros: Commonly affects teenagers/young adults; shows cystic lesions often in upper lobes; cysts can be bizarre and irregular; can cause spontaneous pneumothorax; diffuse micronodular pattern consistent with early LCH, evolving into cysts; smoking not usually a factor in children but can be.  
- Cons: 9-year-old is uncommon but possible; no mention of typical nodules with cavitation or other systemic LCH features; no skin or other organ involvement mentioned.  

4) Cystic pulmonary tuberculosis:  
- Pros: Can cause cystic lesions; tuberculosis can cause pneumothorax.  
- Cons: Typically see cavitary lesions and consolidation; cystic changes not as diffuse or confluent; no mention of typical TB findings or granulomas; age is possible but less common; no mention of TB treatment.  

5) Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM):  
- Pros: Cystic lung disease predominantly in women of childbearing age.  
- Cons: Patient is a male child; LAM is almost exclusive to women; not applicable.  

6) Birt–Hogg–Dubé syndrome (BHDS):  
- Pros: Can present with multiple lung cysts, spontaneous pneumothorax.  
- Cons: Genetic syndrome with skin and kidney involvement; rare in children; no mention of skin lesions or family history; cysts are usually thin-walled and less confluent.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH): Matches best with diffuse micronodular random distribution evolving to confluent cysts predominantly in upper lobes, occurrence of pneumothoraces, and improvement over time. The age is unusual but LCH can affect children and present with respiratory symptoms and pneumothorax.  
2) Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia: Possible due to cysts and pneumothorax, but less likely in a previously healthy child without immunosuppression, and typical radiological features and course less matching.  
3) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia: Less likely due to age and lack of systemic disease.  
4) Cystic pulmonary tuberculosis: Less likely due to absence of typical TB findings.  
5) Birt–Hogg–Dubé syndrome: Improbable in a 9-year-old boy without systemic findings.  
6) Lymphangioleiomyomatosis: Excluded due to gender and age.  

Therefore, the most consistent diagnosis is Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH).  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Langerhans cell histiocytosis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old female patient underwent follow-up at our institution for 14 years due to a pancreatic head lesion. The lesion was monitored at six-month intervals through ultrasound, computed tomography, or magnetic resonance imaging, revealing sustained growth throughout the surveillance period. However, the patient remained asymptomatic, and laboratory tests were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: Cross-sectional imaging of the upper abdomen, namely computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), revealed a multicystic lesion in the pancreatic head, with lobulated margins, enhancing internal septations (Figures 1, 2, and 3) and a central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications (Figures 1a–1h). On ultrasound, the lesion depicts multiple hypoechoic/anechoic cysts and hyperechoic septations (Figure 4).
 
 Over time, the lesion’s enlargement led to gradual dilatation of both the bile ducts and the main pancreatic duct. In the most recent magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 2a–2i), the pancreatic lesion measured approximately 97 x 86 x 126 mm (length x width x anteroposterior axis). The common bile duct had a calibre of 15 mm, and the main pancreatic duct had a maximum calibre of 7 mm. The pancreatic parenchyma was atrophic.

DifferentialDiagnosisList: Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas, Macrocystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas, Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas, Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) of the pancreas, Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas
FinalDiagnosis: Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- A multicystic lesion in the pancreatic head with lobulated margins and enhancing internal septations.  
- Presence of a central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications.  
- Ultrasound shows multiple hypoechoic/anechoic cysts with hyperechoic septations.  
- Lesion is progressively enlarging over 14 years, now measuring approximately 97 x 86 x 126 mm.  
- Causes dilation of both the common bile duct (15 mm) and main pancreatic duct (7 mm).  
- Atrophic pancreatic parenchyma.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas  
- Pros: Multicystic lesion with enhancing septations and central scar with stellate calcifications are classic for serous cystadenomas. Typically microcystic pattern includes multiple small cysts.  
- Cons: Giant serous cystadenoma is rare but fits with large size and central scar. Typically benign and slow-growing, consistent with long follow-up and no symptoms.  

2) Macrocystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas  
- Pros: Also a subtype of serous cystadenoma with predominantly few large cysts (macrocystic).  
- Cons: Macrocystic serous cystadenoma may lack the classic central scar and calcifications. Here there is a central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications, which is more typical for microcystic serous cystadenoma, though macrocystic can also present similarly.  

3) Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas  
- Pros: Typically located in pancreatic body/tail of women, large cystic lesion possible.  
- Cons: Usually a uniloculated or multiloculated cyst with ovarian-type stroma, not multicystic with central scar and calcifications. Can enlarge and cause duct dilatation but central scar/calciifications less common. Lab tests unremarkable, no mention of elevated tumor markers.  

4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) of the pancreas  
- Pros: Can cause segmental ductal dilatation, multicystic appearance.  
- Cons: Typically communicates with the pancreatic duct, no mention of ductal communication. Central scar and stellate calcifications are not typical. Moreover, IPMNs are usually not multicystic with many septations and have mucinous cystic fluid, not the typical microcystic cystadenomas.  

5) Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas  
- Pros: Occurs in young women typically, can be large cystic lesions.  
- Cons: Usually has solid and cystic components with characteristic imaging not described here (no solid components described, no hemorrhagic features). The age and lack of solid components or typical imaging features argue against this.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- 1) Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma fits best: central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications and microcystic pattern (multiple small cysts, multilobulated) with septations and classic imaging features of serous cystadenoma. The lesion is giant and slowly growing over years, which is typical for serous cystadenomas.  
- 2) Macrocystic serous cystadenoma is a possibility but central scar and stellate calcifications favor the microcystic type.  
- 3) Mucinous cystic neoplasm, IPMN, and solid pseudopapillary neoplasm are less consistent with central scar and calcifications and typical demographic/lab findings.  

Final choice: Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas  
</analysis>

<final>Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old female presented to the surgery outpatient department with complaints of painless swelling, posterior to the left knee, for 20 years, with a recent onset of pain (since 3 months) after getting hit by an animal at the site of swelling. On examination, the swelling was firm and lobulated with no overlying skin changes. There was a history of weight loss and loss of appetite. No other comorbidities. No family history of malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A plain lateral radiograph of the knee demonstrated a soft tissue lesion seen on the posterior aspect of the knee (Figure 1).
 
 Soft tissue ultrasound (US) demonstrated a large lobulated and irregularly shaped heterogeneously hypoechoic mass lesion with internal vascularity, seen posterior to the left knee (Figure 2 ). A few central hypoechoic necrotic/cystic areas were also seen. The mass was also seen displacing the popliteal vessels superficially.
 
 MRI (with contrast) findings revealed a large T1 heterointense lobulated mass lesion in the popliteal fossa with patchy areas of T1 hyperintensity (Figure 3). It also showed heterointense signal on T2W images (Figure 4). OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old male, HIV-1 positive since age 22 with no follow-up for 4 years, presented with progressive dyspnoea, cough, thoracic pain, and fatigue. Initially treated for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with partial symptom improvement, but later experienced worsening respiratory symptoms. Denied fever or night sweats.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray revealed mediastinal widening, likely due to a mass, accompanied by ill-defined lower lung opacities and bilateral pleural effusion, more pronounced on the right (Figure 1).
 
 Thoracic CT demonstrated a large anterior mediastinal mass (10.8 × 5.3 × 7.6 cm), heterogeneously enhancing with cystic areas and displacement of adjacent vascular structures without invasion, likely representing a conglomerate of necrotic hypervascular lymph nodes (Figure 2). Additional mediastinal adenopathies were noted.
 
 Pulmonary findings included bilateral “flame-shaped” peribronchovascular opacities (Figure 3a), patchy ground-glass opacities, and interlobular septal thickening (Figure 3b). Bilateral pleural effusion was also evident. Systemic hypervascular adenopathies involved the axillary, mesenteric, and inguinal regions bilaterally (short diameter up to 1.7 cm) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).
 
 Abdominal imaging revealed cutaneous millimetric nodules in the thoracic and abdominal walls (Figure 5), hepatomegaly (17.9 cm in craniocaudal length), and gastric wall thickening (Figures 6a and 6b).
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 36-year-old male, HIV-1 positive with poor follow-up, presenting with progressive dyspnoea, cough, thoracic pain, and fatigue.  
- Chest X-ray: mediastinal widening due to a mass, bilateral pleural effusion, lower lung ill-defined opacities.  
- Thoracic CT: large anterior mediastinal mass (10.8 x 5.3 x 7.6 cm), heterogeneous enhancement with cystic areas, displacement but no invasion of vascular structures, suggesting necrotic hypervascular lymph nodes (conglomerate). Additional mediastinal adenopathies.  
- Pulmonary: bilateral "flame-shaped" peribronchovascular opacities, patchy ground-glass opacities, interlobular septal thickening.  
- Systemic hypervascular adenopathies in axillary, mesenteric, and inguinal regions.  
- Abdominal CT: cutaneous micronodules in thoracic and abdominal walls, hepatomegaly (17.9 cm), gastric wall thickening.  
- Absence of fever or night sweats, partial improvement after antibiotic therapy for CAP, but worsening symptoms thereafter.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: anterior mediastinal mass with necrotic lymphadenopathy, systemic lymphadenopathy, pulmonary infiltrates, systemic involvement, hypervascular adenopathies.  
   - Cons: no definitive biopsy, but clinical picture is classic for lymphoma, especially in HIV patients.  

2) Atypical pneumonia  
   - Pros: cough, chest pain, bilateral opacities.  
   - Cons: presence of a large mediastinal mass and systemic adenopathies argue against simple infection; only partial response to antibiotics.  

3) Mycobacterial infection  
   - Pros: HIV-positive, mediastinal adenopathy, pleural effusions common.  
   - Cons: No fever, no night sweats, no classic miliary pattern, and mass is hypervascular and large conglomerate, less typical.  

4) Multicentric Castleman disease  
   - Pros: hypervascular lymphadenopathy, systemic involvement, HIV-positive, mediastinal mass common.  
   - Cons: Multicentric Castleman's can have similar imaging, but lung parenchymal "flame-shaped" opacities and peribronchovascular distribution less typical; also gastric wall thickening and cutaneous nodules less common.  

5) Disseminated AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma  
   - Pros: HIV-positive, systemic hypervascular adenopathies, cutaneous micronodules, gastric wall thickening, mediastinal mass from hypervascular nodes, bilateral peribronchovascular thickening ("flame-shaped") and ground-glass opacities typical for KS pulmonary involvement. Also hepatomegaly possible from KS involvement.  
   - Cons: The mass could be due to necrotic lymph nodes, but in KS this is common due to nodular vascular tumor infiltration.  

6) Lymphangitic carcinomatosis  
   - Pros: interlobular septal thickening and patchy ground-glass opacities.  
   - Cons: Patient is young, HIV-positive, no known primary malignancy, anterior mediastinal mass hypervascular and lymphadenopathic pattern better explained by above causes.  

7) Pulmonary oedema  
   - Pros: interstitial edema patterns possible.  
   - Cons: Bilateral pleural effusion present, but mass and systemic adenopathies argue against pure cardiac cause; absence of clinical signs of heart failure.  

8) Sarcoidosis  
   - Pros: mediastinal adenopathy and pulmonary infiltrates common.  
   - Cons: Hypervascular adenopathies, cystic necrotic areas rare in sarcoidosis; presence of cutaneous nodules and gastric thickening less classic; HIV status less typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Disseminated AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma

Rationale: The combination of HIV-positive status, hypervascular systemic lymphadenopathy (including large anterior mediastinal mass with cystic necrotic areas), bilateral peribronchovascular "flame-shaped" opacities, patchy ground-glass opacities, interlobular septal thickening, bilateral pleural effusions, cutaneous micronodules, gastric wall thickening, and hepatomegaly is highly characteristic of disseminated Kaposi sarcoma. The mass is likely due to a conglomerate of necrotic hypervascular lymph nodes replaced by KS lesions. While lymphoma is a close differential, the skin and gastrointestinal involvement, typical peribronchovascular infiltrates, and the pattern of enhancement support KS over lymphoma.

</analysis>  
<final>Disseminated AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-day-old female newborn was brought to our hospital because of left breast tumefaction for 2 days, with localised erythema and irritability. No fluctuance or nipple discharge was noted. No fever was present. The white blood cell count was elevated. The delivery was eutocic (40 weeks and 2 days) and unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The right breast was unremarkable (Figure 1a). The breast bud is hypoechoic relative to subcutaneous fat. The left lower quadrant’s mammary tissue was poorly marginated and more echogenic than the contralateral side (Figure 1b), with increased Doppler colour flow in the lower quadrants (Figure 2a). In the inferior aspect of the quadrants, an avascular area without colour flow was noted, measuring 4 x 3 x 3 mm, with through-transmission and surrounding hyperaemia (Figure 2b). The subcutaneous tissue was thick and hyperechoic.
PostDescription:

The left lower quadrant’s mammary tissue was poorly marginated and more echogenic than the contralateral side, with increased Doppler colour flow in the lower quadrants. In the inferior aspect of the quadrants, an avascular area without colour flow was noted, measuring 4 x 3 x 3 mm, with through-transmission and surrounding hyperaemia. DifferentialDiagnosisList: Neonatal physiological breast hypertrophy, Neonatal mastitis, Neonatal mastitis with abscess, Neonatal mastitis with cyst
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Infant (19 days old), left breast tumour with local erythema and irritability (no fluctuance or nipple discharge).  
- Elevated white blood cell count.  
- Imaging: right breast normal. On left breast, the breast bud was hypoechoic relative to subcutaneous fat. The lower quadrants' mammary tissue poorly marginated and more echogenic, with increased Doppler color flow in lower quadrants.  
- At the inferior aspect of the quadrants, an avascular area without color flow measuring 4x3x3 mm with through-transmission and surrounding hyperemia.  
- Subcutaneous tissue thickened and hyperechoic.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons:  
1) Neonatal physiological breast hypertrophy  
   Pros: Newborn, breast is symmetrical usually, physiological enlargement can be bilateral and painless.  
   Cons: This case is unilateral with erythema, irritability, elevated WBC (sign of infection/inflammation), increased Doppler flow in mammary tissue, thickened hyperechoic subcutaneous tissue, and avascular hypoechoic area with surrounding hyperemia; plus presence of erythema and local irritation all point against mere hypertrophy.  

2) Neonatal mastitis  
   Pros: Newborn with breast erythema, irritability, elevated WBC, sonographic signs of increased blood flow (hyperemia), poorly marginated mammary tissue, thickened hyperechoic subcutaneous tissue - all consistent with mastitis, acute inflammation.  
   Cons: No fluctuance or discharge; but mastitis can occur without abscess formation.  

3) Neonatal mastitis with abscess  
   Pros: Presence of an avascular area with through-transmission typical of a fluid-filled collection or abscess; inflammation signs; subcutaneous thickening.  
   Cons: No fluctuance clinically noted; avascular area is small (4x3x3 mm), but abscess can be small; surrounding hyperemia suggests infection; elevated WBC.  

4) Neonatal mastitis with cyst  
   Pros: A small avascular area could represent a cyst; however, in mastitis scenario less likely.  
   Cons: Cysts usually are anechoic and well defined; here it is avascular with surrounding hyperemia - more consistent with abscess/pus; the rest of the findings point to infection with acute inflammatory changes.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Neonatal mastitis with abscess: The avascular, well-defined area with surrounding hyperemia suggests an abscess; the inflammation signs and elevated WBC support infection complicated by abscess formation.  
2) Neonatal mastitis: Abscess is a subset of mastitis; but abscess presence makes this more specific.  
3) Neonatal physiological breast hypertrophy: Unlikely due to erythema, elevated WBC, hyperemia, and subcutaneous changes.  
4) Neonatal mastitis with cyst: The avascular area with hyperemia and surrounding inflammation is more typical for abscess than cyst.  

Thus, best explanation = Neonatal mastitis with abscess.  
</analysis>  
<final>Neonatal mastitis with abscess</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old man presented with two months of right knee pain and swelling. He had recovered from COVID‑19 pneumonia three months earlier after receiving systemic steroids. During his stay in the hospital, the patient has received dexamethasone injection 8 mg/day through the intravenous route for 10 days with gradual tapering over the next 2 weeks. No evidence of diabetes mellitus or other systemic comorbidities. Localised tenderness over the patella was noted; there were no systemic symptoms or history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographs of the knee in anteroposterior, lateral, and skyline views revealed superficial sclerosis of the patella, along with a subtle erosion at its inferior pole (Figures 1a and 1b). There were no signs of fracture or significant soft tissue injury. Given the clinical context and radiographic findings, infection was suspected, and an MRI was recommended.
 
 On MRI, the patellar inferior pole appeared hypointense on T1 images (Figures 2a and 2b) and hyperintense on proton density (PD) fat-saturated sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Diffusion-weighted images (DWI) also indicated restriction (Figures 4a and 4b). The subchondral bone and articular cartilage remained largely intact, although a mild effusion was present. Post-contrast images showed thickening and enhancement of the synovium (Figures 5a and 5b). Osteomyelitis of the patella was considered likely.

OriginalDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination done on a Samsung RS80A ultrasound machine showed a bulky uterine body with hypoechoic endometrial collection (Figure 1). A heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with internal echogenic debris was noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figure 2). The left ovary appears normal.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed using a 1.5 Tesla GE MR machine. T2-weighted sagittal and axial images of the MRI pelvis show the presence of the fundus and body of the uterus, which is oriented in the right lateral position in the pelvic cavity, with the absence of the cervix (Figure 3). However, the vagina is visualised. T1 hyperintense and T2 mildly hypointense endometrial collection is noted within the endometrial cavity (Figures 3 and 4). A well-defined T1 hyperintense and predominantly T2 hypointense (T2 shading sign) cystic lesion with a thin wall is noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figures 5 and 6). Minimal pelvic ascites is noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old man known to have hypertension and type 2 diabetes experienced paraesthesia in his left hand and distal part of his left forearm for several months. On examination, multiple well-defined skin nodules were found, globally distributed, along with reduced sensation distally in the left upper limb.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus with intravenous contrast showed multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus, including the trunks and divisions (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 3), extending into the axilla and proximal parts of the upper arm, suggestive of neurofibromas.
 
 MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.

MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Neurofibromas, Brachial plexus schwannomas, Lymphoma, Brachial plexopathy, Metastases

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Middle-aged man with hypertension and diabetes  
- Clinical symptoms: paraesthesia in left hand and distal forearm for months, reduced distal sensation in left upper limb  
- Physical exam: Multiple well-defined, globally distributed skin nodules  
- Imaging: MRI brachial plexus with IV contrast shows multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular plexus, trunks and divisions, extending to the axilla and proximal upper arm  
- Brain and spine MRI: No abnormalities, specifically no vestibular schwannoma  
- Diagnosis: schwannomatosis  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Neurofibromas  
   - Pros: Well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal on MRI consistent with neurofibromas; multiple and widespread skin nodules can suggest neurofibromas; clinical symptoms consistent with nerve involvement  
   - Cons: No mention of neurofibromatosis type 1 stigmata (no café-au-lait spots, no Lisch nodules noted)  

2) Brachial plexus schwannomas  
   - Pros: Lesions in brachial plexus could be schwannomas  
   - Cons: Schwannomas usually solitary or few lesions, not multiple and widespread as described (multiple lesions including trunks, divisions, extending to axilla and upper arm); also, no vestibular schwannoma or CNS lesions argue against neurofibromatosis type 2 but is consistent with schwannomatosis; however, schwannomatosis typically presents with multiple schwannomas, but schwannomas rather than neurofibromas usually dominate  

3) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Lymphoma can involve brachial plexus and cause nodular lesions  
   - Cons: Imaging features of lymphoma generally more infiltrative and less well-marginated; no systemic signs or evidence of systemic disease described; skin nodules unlikely lymphoma

4) Brachial plexopathy  
   - Pros: Clinical symptoms consistent  
   - Cons: Brachial plexopathy is an umbrella term for nerve dysfunction, not a lesion classification; imaging shows multiple well-marginated lesions, not diffuse nerve enlargement or inflammatory changes  

5) Metastases  
   - Pros: Could cause nodular lesions  
   - Cons: No known primary cancer; no systemic symptoms; lesions are well-marginated and localized in brachial plexus without other systemic lesions, unlikely metastases  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Neurofibromas  
2) Brachial plexus schwannomas (less likely due to multiplicity and pattern)  
3) Brachial plexopathy (clinical term, not primary lesion)  
4) Lymphoma  
5) Metastases (least likely given no cancer history)  

Given multiple (12) well-marginated lesions in brachial plexus, trunks, divisions, and extending distally, best explained as multiple neurofibromas. Although schwannomatosis typically features multiple schwannomas, neurofibromas can also occur; the lack of CNS vestibular schwannoma rules out NF2. The skin nodules and multiple plexus lesions support neurofibromas as the most consistent diagnosis.  
</analysis>

<final>Neurofibromas</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 60-year-old female with a known history of chronic kidney disease, on monthly haemodialysis and type 2 diabetes mellitus, presented with confusion and lethargy for the past 2 days. There was no history of trauma, fever, or seizures. On examination, the patient was disoriented but exhibited no focal neurological deficits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT (Figure 1) shows bilateral symmetric hypodensity in the lentiform nuclei, with surrounding more hypodense internal and external capsules forming the “lentiform fork sign”. There is a sub-centimetric-sized subcortical calcification in the right parietal lobe.
 
 MRI brain T2-weighted (Figure 2) shows bilateral symmetric hyperintensity in the thalamus, lentiform nuclei, particularly in the putamen and globus pallidus, with marked symmetrical hyperintensity of the internal, external capsules and internal and external medullary laminae.
 
 MRI FLAIR axial section (Figure 3) through the basal ganglia shows symmetric hyperintensity in the external capsule, internal capsule and internal and external medullary laminae on both sides. There is also hyperintensity in bilateral thalamus, putamen and globus pallidi.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old man who had undergone a bilateral vasectomy 20 years earlier underwent a scrotal ultrasound due to mild right testicular discomfort. There was no history of trauma or associated symptoms, and no relevant physical examination or laboratory findings. He had no history of travel outside Europe.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound scan using a high-frequency linear transducer (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2) revealed multiple tiny hyperechoic foci within the left epididymal body, measuring less than 1 mm and exhibiting continuous oscillatory movement. The right epididymis had a homogeneous echotexture and normal, symmetric vascularisation using Doppler ultrasound. No abnormal findings were observed in the remaining scrotal ultrasound, including the right testicle and epididymis.
PostDescription:

The right epididymis had a homogeneous echotexture and normal, symmetric vascularisation using Doppler ultrasound. No abnormal findings were observed in the remaining scrotal ultrasound, including the right testicle and epididymis.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Scrotal filariasis, Dancing megasperm, Epididymitis

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 59-year-old man with mild right testicular discomfort, no trauma or systemic symptoms.  
- Imaging: Scrotal ultrasound with high-frequency linear transducer.  
- Left epididymal body: Multiple tiny hyperechoic foci (<1 mm) with continuous oscillatory movement ("dancing" appearance).  
- Right epididymis: Normal echotexture and vascularity.  
- No abnormalities in bilateral testicles or scrotum.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Scrotal filariasis:  
- Pros: Presence of mobile hyperechoic structures (often filarial worms) within the scrotal soft tissues is classically described.  
- Cons: Filariasis is rare in Europe (patient has no travel history outside Europe); typical manifestations include scrotal edema and hydrocele; no other signs of inflammation or systemic symptoms reported.  

2) Dancing megasperm:  
- Pros: The "dancing" hyperechoic foci in the epididymis are pathognomonic for megasperm; movement is continuous and oscillatory; typically seen in the epididymis post-vasectomy.  
- Cons: Usually occurs ipsilateral to vasectomy; here the patient had bilateral vasectomy 20 years ago, and findings are unilateral (left side). However, this is consistent with typical presentation.  

3) Epididymitis:  
- Pros: Could cause epididymal enlargement and hyperechoic areas if infection present.  
- Cons: Doppler ultrasound shows normal vascularization, arguing against infection; no clinical signs of inflammation; movement ("dancing" appearance) not typical for epididymitis.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Dancing megasperm: Classic "dancing" hyperechoic foci within epididymis post-vasectomy fits well; pathognomonic.  
2) Scrotal filariasis: Mobile hyperechoic foci possible, but epidemiology and lack of systemic signs make it less likely.  
3) Epididymitis: Imaging and clinical findings not supportive.  

Thus, the most appropriate diagnosis is Dancing megasperm.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Dancing megasperm

Clinical examination revealed a firm, non-tender, lobulated mass (~8x8 cm) involving bilateral labia with intact overlying skin. It shows mild homogeneous enhancement without necrotic areas (Figure 1d), a very smooth outline and a clear plane with the aorta, cardia and thoracic spine.
 
 MRI pelvis showed a large, lobulated, well-circumscribed mass (~9x7 cm) involving the clitoral region and bilateral labia majora. It appears T1 isointense (Figure 2b) and T2 hyperintense compared to that of skeletal muscle (Figures 2a and 2d), it shows diffusion restriction (Figures 2e and 2f) and a marked homogeneous contrast enhancement (Figure 2c). The mass was seen overhanging the urethral orifice and vaginal introitus without causing urinary outflow obstruction.
 
 Non-contrast PET-CT showed
 
 FDG-avid soft tissue lesions corresponding to the oesophageal wall thickening (SUV max = 9.2) (Figures 1e and 1f) and vulval mass (SUV max = 9.8) (Figures 2g and 2h). There were no calcifications, intralesional fat, or cystic areas within the lesions.

No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Child and mother both have severe limb anomalies: median cleft hand deformities, absence of certain metacarpals and metatarsals, syndactyly, and extra phalanges (right thumb).  
- Radiographs show bilateral median clefts, fused proximal phalanges of middle and index fingers, accessory middle phalanx in the thumb, absent metatarsals.  
- Radiograph of skull with "copper-beaten" appearance, indicating increased intracranial pressure or increased cortical gyral impressions (not typical for this phenotype).  
- Both mother and child have physical limb deformities affecting both hands and feet: missing fingers, hypoplastic index fingers, syndactyly of toes.  
- Clinical presentation fits a genetic/constitutional syndrome rather than an acquired condition.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Syndactyla  
   - Pros: Features syndactyly, which is present.  
   - Cons: No mention of median clefts or absent metacarpals/metatarsals; syndactyly alone does not explain the complex limb patterning.  

2) Roberts syndrome  
   - Pros: Typically involves severe limb defects including preaxial reduction defects, cleft hands/feet, and "copper-beaten" skull radiograph (pathognomonic). Also, autosomal recessive inheritance fits mother and child affected.  
   - Cons: No mention of growth retardation or craniofacial anomalies which often accompany Roberts syndrome, but these may be variable.  

3) Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM)  
   - Pros: Median clefts and missing digits are hallmark features; known as ectrodactyly, "lobster claw" deformity.  
   - Cons: Does not typically include absent metacarpals/metatarsals or the complexity of fusions and accessory bones as described; usually isolated limb deformities without cranial findings.  

4) Ectrodactyly-ectodermal dysplasia-cleft lip and palate (EEC)  
   - Pros: Median cleft hands/feet, ectodermal dysplasia, and facial clefts.  
   - Cons: No mention of ectodermal dysplasia signs or facial clefts; skull radiograph changes unrelated.  

5) Amniotic band sequence  
   - Pros: Can cause severe limb deformities and amputations.  
   - Cons: Usually sporadic and asymmetric; does not explain familial pattern or radiographic skull changes; no mention of band-related constriction.  

6) Lacrimo-Auriculo-dento-digital syndrome (LADD)  
   - Pros: Digital anomalies and possibly limb defects.  
   - Cons: Typically includes lacrimal duct agenesis, salivary gland anomalies, and other craniofacial features; hand/foot anomalies less severe and not median clefts.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Roberts syndrome (pros: median clefts, absent metacarpals/metatarsals, copper-beaten skull appearance, familial inheritance)  
2) Split hand/split foot malformation (pros: median clefts and digit loss, but lacking metacarpal/metatarsal absence and skull changes)  
3) Lacrimo-Auriculo-dento-digital syndrome (less severe limb defects, no skull findings)  
4) Amniotic band sequence (sporadic, asymmetric)  
5) Eectrodactyly-ectodermal dysplasia-cleft lip and palate (lack of ectodermal or facial findings)  
6) Syndactyla (too nonspecific; only syndactyly mentioned)  

Given the classic median clefts, absence of bones, accessory digits, and particularly the "copper-beaten" skull radiograph (a well-known pathognomonic finding in Roberts syndrome), along with familial involvement, Roberts syndrome fits best.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Roberts syndrome  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old male presented to the emergency department with weakness and paraesthesia in the lower limbs, back pain, and fever. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed an increased white blood cell count (predominantly polymorphonuclear) and protein. He had a history of gonococcal urethritis and syphilis and a recent influenza B respiratory infection.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) shows a longitudinally extensive posterior extradural extramedullary collection from T2 to T9 of heterogeneous appearance with isointense and hyperintense areas in T1-weighted sequence (Figure 1) and high signal intensity on T2-weighted and STIR sequences (Figure 2). It causes a compressive effect on the spinal cord with anterior thecal sac displacement (Figure 3). There is increased STIR and T2 cord signal in the compressed segment, suggestive of compressive myelopathy (Figure 2). The contrast-enhanced T1-weighted sequence shows diffuse meningeal spinal enhancement (Figures 4, 5a and 5b). The magnetic field strength of the MRI scanner used was 3T.

- Cons: No history of recent surgery is mentioned; CSF findings and systemic symptoms not consistent; imaging features and clinical context not supportive.

- Pros: Can cause extradural compression; can be seen on MRI.  
- Cons: Usually localized to one or two levels, not longitudinally extensive over many segments (T2-T9 is too long); enhancement and CSF pleocytosis not typical; clinical presentation does not fit.  
4) Spinal epidural abscess:

- Cons: None significant; clinical and imaging findings align well.  
5) Metastatic spinal disease:  
- Pros: Can cause extradural masses; can be extensive.  
- Cons: Usually hypointense or variable on T1; heterogeneous areas possible, but meningeal enhancement and CSF pleocytosis with fever not typical; clinical presentation with infection signs inconsistent.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Spinal epidural abscess (best match with imaging and clinical findings)  
2) Epidural haematoma (possible but less likely due to clinical and imaging mismatch)  
3) Metastatic spinal disease (unlikely due to infection signs)  
4) Disc herniation (unlikely due to length and other features)  
5) Postoperative spinal seroma (excluded due to no surgery history)  

Given the longitudinally extensive lesion, heterogeneous T1 signal, T2/STIR hyperintensity, meningeal enhancement, compressive myelopathy, fever, and CSF pleocytosis with neutrophils, the best diagnosis is spinal epidural abscess.

- Pros: Fluid collection that can cause compression.  
- Cons: No history of recent surgery is mentioned; CSF findings and systemic symptoms not consistent; imaging features and clinical context not supportive.  
3) Disc herniation:  
- Pros: Can cause extradural compression; can be seen on MRI.  
- Cons: Usually localized to one or two levels, not longitudinally extensive over many segments (T2-T9 is too long); enhancement and CSF pleocytosis not typical; clinical presentation does not fit.

- Cons: None significant; clinical and imaging findings align well.

- Pros: Can cause extradural masses; can be extensive.  
- Cons: Usually hypointense or variable on T1; heterogeneous areas possible, but meningeal enhancement and CSF pleocytosis with fever not typical; clinical presentation with infection signs inconsistent.

5) Postoperative spinal seroma (excluded due to no surgery history)  

Given the longitudinally extensive lesion, heterogeneous T1 signal, T2/STIR hyperintensity, meningeal enhancement, compressive myelopathy, fever, and CSF pleocytosis with neutrophils, the best diagnosis is spinal epidural abscess.

Clinical History: A 2-day-old female neonate born through normal vaginal delivery to non-consanguineous marriage was found to have dark pigmentation in the groin, perineum, lower back, upper and lower extremities, including hands, with hair growth in these areas (Figure 1). These did not show restricted diffusion or blooming on SWI. No signal changes were seen on T2 (Figure 3). No leptomeningeal T1 hyperintensities were seen. Post-contrast images showed no leptomeningeal or neuroparenchymal enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old boy presented with a chronic history of rhinosinusitis and productive cough for years. There were no associated symptoms of haemoptysis, fever, or weight loss. On examination, he had mild basal crepitations. Otherwise, no dysmorphic features or dextrocardia. No palpable organomegaly or lymphadenopathy. History of paternal aunt infertility was reported.
 Imaging Findings: Initial chest radiograph showed mild right lower zone faint opacification with no dextrocardia (Figure 1). The high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) revealed mild bilateral lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis with bronchial wall thickening and mucoid impaction as well as centrilobular nodules (Figures 2a and 2b). The rest of the lung parenchyma was well aerated. No situs inversus or dextrocardia was seen. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
 
 Paranasal sinuses (PNS) CT revealed near complete opacification of the paranasal sinuses with frothy secretions as well as associated pharyngitis and left-sided otomastoiditis (Figure 3).

Paranasal sinuses (PNS) CT revealed near complete opacification of the paranasal sinuses with frothy secretions as well as associated pharyngitis and left-sided otomastoiditis.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cystic fibrosis, Primary ciliary dyskinesia, Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA), Williams–Campbell syndrome
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 14-year-old boy  
- Chronic rhinosinusitis with frothy secretions in sinuses, PNS CT showing near complete opacification and pharyngitis, otomastoiditis  
- Chronic productive cough  
- Mild basal crepitations on exam  
- Chest radiograph and HRCT show mild bilateral lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis, bronchial wall thickening, mucoid impaction, centrilobular nodules  
- No dextrocardia or situs inversus on imaging  
- No mediastinal lymphadenopathy  
- Family history: paternal aunt infertility (no relevant for primary ciliary dyskinesia or CF directly)  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Cystic fibrosis  
- Pros: Similar bronchiectasis distribution; chronic productive cough; sinus disease common; mucoid impaction typical.  
- Cons: No mention of recurrent infections or pancreatic insufficiency; normal growth/unremarkable exam; no neonatal history; genetic confirmation not provided; no mention of clubbing or malnutrition. Usually significant lung disease by this age; bronchiectasis often bilateral and diffuse, not only lower lobes.  
2) Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD)  
- Pros: Chronic rhinosinusitis, bronchiectasis predominant in lower lobes, mucoid impaction, chronic productive cough; normal situs (situs solitus) possible as 50% have situs inversus, so no dextrocardia does not exclude PCD. Family history (paternal aunt infertility) supports possible autosomal recessive trait; infertility in females and males is a known PCD feature.  
- Cons: No mention of recurrent otitis media or ear infections (though left-sided otomastoiditis present); no infertility in patient yet (expected at older age); no obvious situs inversus but this is not required; no clear evidence of immotile sperm or ciliary microscopy.  
3) Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)  
- Pros: Mucoid impaction and central bronchiectasis could be ABPA.  
- Cons: ABPA is usually in asthmatic patients, with elevated IgE and eosinophilia, which is not mentioned; no recurrent mucus plugging or fleeting infiltrates; no history of asthma or atopy; no mention of hyperattenuating mucus or aspergilloma. Lower lobe bronchiectasis is not typical; usually central or upper lobe.  
4) Williams–Campbell syndrome  
- Pros: Bronchiectasis typical.  
- Cons: Rare, with congenital absence of bronchial cartilage leading to severe bronchiectasis, usually diffuse and severe by adolescence; no evidence of large airway malacia; not associated with chronic sinus disease; the clinical picture does not fit well.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD)  
2) Cystic fibrosis  
3) Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis  
4) Williams–Campbell syndrome  

Rationale: The combination of chronic rhinosinusitis with frothy secretions, bilateral lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis without situs inversus, and family history of infertility aligns best with PCD. Although no ciliary function test or electron microscopy is given, the clinical and imaging features are typical. Cystic fibrosis is less likely without other systemic features or more severe lung disease. ABPA and Williams–Campbell syndrome are less consistent with the overall clinical picture and imaging findings.  

</analysis>  
<final>Primary ciliary dyskinesia</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously well 12-year-old male presented to the accident and emergency department of a regional paediatric hospital with a 2-week history of worsening bilateral lower limb paraesthesia in the form of loss of fine touch, vibration and proprioception from the level of T5 distally, signifying mass effect on the dorsal columns with sparing of pain and temperature sensation. He also had reduced motor function of the lower limbs with clonus and brisk reflexes.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain was normal. MRI of the whole spine (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 1d, and 1e) demonstrated a large solitary solid cystic lesion, centred over the spinous process and the lamina of T5 bilaterally, more extensive on the right. The solid lesion is iso/mildly hypointense to bone, with areas of T1 and T2 hypointense signal, suggestive of calcifications. The solid component measured 3.4 x 3.7 x 4.4 cm (transverse x anteroposterior x craniocaudal) and demonstrated avid enhancement. The mass is expansile with cortical expansion and trabecular replacement. It extends into the spinal canal, causing severe canal stenosis and cord compression. There was evidence of bone marrow oedema within the body of T5. No other intra or extramedullary lesions were identified.
 
 A follow-up CT thorax, abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a and 2b) with bony reformats demonstrated an expansile lytic lesion with cortical expansion involving the posterior elements of T5. There was no bony destruction. There are areas of calcification seen within the internal osseous matrix. No other bony lesions were identified, and no abnormality of note was found in the thorax, abdomen or pelvis.

A) Key findings:  
- 12-year-old male with 2-week history of bilateral lower limb sensory loss (loss of fine touch, vibration, proprioception) from T5 distally, with sparing of pain and temperature (dorsal column involvement).  
- Reduced motor function with clonus and brisk reflexes (corticospinal tract involvement).  
- MRI brain normal.  
- MRI whole spine shows large solitary solid cystic lesion centered over T5 spinous process and lamina bilaterally (posterior elements), expansile with cortical expansion and trabecular replacement.  
- Solid component iso/mildly hypointense to bone with T1 and T2 hypointense signal suggesting calcifications.  
- Avid enhancement of the solid component.  
- Lesion causing severe canal stenosis and cord compression.  
- Bone marrow edema within T5 body.  
- CT confirms expansile lytic lesion with cortical expansion involving posterior elements, no bony destruction, internal calcifications present.  
- No other skeletal lesions.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Osteoblastoma  
   - Pros: Commonly affects posterior elements of spine in adolescents/young adults, expansile lesion with cortical expansion, internal calcifications possible, avid enhancement, can cause neurological symptoms due to mass effect.  
   - Cons: Typically does not cause marrow edema or bony destruction; usually benign and less aggressive than osteosarcoma.  

2) Fibrous dysplasia  
   - Pros: Can involve posterior elements, expansile, internal ground glass matrix (might mimic calcifications), occurs in adolescents.  
   - Cons: Usually less likely to cause severe canal stenosis and cord compression, minimal or no enhancement, usually not a solid cystic lesion with avid enhancement, less likely to cause cord compression symptoms acutely.  

3) Telangiectatic osteosarcoma  
   - Pros: Aggressive lesion with cystic component and hemorrhagic areas, can have internal calcifications, avid enhancement, marrow edema, cortical expansion and destruction possible.  
   - Cons: Rare in posterior elements, typically destructive rather than just expansile, more aggressive with poor prognosis, usually has aggressive periosteal reaction and soft tissue mass.  

4) Giant cell tumour  
   - Pros: Typically occurs in epiphyses near growth plates, may cause lytic expansile lesion, some calcifications possible.  
   - Cons: Rare in vertebrae (especially posterior elements), typically does not cause internal matrix calcification, less likely to cause marrow edema, usually not in the posterior elements as a solitary expansile lesion in this age.  

5) Ewing sarcoma  
   - Pros: Can affect vertebrae, aggressive lytic lesion, marrow edema, soft tissue mass possible, enhances avidly.  
   - Cons: Usually destructive with periosteal reaction, often presents with pain and systemic symptoms, less commonly an expansile lesion centered solely on posterior elements without soft tissue mass, internal calcifications uncommon.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Osteoblastoma: Fits age group, posterior element location, expansile lesion with cortical expansion, internal calcifications, avid enhancement, and neurological symptoms due to compression.  
2) Telangiectatic osteosarcoma: Aggressive features may match some findings, but location and lack of destructive features less typical.  
3) Ewing sarcoma: Possible, but less likely due to the lesion’s expansile nature and lack of periosteal reaction or soft tissue mass.  
4) Fibrous dysplasia: Possible internal calcifications and expansile lesion but less likely to cause such aggressive cord compression and marrow edema, less enhancement.  
5) Giant cell tumour: Unlikely given location and imaging features.  

Thus, the best match is Osteoblastoma.  
</analysis>  
<final>Osteoblastoma</final>
<|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 15-year-old female presented with a two-year history of progressively enlarging, painful swellings on both sides of the neck, along with a recent onset of abdominal pain of one week’s duration. Examination revealed multiple palpable swellings in the axillary, cervical and inguinal regions as well as café-au-lait spots over the extremities and trunk.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the neck revealed multiple oval and spindle-shaped hypoechoic lesions in the bilateral posterior triangle (Figures 1a and 1b) and the anterior neck region. Similar subcutaneous lesions were noted in bilateral level 4 regions (Figures 1c and 1d), abutting carotid vessels.
 
 The contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) abdomen revealed heterogeneously enhancing oval-shaped lesions abutting the duodenojejunal flexure (Figures 2a and 2b) and a similar enhancing oblong lesion in the pelvis, posterior to the rectum at L5–S1 level (Figures 3a,3b, and 3c). Multiple hypodense, hypoenhancing nodules were imaged in the mesentery encasing the inferior mesenteric artery from its origin (Figures 4a and 4b), and extending into the pelvis and bilateral retroperitoneum. Similar nodular lesions were seen extending from the neural foramina of the lumbar spine, extending along the course of nerve roots into sciatica foramina and along the sciatic nerve (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c). A few small nodules were also noted adjacent to bilateral femoral vessels (Figures 6a and 6b). Multiple variable-sized hypodense subcutaneous nodules were noted diffusely scattered in the abdominal wall.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old male agricultural worker from a rural area, with a long history of barefoot fieldwork, presented with a long-standing history of swelling over the plantar surface of the left foot, now progressing to involve the dorsal surface and with a history of a discharging sinus. The patient denies any history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: A clinical image of the plantar (Figure 1a) and dorsal surface (Figure 1b) of the left foot shows multiple soft and firm nodules that were nontender, along with no discharging sinus at present.
 
 X-ray of the left foot (Figure 2) and lateral views show soft-tissue density over the lateral aspect of the left foot with cortical remodelling of the diaphysis of the 4th metatarsal.
 
 Ultrasound shows multiple cystic lesions, with a few of them showing tiny echogenic grains in the dependent portion (dot in a circle) (arrow in Figure 3).
 
 On T2W sagittal (Figure 4a), soft tissue mass shows conglomerates of small (2–5 mm), rounded, hyperintense lesions with few of them showing central low-signal intensity dots and peripheral low-signal intensity rim around these hyperintense areas, representing a “dot-in-circle” sign. A few cystic areas are noted within this mass, which contains T2 hypointense foci—likely spores. On post-contrast T1 fat-saturated (FS) coronal image (Figure 4b), the involved region of soft tissue shows rim enhancement around the granulation tissue along with minimal enhancement of the central dot. There is sparing of the underlying bones and tendons. No evidence of marrow oedema noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A man in his mid-20s with a 2–3 week history of intermittent severe headache, photophobia, nausea and vomiting, presented with generalised seizures. Treatment for meningoencephalitis was given, and the patient improved. Two months later, he developed sudden bilateral lower limb weakness and pins and needles sensation below the waist.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed diffuse cortical and subcortical white matter T2 hyperintensity involving the hippocampi and parahippocampal gyri bilaterally and cortical signal abnormality in the left superior frontal lobe (Figures 1a and 1b). Two weeks later, an MRI (not shown) showed near complete resolution of the abnormal signal intensities. No abnormalities were seen in the cervicothoracic spine.
 
 MRI whole spine two months later showed extensive increased signal of the central cord spanning from C2 to T8 vertebrae with cord expansion (Figures 2a and 2b), and areas of ring-enhancing foci within the cord at C2 (Figures 2c and d). There were also confluent and continuous lesions involving the medial aspect of the right temporal lobe, optic tract, corticospinal tract and hypothalamus. This extends into the right cerebral peduncle and the pyramids of the right pons (Figure 3).
PostDescription:
