A) Key findings:  
- Young man with initial presentation of encephalitic symptoms (severe headache, photophobia, nausea, vomiting, seizures), initially treated as meningoencephalitis with improvement.  
- MRI shows diffuse cortical and subcortical T2 hyperintensity involving bilateral hippocampi and parahippocampal gyri, and left superior frontal lobe cortical signal abnormality, with near resolution on repeat MRI two weeks later.  
- Subsequent clinical deterioration with bilateral lower limb weakness and numbness.  
- MRI of spinal cord two months later shows extensive central cord T2 hyperintensity with cord expansion from C2 to T8, and ring-enhancing lesions at C2.  
- MRI brain shows confluent lesions in right medial temporal lobe, optic tract, corticospinal tract, hypothalamus, extending into right cerebral peduncle and pyramids of right pons.  
- No cervical-thoracic spine abnormalities on imaging immediately after initial brain findings.  
- The combination of brain (brainstem, corticospinal tract, optic tract, hypothalamus) and extensive longitudinally extensive spinal cord lesions with ring enhancement is notable.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Multiple sclerosis (MS):  
- Pros: Cervicothoracic spinal cord lesions, brain lesions in corticospinal tract.  
- Cons: MS lesions usually periventricular white matter, less often extensive central cord lesions with cord expansion; ring-enhancing central spinal cord lesions and involvement of hypothalamus, optic tracts are less typical; MRI initial brain lesions involving hippocampi are unusual; also optic tract involvement and extensive spinal cord lesions are atypical for MS.  

2) Neuro-Behçet’s disease:  
- Pros: Can cause brainstem and limb weakness; inflammatory lesions can involve CNS.  
- Cons: Usually involves variable brainstem and cerebellar regions, and meningoencephalitic picture can be seen; however spinal cord lesions usually peripheral or patchy, not extensive longitudinally extensive central cord with ring enhancement; no mention of systemic Behçet criteria; lower limb weakness and CNS imaging findings less typical.  

3) Aquaporin-4 IgG-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders (AQP4-NMOSD):  
- Pros: Classic longitudinally extensive spinal cord lesions (over >3 vertebral segments), central cord involvement with spinal cord expansion, ring-enhancing lesions at C2 typical; brain involvement includes optic tracts and diencephalic structures (hypothalamus), corticospinal tracts and area postrema, medial temporal lobe lesions can be seen; episodic encephalitic presentations are not uncommon; bilateral hippocampal involvement possible.  
- Cons: Clinical presentation initially as encephalitis unusual but can be compatible; no mention of serum AQP4 testing but imaging pattern is typical.  

4) Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody disease (MOGAD):  
- Pros: Can cause multifocal brain lesions including in cortical/subcortical regions and brainstem; spinal cord lesions present but usually less extensive and more asymmetric; imaging may show brain lesions and myelitis.  
- Cons: Lesions tend to be more patchy, can have leptomeningeal enhancement, but extensive longitudinally central spinal cord lesions and ring-enhancing lesions less common; optic tract involvement and hypothalamus involvement less typical.  

5) Neurosarcoidosis:  
- Pros: Can cause enhancing lesions in brain and spinal cord, often ring-enhancing; brainstem and spinal cord lesions possible; multiple organ involvement possible.  
- Cons: Usually systemic sarcoidosis signs; bilateral hippocampi involvement uncommon; spinal cord lesions usually intramedullary enhancing masses but less extensive longitudinally; no systemic signs or pulmonary involvement mentioned; clinical evolution less typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Aquaporin-4 IgG-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders (AQP4-NMOSD)  
2) Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody disease (MOGAD)  
3) Neuro-Behçet’s disease  
4) Neurosarcoidosis  
5) Multiple sclerosis  

The imaging pattern of extensive longitudinally extensive spinal cord lesion with central cord expansion and ring enhancement, involvement of optic tract, hypothalamus, corticospinal tracts, temporal lobe and the initial encephalitis-like presentation best matches classic AQP4-NMOSD.  

</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old child presented with a pricking type of upper abdominal pain on and off for 6 months. No history of fever, vomiting, or loss of appetite. No previous history of similar complaints. On examination, tenderness is present over the epigastric and left lumbar region. Routine laboratory investigations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound, a relatively well-defined heteroechoic lesion with a hyperechoic rim is noted in the retropancreatic area, with a focal exophytic hypoechoic area in the anterior aspect of the lesion (Figure 1). No obvious internal vascularity on colour Doppler.
 
 On contrast-enhanced (CE) CT, a ~ 6.8 x 7 x 8.1 cm well-defined, smoothly marginated, heterodense soft tissue density lesion (predominantly fat component in the posterior aspect with few small solid components and calcific focus in the anterior aspect) is noted in the retropancreatic region (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Regarding relations and mass effect, the lesion, superiorly, abuts the coeliac trunk and inferiorly displaces the third part of the duodenum. Anteriorly, it displaces the body and tail of the pancreas, abuts the splenic artery and mildly compresses the splenic vein. Posteriorly, abuts the left renal vein. Medially, there is a widening of the duodenal C loop, and laterally, a displacement of adjacent bowel loops. There is no obvious evidence of infiltration with the structures mentioned above or vascular complications (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).

A) Key findings:  
- 11-year-old child with intermittent upper abdominal pricking pain, localized tenderness.  
- Ultrasound: well-defined heteroechoic lesion with hyperechoic rim in retropancreatic area; focal exophytic hypoechoic area anteriorly; no internal vascularity.  
- Contrast-enhanced CT: lesion size ~7 cm, well-defined, smoothly marginated, heterodense with predominantly fat density posteriorly, few small solid components, calcific focus anteriorly.  
- Retropancreatic location: lesion abuts/coeliac trunk, duodenum (third part), pancreas (body and tail), splenic artery & vein, left renal vein, and causes mass effect (duodenal C loop widening, lateral bowel displacement).

- Cons: Patient age (11 years) is atypical; liposarcomas usually occur in older adults. Lesion is well-defined without infiltrative features, and no hypervascularity. Calcification is uncommon. Location retropancreatic but liposarcoma more typical in retroperitoneum, but age and well-defined nature are less typical.  
2) Mesenteric teratoma  
- Pros: Contains fat, calcification, solid components typical for teratoma. Possible in children.  
- Cons: Site is retropancreatic, not mesenteric. Location is not consistent; mesenteric teratoma usually arises from mesentery.  
3) Retropancreatic immature teratoma  
- Pros: Similar site; contains fat, solid components, calcification.  
- Cons: Immature teratomas typically present with more aggressive features, heterogeneous with solid components, possible calcification; less likely to be well-defined and smooth marginated. No mention of elevated tumor markers or systemic symptoms. Also, immature teratomas more common in younger children but can have more infiltrative or aggressive features radiologically.  
4) Retropancreatic mature teratoma  
- Pros: Lesion location matches exactly; mixture of fat posteriorly, small solid components, and calcification typical of mature cystic teratomas. Well-defined, smoothly marginated lesion with no infiltrative features or vascular invasion, consistent with benign mature teratoma. Patient's age (~11 years) is within typical age range. Imaging features: fat predominant, small solid components, calcific foci, hyperechoic rim and heteroechoic mass on US; well-circumscribed heterogeneous lesion with fat and calcification on CT, no invasion.  
- Cons: None.

2) Retropancreatic immature teratoma  
3) Liposarcoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension and vague pelvic discomfort. Physical examination revealed a palpable midline abdominal mass. Routine laboratory investigations were unremarkable. Abdominopelvic CT was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Unenhanced abdominopelvic CT demonstrated two large, well-defined, predominantly cystic pelvic masses (Figure 1).
 
 The right-sided lesion (22 × 15 × 19 cm), centred in the cul-de-sac and extending into the right iliac fossa, was intraperitoneal, displacing small-bowel loops superiorly and the uterus postero-leftward while preserving a fat plane with the right psoas muscle (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The left-sided mass (15 × 17 × 15 cm) occupied the left flank/iliac fossa, had a slightly irregular thick wall, and abutted the left psoas muscle, kidney, aorta and iliac vessels without an intervening fat plane, displacing ileo-jejunal loops anteromedially—features consistent with a retroperitoneal origin (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Compression of the left ureter produced moderate pelvicalyceal and proximal ureteral dilatation (Figure 4). A morphologically normal left ovary was identified caudal to the mass (Figure 5), whereas the right ovary was obscured. No ascites, peritoneal implants or pathological lymph nodes were present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old male presented with headache and giddiness for 3–4 months. He previously experienced diplopia for one month, which resolved following a right-sided medium-pressure ventriculoperitoneal shunt surgery 1 year ago.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed a predominantly cystic lesion with minimal solid components, measuring approximately 24 × 33 × 73 mm, centred in the lower fourth ventricle. It appeared T1 hypointense (Figure 3a) and heterogeneously T2 hyperintense, with multiple fluid-intensity cystic areas (Figure 1a). The solid areas demonstrated minimal post-contrast enhancement (Figure 3b), while the cystic portions remained non-enhancing. Mild diffusion restriction on diffusion weighted imaging–apparent diffusion coefficient (DWI–ADC) sequences (Figures 4a and 4b), and blooming foci were noted on fast field echo sequences, with corresponding PHASE hyperintensities, suggesting haemosiderin deposition (Figures 5a and 5b). CT screening showed tiny calcific foci.
 
 The lesion caused anterior displacement of the posterior pons (Figure 1a), medulla, and upper cervical cord, and abutted the cerebellar hemispheres, vermis, and both middle and inferior cerebellar peduncles. Posteroinferiorly, it extended into the cisterna magna via the foramen of Magendie and into the spinal canal up to 28 mm below McRae’s line. Lateral extension into both cerebellopontine angles was noted via the foramina of Luschka, with associated mild upstream hydrocephalus (Figures 1b and 2a).

- CT shows tiny calcific foci.
- Mass effect: anterior displacement of posterior pons, medulla, upper cervical cord, abutting cerebellar structures.
- Extension: through foramen of Magendie into cisterna magna and spinal canal (28 mm below McRae’s line), lateral extension into both cerebellopontine angles via foramina of Luschka.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

   - Typically cystic with mural nodule, often occurs in cerebellum in young patients.
   - Usually T1 iso to hypointense, T2 hyperintense, often cystic with robust solid nodule showing strong enhancement.
   - Usually no intraventricular location or prominent intraventricular extension.
   - Rarely shows haemosiderin or calcification; no typical foramen of Luschka extension.
   - Verdict: Less likely due to location and features.

2) Ependymoma:
   - Commonly intraventricular, especially in fourth ventricle in young adults.
   - Typically heterogeneously enhancing, often with solid and cystic components.
   - Frequently shows calcification, hemosiderin deposition (due to hemorrhage), and diffusion restriction.
   - Can extend through foramina of Luschka and Magendie, causing hydrocephalus.
   - This lesion is centered in lower fourth ventricle, with cystic and minimal solid areas, hemosiderin deposition, calcifications, and extension via ventricle foramina.
   - Verdict: Fits well.

3) DNET:
   - Typically cortical/subcortical lesion, often in temporal lobe, pediatric/young adult.
   - Usually cortical-based, not intraventricular.
   - Typically does not enhance or calcify significantly.
   - No typical intraventricular location or extension through foramina of Luschka.
   - Verdict: Unlikely.

4) RGNT:
   - Typically arises in fourth ventricle, especially lower, affecting young adults.
   - Usually solid or solid-cystic lesion with both neurocytic and glial components.
   - Typically has low cellularity with minimal enhancement, cystic components.
   - Can have hemosiderin deposition due to hemorrhage.
   - Calcification can be present but not common.
   - Extension via foramina of Luschka possible but less common.
   - No upstream hydrocephalus typically.
   - Verdict: Possible but less likely given strong hemosiderin and calcification.

   - Usually cystic with solid nodules, strong enhancement.
   - Rarely intraventricular.
   - Hemosiderin possible but no typical intraventricular extension via foramen of Luschka.
   - Verdict: Unlikely.

6) Medulloblastoma:
   - Highly cellular, solid tumors, usually in posterior fossa, midline vermis.
   - Hyperdense on CT, intense diffusion restriction.
   - Usually solid mass, rare cystic parts.
   - Enhancement is strong.
   - Usually does not have cystic fluid areas or hemosiderin blooming.
   - Rarely intraventricular.
   - Verdict: Less likely here.

2) RGNT: Possible due to location and cystic nature but less hemosiderin calcification, and no significant enhancement.
3) Pilocytic astrocytoma: Similar cystic nature but usually solid nodule enhancement strong, no intraventricular predominance, less hemosiderin/calci

Thus, the most likely diagnosis is Ependymoma.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with acute cholecystitis. During imaging work-up, an incidental iliopsoas mass was detected on CT. MRI was subsequently performed for further characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows a well-defined, complex iliopsoas mass containing fat and soft-tissue components, demonstrating heterogeneous enhancement and prominent internal vessels (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 MRI reveals an encapsulated lesion with high T1 signal intensity, slightly lower than subcutaneous fat, and heterogeneously high T2 signal intensity, with only partial fat suppression (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 2e, and 2f). It exhibits relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations, with no restricted diffusion.

- Patient: 43-year-old female with acute cholecystitis.

- MRI characteristics:  
  - Encapsulated lesion with high T1 signal intensity, slightly lower than subcutaneous fat.  
  - Heterogeneously high T2 signal intensity with only partial fat suppression.  
  - Relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations.  
  - No restricted diffusion on MRI.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Lipoma  
   - Pros: Well-defined encapsulated lesion containing fat; high T1 signal intensity (fat).  
   - Cons: Lipomas typically have homogeneous fat signal with complete fat suppression on T2/STIR images; they usually do not have prominent internal vessels, heterogeneous enhancement, or soft-tissue components. The presence of hypervascular soft tissue and internal vessels argues against simple lipoma.  

2) Liposarcoma  
   - Pros: Heterogeneous lesion with soft-tissue components and enhancement; internal vessels can be prominent; fat and soft tissue mix.  
   - Cons: Liposarcomas often show more irregular borders, infiltrative margins, and heterogeneity; more common in deeper soft tissues but can arise in iliopsoas; typically show restricted diffusion due to higher cellularity; not usually encapsulated. No restricted diffusion here argues against high-grade liposarcoma. Could be a well-differentiated liposarcoma? However, prominent internal vessels branching and partial fat suppression may not be typical.  

3) Angiomyolipoma  
   - Pros: Contains fat and soft tissue components; hypervascular with internal vessels; common histologically with prominent vessels, smooth muscle and fat. Usually found in kidneys but extrarenal angiomyolipomas exist but are rarer.  
   - Cons: Location (iliopsoas) is uncommon but possible; MRI signal characteristics partially match; prominent internal vessels and partial fat suppression support this. No restricted diffusion expected.  

4) Hibernoma  
   - Pros: Contains adipocytes with brown fat; can have high T1 signal intensity but slightly less than subcutaneous fat; partial fat suppression due to brown fat content; hypervascular.  
   - Cons: Typically shows very high T2 signal; often a well-circumscribed mass with hypervascularity; may have fat and soft tissue; internal vessels less prominent; location can be varied but commonly in soft tissue. No restricted diffusion.

   - Pros: Contains mature fat and hematopoietic tissue; common in adrenal gland but can be ectopic; well-defined encapsulated lesion; moderate to high T1 fat signal.  
   - Cons: Usually adrenal location; less commonly iliopsoas; internal vessels less prominent; enhancement less prominent; no hypervascular soft tissue typically.

   - Cons: Usually history of trauma; lesion often more ill-defined, may not be encapsulated; internal vessels prominent less likely; chronicity and lack of restricted diffusion could be compatible, but encapsulated lesion less typical; lesion typically more heterogeneous, less well defined.

- Lipoma: Inconsistent with internal vessels and soft tissue components.  
- Myelolipoma: Location and imaging findings less typical.  
- Fat necrosis: Less likely due to encapsulation, lack of trauma, and vascularity features.

Given the location (iliopsoas), the complex lesion composed of fat and soft tissue components with prominent branching vessels, hypervascular well-circumscribed encapsulated lesion with partial fat suppression, and absence of diffusion restriction, Angiomyolipoma is the best match.

</analysis>

Angiomyolipoma
</final><|return|>

 Imaging Findings: Initial contrast-enhanced pelvic CT (Figures 1a and 1b) demonstrated a 17 × 16 × 12 cm, predominantly solid, left retro-uterine mass with heterogeneous enhancement on the left ovary, anterior displacement of the uterus, and compression of both distal ureters, resulting in moderate bilateral hydroureteronephrosis.  Subsequent pelvic MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) showed mainly solid tissue with intermediate T2 signal (Figures 2a and 2b) and avid post-gadolinium enhancement (Figure 3), containing scattered cystic-necrotic foci. A) Key findings:
- Young woman (23 years old) with abdominal distension, pelvic pain, and urinary frequency.
- Large (17 × 16 × 12 cm) predominantly solid left retro-uterine mass.
- Heterogeneous enhancement, with scattered cystic-necrotic foci.

- Smooth tumour-uterus interface.
- Right ovary normal but displaced.
- Minimal ascites, no peritoneal implants.
- DWI with marked hyperintensity and low ADC values consistent with high cellularity.
- Pelvic MRI with intermediate T2 signal and avid post-gadolinium enhancement in solid components.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Dysgerminoma:
   - Pros: Occurs in young women; often large and solid ovarian mass; homogeneous or slightly heterogeneous enhancement; often bilateral or unilateral; high cellularity leading to restricted diffusion.
   - Cons: Usually presents with a homogeneous mass; may have lymphadenopathy or ascites; no mention of syncytiotrophoblastic cells (would raise hCG); no calcification or necrosis typical.

2) Yolk sac tumour (endodermal sinus tumour):
   - Pros: Occurs in young women; large mixed solid-cystic ovarian mass; heterogenous enhancement with cystic-necrotic areas; usually unilateral; high cellularity with diffusion restriction; can have very elevated AFP; often presents late with large tumour.
   - Cons: May have ascites or peritoneal spread; often very aggressive.

3) Juvenile granulosa cell tumour (JGCT):
   - Pros: Occurs in young women (including adolescents); solid ovarian mass; often large at presentation; can cause uterine displacement; high cellularity and diffusion restriction.
   - Cons: Typically unilateral; cystic change possible; usually hormonal effects causing precocious puberty but not always; generally better prognosis; no mention of hormonal signs.

4) Mixed malignant ovarian germ cell tumour:
   - Pros: Occurs in young women; can present with large ovarian mass with heterogeneous solid and cystic components; mixture of histologic patterns, often predominated by one component like yolk sac or dysgerminoma.
   - Cons: Diagnosis requires histopathology; on imaging, usually heterogeneous mass with varying signal intensities; imaging can be similar to yolk sac or dysgerminoma.

   - Pros: Rare sex cord-stromal tumour; typically unilateral; may be solid with cystic changes; can cause hormonal symptoms.
   - Cons: Less common in this age group; usually smaller; not typically as large and with such extensive cystic-necrotic change; no hormonal symptoms reported.

   - Pros: Occurs in young women; often large ovarian mass; heterogeneous with fat, calcifications, and solid components; variable signal intensity on MRI; high-grade immature teratomas show solid enhancing areas.
   - Cons: No macroscopic fat or calcification seen here, which argues against immature teratoma.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
- First, yolk sac tumour fits due to large size, heterogeneous solid-cystic nature with necrotic foci, young age, high cellularity, and typical radiologic features. 
- Second, mixed malignant germ cell tumour can appear similar but requires histology to confirm mixture; radiologically often heterogeneous.
- Third, dysgerminoma could be considered, but cystic-necrotic areas and the large size lean less towards dysgerminoma.
- Juvenile granulosa cell tumour is possible but less common and no hormonal signs.
- Sertoli–Leydig and immature teratoma less likely due to lack of fat/calcifications and typical features.

 Imaging Findings: A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound was performed and revealed a significant distension of the vagina, measuring 6.8 x 8.3 x 15.3 cm, with echogenic material occupying its lumen (approximately 450 ml). OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 3-year-old boy presented with recurrent atraumatic pain and refusal to walk for several weeks. The right proximal lower leg was swollen, and mobilisation was painful. Laboratory analysis at admission showed increased white blood cell count of 12.6 x 10³/mm³ and C-reactive protein of 20.2 mg/l.
 Imaging Findings: Radiography showed an irregularly moth-eaten mixed osteolytic-sclerotic lesion with a wide zone of transition in the proximal tibial diaphysis. The cortex was permeated with a transverse pathological fracture, interrupted lamellar periosteal reaction, and soft tissue swelling (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed extensive bone marrow replacement in the proximal tibia with adjacent soft tissue mass showing heterogeneous contrast enhancement, cortical permeation, bone destruction with focal cortical breakthrough (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Bone marrow changes were hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI) and heterogeneous hyperintense on STIR and fat-suppressed PD images.
 
 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography–computed tomography (FDG PET–CT) scan showed markedly increased uptake in the proximal right tibia, adjacent soft tissue, and right popliteal, inguinal, iliac and retroperitoneal lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Other distant metastases were absent.

FinalDiagnosis: B-cell acute lymphoblastic lymphoma (small round blue cell tumour)
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 3-year-old boy with atraumatic limb pain and refusal to walk, local swelling, painful mobilisation.  
- Lab: Elevated WBC and CRP (signs of inflammation/infection).  
- Radiograph: Mixed osteolytic-sclerotic lesion in the proximal tibial diaphysis, irregular moth-eaten appearance, wide zone of transition, cortical permeation, transverse pathological fracture.  
- MRI: Extensive bone marrow replacement, adjacent soft tissue mass with heterogeneous contrast enhancement, cortical breakthrough, hypo-T1 and heterogeneous hyper-STIR/fat-suppressed signal in marrow.  
- FDG PET-CT: Marked uptake in proximal tibia, adjacent soft tissue, and multiple regional lymph nodes (popliteal, inguinal, iliac, retroperitoneal); no distant metastases.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Osteomyelitis  
- Pros: Elevated WBC and CRP; periosteal reaction; soft tissue swelling; lesion in metaphyseal/diaphyseal region typical for acute infection.  
- Cons: Lesion described as mixed osteolytic-sclerotic with cortical breakthrough and soft tissue mass with heterogeneous enhancement, which is more typical for malignancy; pathological fracture and extensive marrow and soft tissue involvement uncommon in early osteomyelitis; FDG PET-CT positivity in multiple lymph nodes suggests malignant infiltration over reactive nodes.  

2) Osteosarcoma  
- Pros: Mixed osteolytic-sclerotic lesion at metaphysis/diaphysis in a child; cortical breakthrough and periosteal reaction; marrow involvement and soft tissue mass; pathological fracture.  
- Cons: Patient is younger than typical for osteosarcoma (usually >10 years); FDG-PET positive regional lymphadenopathy is uncommon in osteosarcoma; lesion is in proximal tibial diaphysis rather than metaphysis.  

3) B-cell acute lymphoblastic lymphoma (small round blue cell tumour)  
- Pros: Age appropriate (young child); lesion with bone marrow replacement and soft tissue mass; lymph node involvement with FDG uptake compatible with lymphoma; mixed lytic-sclerotic bone lesion possible in lymphoma; hypermetabolism in lesion and multiple regional lymph nodes typical.  
- Cons: Bone involvement less common as initial manifestation; pathological fracture possible but less typical; periosteal reaction and soft tissue enhancement less specific but can be present.  

4) Ewing sarcoma (or other small round blue cell tumour)  
- Pros: Common in children and adolescents; mixed lytic-sclerotic lesion with diffuse bone marrow involvement; cortical perforation; soft tissue mass with heterogeneous contrast enhancement; diaphyseal lesion typical; pathological fracture common; elevated FDG uptake.  
- Cons: Lymphadenopathy is uncommon in Ewing sarcoma; FDG PET-CT showed regional lymph node involvement which is less typical but possible in advanced disease.  

5) Langerhans cell histiocytosis  
- Pros: Can cause lytic bone lesions in children; soft tissue masses possible; regional lymph node involvement possible.  
- Cons: Usually purely osteolytic without sclerotic component; less aggressive periosteal reaction and soft tissue enhancement less typical; FDG uptake patterns differ; pathological fracture and periosteal reaction less common.  

6) Occult fracture in early stage  
- Pros: Clinical history of pain and refusal to walk; radiograph may miss early fracture.  
- Cons: Imaging findings (mixed lesion, soft tissue mass, marrow replacement, regional lymphadenopathy) not explained by simple fracture; elevated inflammatory markers and FDG uptake in nodes would be inconsistent.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Ewing sarcoma (strongly fits age, location, radiologic features, marrow and soft tissue involvement, pathological fracture, and FDG PET-CT findings with marrow and soft tissue uptake).  
2) B-cell acute lymphoblastic lymphoma (lymphadenopathy and FDG uptake support; bone and soft tissue involvement fits lymphomas but lymphadenopathy is unusual in Ewing but possible).  
3) Osteosarcoma (age less typical, regional lymphadenopathy less common for osteosarcoma).  
4) Osteomyelitis (raised labs and soft tissue findings but lesion and lymphadenopathy more suggest malignancy).  
5) Langerhans cell histiocytosis (less likely due to mixed lesion and periosteal reaction).  
6) Occult fracture (inconsistent with imaging and lymphadenopathy).  
<final>  
Ewing sarcoma (or other small round blue cell tumour)  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 66-year-old woman developed progressive neck pain, headache, and ascending lower-limb paraesthesia over 3 months that culminated in sudden bilateral leg weakness and ataxia. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) showed lymphocytic pleocytosis with hyperproteinorrachia, and a 2024 mediastinal node biopsy revealed non-necrotising granulomas. Her history includes uterine ablation (2006) and sinus surgery (1995).
 Imaging Findings: Sagittal T2-weighted MRI of the spine (Figure 1) revealed diffuse hyperintensity and swelling from C2 to T10 with a sharp transition at T11. Post-gadolinium T1 images (Figure 2) showed confluent dorsal subpial and central canal enhancement, producing the axial “trident sign”. There was patchy nodular enhancement involving the posterior and circumferential pial surfaces. The conus was spared.
 
 Brain MRI demonstrated two punctate cortical–subcortical enhancing foci without mass effect (Figures 3a and 3b). CT chest (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed bulky right paratracheal and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Compared with prior studies (December 2020), cord swelling and enhancement were mildly reduced after steroid therapy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old patient with a history of type 2 diabetes, arterial hypertension, and knee osteoarthritis treated with intra-articular steroids was admitted due to fever of unknown origin. The patient reported lumbar pain for the past week, with no associated symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast and contrast-enhanced CT of the skull, thorax and abdomen was performed to identify a possible infectious focus. The key findings included heterogeneous density in both psoas muscles due to the presence of gas bubbles, with no collections detected following contrast administration (Figures 1a and 1b). In addition to the gas identified in the psoas muscles, gas bubbles were observed within the L3 vertebral body.
 
 Unenhanced and contrast-enhanced lumbar spine MRI was performed. The axial T2-weighted image (Figure 2a) shows an enlarged left psoas muscle due to multiple round collections with increased signal intensity. A similar collection is observed in the ipsilateral paraspinal muscle and the right psoas muscle. In the axial post-contrast T1-weighted sequence (Figure 2b), the described lesions, which exhibit enhancement after contrast administration, are also seen (marked with blue stars). Sagittal T1-weighted images pre (Figure 2c) and post-contrast (Figure 2d) demonstrate heterogeneous very subtle enhancement of the L2, L3, L4, and L5 vertebral bodies, as well as the intervertebral discs. The sagittal views demonstrate lesions in the psoas and paraspinal muscles, with hyperintense signal on T2-weighted (Figure 2e) and STIR (Figure 2f) sequences at the level of the L2, L3, L4, and L5 vertebral bodies.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient with fever of unknown origin, lumbar pain for a week, with known diabetes and hypertension.  
- Imaging (CT and MRI) shows heterogeneous density in both psoas muscles with gas bubbles, but no collections on CT with contrast.  
- MRI shows multiple round collections in bilateral psoas muscles and a ipsilateral paraspinal muscle, which enhance with contrast.  
- Multiple vertebral bodies (L2-L5) and intervertebral discs exhibit very subtle heterogeneous enhancement.  
- T2/STIR sequences show hyperintense lesions in vertebral bodies (L2-L5) and paraspinal muscles.  
- Presence of gas bubbles in the vertebral bodies (L3) and psoas muscles.  
- Clinical picture: fever, lumbar pain, diabetic patient, treated previously with intra-articular steroids (immunosuppression risk).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Tuberculous spondylodiscitis:  
  - Pros: Involvement of multiple vertebral bodies and intervertebral discs typical for TB. Fever and back pain fit clinical picture.  
  - Cons: Gas formation within vertebral bodies and psoas muscles is rare in TB; presence of gas more typical of pyogenic infection or anaerobic infection.  

- Pyogenic spondylodiscitis:  
  - Pros: Gas formation within vertebral bodies and psoas muscles suggests pyogenic (especially anaerobic) infection. Multiple round collections with enhancement and gas bubbles present. Patient has risk factors (diabetes, steroids). Fever supports infection.  
  - Cons: None significant; findings and clinical picture align well.  

- Charcot joint:  
  - Pros: Charcot joint is related to neuropathic osteoarthropathy, which can involve vertebrae.  
  - Cons: Charcot joint usually occurs in the neuropathic joint (e.g., diabetic foot), not typically in lumbar spine. Imaging does not show destructive arthropathy; also presence of gas and infection signs not typical of Charcot joint.  

- Modic type 1 endplate changes:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with long-standing recurrent epistaxis and progressive fatigue. Physical examination revealed mucocutaneous telangiectasias on the nasal mucosa and tongue, as well as mild hepatomegaly. Laboratory tests showed iron-deficiency anaemia and mildly altered liver function tests, with cholestasis but no hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Colour-Doppler abdominal ultrasound revealed multiple dilated arterial vessels in both liver lobes, corresponding to branches of the hepatic artery, with one vessel forming a direct communication with the main portal vein (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Subsequent contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated diffuse dilatation of intrahepatic arterial branches and early filling of the dilated hepatic veins during arterial phase, suggestive of arteriovenous shunting, and multiple small hypervascular nodules consistent with hepatic telangiectasias. Mild intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation was also noted, likely due to extrinsic compression by dilated vessels (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Further evaluation with MRI confirmed the presence of intrahepatic arteriovenous malformations. Post-contrast T1-weighted Dixon sequences and MR angiography provided superior characterisation of the vascular anomalies, ruling out portal vein thrombosis and parenchymal changes typical of chronic liver disease (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old patient undergoes a scheduled MRI study due to a one-year history of migraine-like headaches, accompanied by visual disturbances, paraesthesias in the right upper limb, and instability before and during the pain.
 Imaging Findings: The MRI reveals a lesion located in the right temporal horn of the lateral ventricle, with a prominent cystic component and a solid nodule that appears hypointense on T1-weighted images (Figures 1a and 1b) and hyperintense on T2-weighted images (Figure 2). This solid, heterogeneous nodule shows intense enhancement following gadolinium administration (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e) and areas of microcystic architecture, without evidence of subependymal spread. Extensive T2-FLAIR hyperintensity is observed throughout the surrounding white matter, consistent with vasogenic oedema. The mass exerts a moderate compressive effect on the ventricular system, causing a slight midline shift (Figure 2).
 
 Notably, the lesion displays prominent draining veins, reflecting its hypervascular nature (Figures 3a and 3b). No diffusion restriction is identified on DWI sequences (Figure 4). Perfusion imaging confirms a highly vascularised tumour (Figure 5a, 5b, 5c).
 
 Magnetic resonance spectroscopy demonstrates a prominent lipid and lactate peak, likely indicative of necrotic components (Figure 6).

   - Pros: Highly vascularized tumor, hypervascular with prominent draining veins, intense enhancement, cystic-nodular appearance, common in posterior fossa but can be supratentorial.

   - Pros: Highly vascular, can have cystic areas.  
   - Cons: Usually posterior fossa location in children, rare in adults, and intraventricular location here not typical.

   - Pros: Enhances intensely, hypervascular tumor, can have cystic change but typically extraventricular and adjacent to dura.

2) Astrocytoma: Possible but lacks pathognomonic features like calcification or typical low-grade spectrum despite cystic component.  
3) Menigioma: Unlikely due to atypical intraventricular location without dural attachment.  
4) Medulloblastoma: Unlikely in adult supratentorial location.  
5) Metastasis: Less likely due to solitary lesion and no clinical oncologic history.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old lady presented with a gradually progressive, painless, cystic swelling in the left infra-auricular region for the last 12 months. No other swellings were noted in the neck. There was no prior history of ear discharge or parotitis. Clinical examination showed no evidence of skin changes or facial nerve palsy.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography reveals a cystic lesion with internal echoes within the substance of the left parotid gland with a beak-like projection on its deeper aspect (Figure 1).
 
 CT scan demonstrates a well-defined, homogeneous, smoothly marginated, non-enhancing lesion (density of 35 HU before and 36 HU after contrast administration) located in the left parotid gland, causing thinning and outward displacement of the residual gland parenchyma (Figure 2).
 
 MRI reveals a T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and STIR hyperintense, intra-parotid lesion occupying superficial and deep lobes of the parotid with a small blind-ending tract projecting anteromedially into the parapharyngeal space. No fistulous tracts to the external auditory canal were noted. There was no evidence of fat stranding or enlarged lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

Clinical History: A 47-year-old male presented with complaints of headaches for 6 months. No history of loss of consciousness, projectile vomiting or seizures. No history of trauma. The patient is a known case of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension on treatment for 10 years. On examination, macrognathia and frontal bossing were noted.
 Imaging Findings: The skull radiograph (Figure 1a) shows an enlarged sella turcica, frontal sinus hyperpneumatisation, and calvarial hyperostosis. Prognathism and widened mandibular angle are seen (Figure 1b). Hand and feet radiographs (Figures 2a and 2b) show spade-like distal phalanges with prominent ungual tufts. The chest radiograph (Figure 3) reveals widened glenohumeral joints and thickened scapular borders.
 
 CT brain shows calvarial hyperostosis (Figure 4a) and a hyperdense sellar mass (Figure 4b).
 
 MRI reveals a lobulated sellar-suprasellar lesion—T1 isointense (Figure 7), T2 and FLAIR hyperintense with “snowman” appearance—causing sella widening and displacing adjacent vessels (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), with heterogeneous enhancement (Figures 9b and 9c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 61-year-old male, with a history of a pontine stroke 10 weeks before, presented with new neurological symptoms of staccato speech and an uncoordinated gait. Neurological examination revealed nystagmus, mild dysarthria, truncal and limb ataxia, and an unsteady gait. An MRI was performed to rule out a new ischemic stroke.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI showed a cavitated lesion in the left paramedian pons, hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI) (Figure 1a), hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 1b), without diffusion restriction (Figure 1c), consistent with the previous pontine stroke. Additionally, symmetrical ovoid lesions in the middle cerebellar peduncles (MCPs), hypointense on T1WI (Figure 1d) and hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 1e), and mild T2WI hyperintensity in the left anterior pyramid (Figure 1g), without diffusion restriction (Figures 1f and 1h), were also noted.
PostDescription:

Additionally, symmetrical ovoid lesions in the middle cerebellar peduncles (MCPs), hypointense on T1WI and hyperintense on T2WI, and mild T2WI hyperintensity in the left anterior pyramid, without diffusion restriction, were also noted.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles ischemic stroke, Wallerian degeneration of pontocerebellar tracts secondary to unilateral pontine infarction, Multiple system atrophy, Fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome, Wilson’s disease
FinalDiagnosis: Wallerian degeneration of pontocerebellar tracts secondary to unilateral pontine infarction

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 61-year-old male with recent pontine stroke (10 weeks prior) now presents with new neurological symptoms (staccato speech, uncoordinated gait, nystagmus, mild dysarthria, truncal and limb ataxia).  
- MRI shows a cavitated lesion in the left paramedian pons (hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2, no diffusion restriction) consistent with chronic stroke changes.  
- Symmetrical ovoid lesions in both middle cerebellar peduncles (MCPs), hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2, also without diffusion restriction.  
- Mild T2 hyperintensity in the left anterior pyramid (part of the corticospinal tract), no diffusion restriction.  
- Diffusion is normal, indicating chronic or subacute changes rather than acute infarction.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles ischemic stroke  
- Pro: Lesions in MCPs are present.  
- Con: Bilateral acute ischemic stroke would show diffusion restriction; lesions have no diffusion restriction, and history of only a single pontine stroke, not bilateral MCP infarcts. Also, no clinical history of acute cerebellar stroke. Therefore, less likely.  

2) Wallerian degeneration of pontocerebellar tracts secondary to unilateral pontine infarction  
- Pro: Patient had a unilateral pontine infarct (left paramedian pons), and now shows symmetrical MCP lesions and pyramidal tract changes, which are downstream tracts from the pons. MRI shows T2 hyperintensity without diffusion restriction in MCPs and anterior pyramid, consistent with chronic Wallerian degeneration.  
- Con: None significant. The time course and imaging fits with Wallerian degeneration rather than acute injury.  

3) Multiple system atrophy  
- Pro: MCPs can show hyperintensity in MSA, often with cerebellar signs.  
- Con: The patient has a recent stroke, symmetrical MCP lesions are less typical and usually show T2 hyperintensity with atrophy, but here lesions are symmetric and there is a recent clear stroke. Also, no mention of autonomic dysfunction or typical MSA features.  

4) Fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome  
- Pro: Ataxia and cerebellar signs could relate.  
- Con: Typically a degenerative disorder in older males, but no mention of tremor or relevant family history. Also, no characteristic MRI findings. Not consistent with acute changes or stroke history.  

5) Wilson’s disease  
- Pro: Neurologic symptoms and basal ganglia involvement possible.  
- Con: No mention of hepatic or other systemic signs; Wilson's disease does not cause symmetric MCP lesions related to a focal stroke. No mention of characteristic MRI signs.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Wallerian degeneration of pontocerebellar tracts secondary to unilateral pontine infarction  
2) Bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles ischemic stroke  
3) Multiple system atrophy  
4) Fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome  
5) Wilson’s disease

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old man presented to the Emergency Department with a 4-day history of right iliac fossa pain. He denied fever, nausea, or vomiting. Physical examination revealed rebound tenderness. Laboratory tests showed elevated C-reactive protein (19.6 mg/L) with a normal white-cell count. Two months earlier, he had undergone a laparoscopic appendectomy.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (Figure 1) revealed a blind-ending, aperistaltic, non-compressible tubular structure (luminal diameter ≈ 10 mm) arising from the caecum at the appendectomy bed. The wall was thickened with surrounding echogenic fat. A minimal amount of free fluid was also observed.
 
 In contrast-enhanced computer tomography (CT), multiplanar reformats demonstrated an enhancing tubular remnant arising from the caecum, capped by two metallic clips at its distal end (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3). The stump showed concentric mural thickening, pericaecal fat stranding, and a trace of free fluid. No evidence of abscess or pneumoperitoneum was identified.

- Ultrasound: blind-ending, aperistaltic, non-compressible tubular structure (≈10 mm lumen) arising from caecum at previous appendectomy site, wall thickened, surrounding echogenic fat, minimal free fluid.  
- CT: enhancing tubular remnant with two metallic clips at distal end, concentric mural thickening, pericaecal fat stranding, trace free fluid, no abscess or pneumoperitoneum.

- Pros: RLQ pain, fat stranding possible.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 8-year-old male patient presented with chronic left lumbar discomfort, claudication, anaemia (Hb = 8 g/dL), and hyperleukocytosis (12000/mm3). He has been receiving therapy for anaemia for two years.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (US) shows multiple thick-walled collections with echogenic fluid content occupying the left renal fossa and loss of renal parenchyma’s architecture with no corticomedullary differentiation. The thin remaining peripheric parenchymal bundle shows no limits with the thick-walled collections. There is a central pyelic stone measuring 20 mm with a posterior acoustic shadow and a hyperechoic aspect of the perirenal fat.
 
 CT (computed tomography) shows left renal enlargement with destruction of parenchymal architecture and dilated calyces consistent with the “bear paw sign” (Figure 1a), coexisting blurring of perirenal fat, psoas muscle enlargement and abscess formation (Figure 1b), as well as nephroureterolithiasis (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). The left kidney was nonfunctional at the excretory phase scan (Figures 1e and 1f).
 
 On renal scintigraphy (Figure 2), DTPA injection demonstrates reduced relative renal function on the left (17.7% left and 82.3% right), with pyelocaliceal stasis at the excretory phase. Reduced cortical uptake, particularly in the left upper pole, was seen both at DMSA and DTPA scans.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Clinical: 8-year-old male with chronic left lumbar pain, claudication, anemia, and hyperleukocytosis; ongoing anemia therapy.  
- Ultrasound: Multiple thick-walled collections with echogenic fluid, loss of corticomedullary differentiation, thin peripheral parenchyma abutting collections, large central 20 mm pelvi-ureteric stone, hyperechoic perirenal fat.  
- CT: Left renal enlargement with destruction of parenchymal architecture, dilated calyces forming the "bear paw sign," blurred perirenal fat, psoas muscle enlargement with abscesses, nephroureterolithiasis, nonfunctional left kidney on excretory phase.  
- Scintigraphy: Reduced relative function on left (17.7%), pyelocaliceal stasis, reduced cortical uptake particularly in upper pole.  
- Other: anemia, history of anemia for 2 years, hyperleukocytosis (indicative of infection/inflammation).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Renal fossa tumour:  
- Pros: Renal enlargement, possible perirenal involvement.  
- Cons: Collections with thick walls and echogenic fluid indicate infection/inflammation rather than mass. Bear paw sign, stone, infection parameters favor inflammatory/infectious process not neoplasm. No mention of solid enhancing mass.  

2) Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (diffuse form):  
- Pros: Classic findings include renal enlargement with parenchymal destruction, multiple thick-walled collections (abscesses), hyperechoic/blurred perirenal fat, nephroureterolithiasis (stones), nonfunctionality, bear paw sign on CT, psoas involvement with abscess, chronic infection signs (anemia, leukocytosis).  
- Cons: Age is somewhat less typical (more common in adults), but can occur in children.  

3) Pyonephrosis:  
- Pros: Thick-walled dilated calyces with pus/fluid content, renal enlargement, nonfunctionality.  
- Cons: Typically a localized intrarenal abscess, often unilateral and may not show diffuse parenchymal destruction or bear paw sign. Usually no nephrolithiasis with large stone. Here, the findings are more extensive than simple pyonephrosis.  

4) Hydronephrosis:  
- Pros: Dilated calyces, renal enlargement.  
- Cons: No collection/fluid with echogenicity or thick walls, no perirenal fat inflammation, no abscess, no infection signs, no stone; also renal parenchyma destruction and nonfunctionality not explained solely by obstruction.  

5) Urogenital tuberculosis:  
- Pros: Renal parenchymal destruction may occur, chronic infection.  
- Cons: Typically shows calcifications, less commonly the bear paw sign; perirenal abscesses and large stones less common; anemia and leukocytosis could be seen, but US and CT more suggestive of XGP rather than TB.  

C) Ranking:  
1. Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (diffuse form) - best fits all imaging and clinical features (bear paw sign, stone, inflammatory collections, parenchymal destruction, infection signs).  
2. Pyonephrosis - less extensive inflammation, no stone, less likely to cause paraparenchymal thick-walled collections and psoas involvement.  
3. Urogenital TB - possible but less typical imaging and stone not classic.  
4. Renal fossa tumour - infection/inflammation signs rule out neoplasm.  
5. Hydronephrosis - does not explain collections, inflammation, stones, abscesses.  
Thus, the best-matching diagnosis is Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (diffuse form).  
<final>  
Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (diffuse form)  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old male presented to the emergency department following the onset of acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain, later diagnosed as inflamed, nonperforated appendicitis. On CT abdomen and pelvis (CTAP), an incidental adrenal mass was found on the left kidney. Work-up for the adrenal mass was conducted.
 Imaging Findings: In 2018, a CTAP noted a 17 mm left adrenal nodule without normal washout characteristics (Figure 1). The work-up at the time revealed no functional secretion, and the patient was also asymptomatic.
 
 A new CTAP revealed that the previous nodule had grown to a size of 6.4 x 6 x 6.7 cm (Figure 2). A chest CT with contrast was unremarkable.
 
 An MRI was ordered for further characterisation of the mass. T1 MRI visualised a heterogeneous enhancing left adrenal mass that was suspicious of malignancy (Figure 3). The size of the lesion was consistent compared to the CTAP.

- Follow-up: Growth to 6.4 x 6 x 6.7 cm, significant increase in size over approximately 5 years.  
- Functional: No functional secretion noted during initial work-up.  
- Imaging: CT and MRI show heterogeneous enhancing mass with suspicious features for malignancy.  
- Chest CT negative for other lesions (no metastases found).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Adrenal adenocarcinoma  
   - Pros: Size increase (over 5.8 cm now), heterogeneous enhancement, lack of typical adenoma washout, suspicious for malignancy, no functional secretion.  
   - Cons: None significant.  

2) Adrenal adenoma  
   - Pros: Common adrenal mass in this size range.  
   - Cons: Lack of normal washout on CT, significant growth over time, heterogeneous enhancement suspicious for malignancy, no functional changes. Adenomas usually show stable size or slow growth and typical washout.  

3) Pheochromocytoma  
   - Pros: Can present as heterogeneous enhancing adrenal mass; can cause growth.  
   - Cons: No functional secretion (if biochemical work-up normal), no symptoms suggestive of catecholamine excess, no mention of other related clinical signs.  

4) Adrenal myelolipoma  
   - Pros: Could be a large mass.  
   - Cons: Typically shows macroscopic fat on CT/MRI; no mention of fatty components; heterogeneous enhancement not typical; growth to large size without typical benign features.  

5) Adrenal metastatic lesion  
   - Pros: Heterogeneous enhancement; growth over time; suspicious for malignancy.  
   - Cons: No known primary malignancy; chest CT negative; no other metastatic lesions seen; pheochromocytoma or primary adrenal cortical carcinoma more likely with no other known primary.  

C) Ranking and decision:  
- #1 Adrenal adenocarcinoma (primary adrenal cortical carcinoma): Fits growth, enhancement, and suspicion for malignancy.  
- #2 Pheochromocytoma: Possible but less likely given no functional findings.  
- #3 Adrenal metastatic lesion: Least likely due to no identified primary and negative chest imaging.  
- #4 Adrenal myelolipoma: Unlikely due to lack of fat.  
- #5 Adrenal adenoma: Contradicted by imaging and growth characteristics.  

Conclusion: Adrenal adenocarcinoma is the most consistent diagnosis based on size, growth, lack of washout, and suspicious imaging features.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Adrenal adenocarcinoma  
</final>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old healthy male presents with three months of left shoulder pain during activities, followed by a gradual restriction in arm abduction. He denies any trauma, fever, or involvement of other joints. Physical examination reveals swelling in the deltoid region and an inability to raise the left arm to 90°.
 Imaging Findings: Left shoulder x-ray (Figure 1) revealed a mixed sclerotic-lytic lesion involving the proximal diaphysis and metaphysis of the humerus, characterised by poorly defined margins, a wide transition zone, permeative osteolysis, cortical thinning, and a spiculated periosteal reaction with Codman’s triangle, consistent with a Bone-RADS 4 lesion.
 
 MRI demonstrated a large lobulated mass centred in the proximal humerus, hypointense on T1-weighted images (Figure 2a), hyperintense on T2 (Figure 2b) and STIR (Figures 2c and 2d) sequences, with associated extraosseous soft tissue extension and glenohumeral joint effusion. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 83-year-old gentleman presented to the Urosurgery department with a history of lower urinary tract symptoms in the form of increased frequency and incomplete evacuation of urine. No history of haematuria, burning micturition or fever. On digital rectal examination, the prostate gland was felt to be enlarged with nodularity within felt. On biochemical evaluation, prostate specific antigen (PSA) was marginally raised.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound examination, an enlarged prostate with a volume of 36 cc. There were features of chronic bladder outlet obstruction seen in the form of thickening of the bladder wall (maximum thickness of 8 mm) and a few trabeculations in the bladder wall.
 
 In view of the elevated PSA levels, an MRI prostate was done, which revealed a well-defined, solid, irregularly shaped lesion measuring 3.5 x 5.4 x 5.7 cm (AP x TR x CC) arising from the prostatic urethra and causing its widening and extending into the bladder. This lesion was homogeneously hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted and T2-weighted fat-saturated images (Figures 1 and 2). It was showing homogeneous restriction of diffusion on DWI (Figures 3a and 3b) and was heterogeneously enhancing on post-contrast images. Also, multiple well-defined T2 heterogeneous nodules were seen in the transitional zone (Figure 4). No T2 hypointense or diffusion-restricting lesion was seen in the prostate.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 74-year-old female undergoing immunosuppressive therapy was referred to the orthopaedic department for evaluation of a progressively enlarging mass on the medial side of the left knee, evolving over six months. Initially asymptomatic, the lesion became painful. Notably, she had undergone an articular puncture one year earlier.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with a large mass on the medial aspect of the left knee (Figures 1a and 1b), which was also evident on plain radiography (Figure 2).
 
 Ultrasonography demonstrated a sizable extra-articular heterogeneous lesion with no internal vascularisation, accompanied by a moderate joint effusion and synovial hypertrophy (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed a large extra-articular collection, hyperintense on proton density-weighted fat-suppressed sequences (Figures 4a and 4b) and hypointense on T1-weighted sequences (Figure 4c), with extensive peripheral enhancement following gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b) and no enhancement of solid components. Additionally, MRI revealed extensive synovitis and bone marrow oedema, along with juxta-articular and intra-articular erosions exhibiting peripheral contrast enhancement. No blooming artefacts were identified (Figure 5c).
PostDescription:

Ultrasonography demonstrated a sizable extra-articular heterogeneous lesion with no internal vascularisation, accompanied by a moderate joint effusion and synovial hypertrophy. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed a large extra-articular collection, hyperintense on proton density-weighted fat-suppressed sequences and hypointense on T1-weighted sequences, with extensive peripheral enhancement following gadolinium administration and no enhancement of solid components. Additionally, MRI revealed extensive synovitis and bone marrow oedema, along with juxta-articular and intra-articular erosions exhibiting peripheral contrast enhancement. No blooming artefacts were identified.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Infection by Mycobacterium avium, Infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Pyogenic arthritis, Inflammatory arthritis

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 74-year-old female on immunosuppressive therapy  
- Large, progressively enlarging extra-articular mass on medial side of left knee, with pain development over six months  
- History of articular puncture one year prior  
- Imaging: Plain radiography shows large mass; ultrasound shows heterogeneous extra-articular lesion with moderate joint effusion and synovial hypertrophy, no internal vascularization; MRI shows large extra-articular collection hyperintense on PD FS (fat-suppressed proton density), hypointense on T1, with extensive peripheral enhancement after gadolinium (rim enhancement) and no solid component enhancement.  
- Additional findings: extensive synovitis, bone marrow oedema, juxta-articular and intra-articular erosions with peripheral contrast enhancement; no blooming artefacts (thus less likely to be hemorrhagic or have calcified nidus).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Infection by Mycobacterium avium  
   - Pros: Immunosuppressed host, chronic presentation, extra-articular collection with rim enhancement and no solid enhancement can be consistent with granulomatous or mycobacterial infection.  
   - Cons: Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infections are less likely to cause bone erosions and synovitis with marrow edema; more commonly cause tenosynovitis or soft tissue mass in immunocompromised patients but less commonly affect the bone or cause erosions on MRI. Also, MAC often presents slower, but joint involvement with erosions is less common.  

2) Infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis  
   - Pros: Classic for extra-articular abscess with rim enhancement, synovitis, bone marrow edema, and erosions are hallmark features of osteoarticular tuberculosis. The immunosuppressed status and prior joint puncture increase risk. The peripheral enhancement pattern (caseating granuloma) is characteristic. The mass being extra-articular but with adjacent bone marrow edema and intra-articular erosions strongly support TB infection.  
   - Cons: No mention of calcifications or "rice bodies", but imaging features and clinical scenario strongly suggest TB.  

3) Pyogenic arthritis  
   - Pros: Joint puncture history with subsequent joint effusion and erosion could be consistent with bacterial infectious arthritis.  
   - Cons: The lesion is extra-articular with a large collection showing rim enhancement but no internal vascularity and no solid enhancement; pyogenic infections often show more solid component enhancement and have more acute inflammatory changes; bone erosions may be present but often more aggressive and associated with synovitis but the chronicity and immunosuppression context favour TB over acute pyogenic infection.  

4) Inflammatory arthritis  
   - Pros: Erosions and synovitis can be seen in inflammatory arthropathies like RA.  
   - Cons: Large extra-articular heterogeneous collection with peripheral rim enhancement is not typical for inflammatory arthritis; these usually cause pannus formation and synovial thickening without large cystic collections. Also, history of immunosuppression and puncture with abscess formation less favours just inflammatory arthritis.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis – best fits large extra-articular collection with rim enhancement, synovitis, bone marrow edema, and erosions; immunosuppressed host and prior puncture increase susceptibility.  
2) Infection by Mycobacterium avium – less likely due to bone involvement and typical radiological pattern.  
3) Pyogenic arthritis – possible but less likely given rim enhancement without solid components and chronic course.  
4) Inflammatory arthritis – least likely due to presence of large abscess-like collection and clinical context.  
</analysis><final> Infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis </final>
<|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male presented with headache, giddiness, disorientation, and right lower limb weakness. He had a recent history of a fall.
 Imaging Findings: Initial MRI revealed a crescent-shaped extra-axial lesion along the right frontoparietotemporal convexity, hyperintense on T1 and FLAIR-weighted sequences (Figures 1a and 1b), with mild mass effect and midline shift, suggesting a subacute subdural haematoma. A similar extra-axial collection was seen on the left side. No diffusion restriction was observed on DWI (Figure 1c). A well-defined enhancing lesion was also seen in the right lateral ventricle (persistent in serial imaging).
 
 The patient was initially managed conservatively and scheduled for follow-up. Two months later, a non-contrast CT was performed due to recurrence of symptoms, including disorientation and right lower limb weakness. CT revealed a well-defined subdural extraxial crescent-shaped collection along the cerebral convexity in the left frontoparietal region. It was causing a mass effect on the underlying cerebral parenchyma. Midline shift was noted towards the right, with left uncal herniation and dilatation of the contralateral lateral ventricle. A heterogeneous predominantly iso to hypodense lesion was seen along the left frontoparietal convexity, causing mass effect on the underlying brain parenchyma and left lateral ventricle (Figure 2a). Mild periosteal reaction along the inner table of the left parietal bone was observed (Figure 2b).
 
 No update MRI with gadolinium contrast was performed between this CT and surgery. This may have limited diagnostic clarity and contributed to incomplete preoperative planning.
 
 Intraoperatively, no haematoma was identified. Instead, thickened dura, granular periosteum and hyperostosis of underlying bone were noted, raising concern for an underlying neoplastic process (Figure 3).
 
 Postoperative contrast-enhanced MRI revealed a well-defined extra-axial lesion in the left frontoparietotemporal region. It was hyperintense on T2/FLAIR, isointense on T1, with mild diffusion restriction and heterogeneous peripheral enhancement (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c and 4d). There was also a significant mass effect on the adjacent brain parenchyma and ventricles. T1 hyperintense and T2 hypointense areas likely represented postoperative changes. A thin underlying T2 hyperintense, T1 hypointense, and non-enhancing collection were also noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old male presented with worsening lower back pain for 6 months. On physical examination there was no deformity or neurological deficits noted. There was no history of fever, night sweats or weight loss.
 Imaging Findings: An anteroposterior (AP) and lateral radiograph (Figures 1a and 1b) of the lumbar spine was done initially. It showed reduced intervertebral disc space at the L2–L3 level with adjacent end plate erosions and sclerosis, and soft tissue opacity in the paravertebral region at the L2–L3 lumbar vertebral level. Loss of the psoas muscle outline was also noted.
 
 On MRI, partial destruction of the posteroinferior aspect of L1 and posterosuperior aspect of L2 is seen with involvement of the intervening IV disc (Figures 2a and 2b). Altered signal intensity is seen in the vertebral bodies of L4 and L5, which appear hypointense on T1-weighted (T1WI) and hyperintense on T2-weighted (T2WI). There is an associated extensive, multiloculated prevertebral and bilateral paravertebral collection seen (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). The soft tissue components appear hypointense on T1WI and hyperintense on T2W images. These show peripheral restriction of diffusion (Figures 7a and 7b). On post-contrast imaging (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), there is enhancement of the soft tissue components with central necrotic areas and the involved parts of L1 and L2 vertebral bodies.
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 20-year-old male with worsening lower back pain for 6 months, no fever/night sweats/weight loss.  
- Radiograph shows reduced disc space at L2-L3, adjacent end plate erosions and sclerosis, and soft tissue opacity in the paravertebral region at L2-L3.  
- Loss of psoas muscle outline on imaging.  
- MRI shows partial destruction of the posteroinferior aspect of L1 and posterosuperior aspect of L2 with involvement of the intervening intervertebral disc (IV disc).  
- Vertebral bodies L4 and L5 show altered signal: hypointense on T1WI and hyperintense on T2WI (implies marrow edema or infiltration).  
- Extensive multiloculated prevertebral and bilateral paravertebral collection with soft tissue components that are T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and show peripheral diffusion restriction.  
- Post-contrast shows enhancement of soft tissue with central necrosis, and enhancement of involved vertebral parts.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Pott’s spine with psoas abscess  
Pros: Classic for tubercular (Pott's) spine; disc space narrowing at L2-L3 with adjacent end plate erosion and sclerosis is classic; involvement of multiple vertebrae (L1, L2, L4, L5), soft tissue collections with enhancement and central necrosis typical for tubercular abscesses; loss of psoas muscle outline suggests psoas abscess, a known complication of Pott's disease.  
Cons: No fever/weight loss or constitutional symptoms reported; however, TB can be indolent.  

2. Pyogenic spondylodiscitis  
Pros: Disc space involvement and vertebral body destruction can be seen in pyogenic infection; presence of paravertebral abscess.  
Cons: End plate erosion with sclerosis is less typical for acute pyogenic infections, which usually cause less sclerosis and more rapid destruction; typically more acute presentation with constitutional symptoms; multiloculated abscess and central necrosis more typical for TB. The chronicity (6 months) and lack of constitutional symptoms favor TB over pyogenic etiology.  

3. Metastasis  
Pros: Vertebral lesions with marrow signal change can be due to metastasis.  
Cons: Age (20-year-old) is far less typical; metastasis usually does not cause disc space involvement or paravertebral abscess formation; soft tissue masses with abscess formation and psoas involvement are not typical for metastasis.  

C) Ranking:  
1. Pott’s spine with psoas abscess - pathognomonic features include disc space narrowing with adjacent end plate erosion and sclerosis, multiloculated paravertebral abscess with psoas involvement, chronic symptoms.  
2. Pyogenic spondylodiscitis - plausible infectious cause, but less likely due to chronicity and presence of sclerosis/end plate changes.  
3. Metastasis - unlikely given age and abscess features.  

Thus, the most consistent diagnosis with imaging and clinical findings is Pott’s spine with psoas abscess.  
</analysis>

</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old female presented to the emergency department with acute lower abdominal pain, accompanied by chronic burning sensations and stabbing pain in the right lower leg. Based on the initial clinical presentation, diverticulitis was suspected, and a routine abdominal CT scan was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: CT imaging demonstrated a lipomatous, highly vascularised mass located posterior to the proximal femoral diaphysis of the right hip, with extension into the ischiofemoral space (Figures 1a and 1b). The lesion was predominantly composed of fatty tissue and exhibited intense arterial enhancement, consistent with a hypervascular process.
 
 Subsequent MRI offered further characterisation, identifying a complex intramuscular mass within the right quadratus femoris muscle, composed of both lipomatous (hyperintense on T1-weighted images) and nodular components (Figure 2). The nodular areas appeared hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2 fat-suppressed sequences (Figure 3). These regions demonstrated moderate, heterogeneous contrast enhancement, indicating variable internal vascularity (Figure 4). There were no significant signal voids on T2* sequences, effectively excluding haemorrhage or calcification. The lesion did not invade surrounding structures. However, the adjacent sciatic nerve exhibited focal thickening, which was suggestive of possible irritation due to mass effect or local inflammation (Figure 5).

   - Cons: No invasion of adjacent structures; lack of T2* susceptibility is atypical; generally sarcomas may have infiltrative borders, heterogeneity including hemorrhage or necrosis (not seen), and more aggressive features.

2) Soft tissue sarcoma: less likely due to lack of aggressive features and invasion.  
3) Atypical lipoma: less vascular and lacks nodular components as described.  
4) Myositis ossificans: lacks calcification or ossification.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 15-year-old female patient with right lower limb shortening since childhood was evaluated in consultation for limb lengthening surgery. Her personal history was negative for trauma. On physical examination, she had a clinically measured leg-length discrepancy of 4 cm.
 Imaging Findings: Full-length standing anteroposterior view radiograph (Figure 1a) demonstrated right lower limb shortening secondary to femoral hypoplasia, with normal ossification and no proximal deformity, consistent with Paley type 1A congenital femoral deficiency (CFD). Mild proximal fibular shortening was also noted, compatible with fibular hemimelia (Achterman–Kalamchi type IA).
 
 On the magnified AP radiograph of the right knee (Figure 1b), the femoral intercondylar notch appeared shallow, and the tibial spines were markedly hypoplastic.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur (anterior drawer sign) and uncovering of the lateral meniscus posterior horn (Figure 2b), findings suggestive of anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) insufficiency. On mid-sagittal fat-suppressed proton-density (PD) and oblique sagittal PD-weighted images (Figures 2c and 2d), the ACL was not visualised. The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) was present and vertically oriented (positive PCL line sign). No bone marrow oedema was seen, and the menisci were unremarkable.

CON - Young patient with congenital limb shortening and radiographic signs of tibial spine and intercondylar notch hypoplasia; MRI shows complete absence of ACL, no bone marrow edema or acute injury signs; no trauma history.

CON - none significant.

C) Ranking:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 57-year-old diabetic man, with 25 years of bilateral lower limb weakness, presented with a 1.5-month history of a mildly painful, firm, non-tender swelling over the left shoulder. Limited shoulder movement, reduced sensation, and a scar noted (Figures 1a and 1b). No trauma or aspiration records.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the left shoulder (Figure 2) showed joint destruction, including humeral head and glenoid resorption, widened joint space, periarticular calcifications, and soft tissue inflammation.
 
 MRI of the left shoulder (Figures 4, 5, 6, 7a, and 7b) revealed extensive bony damage, complete tears in the rotator cuff tendons and complete destruction of the glenoid labrum, glenohumeral ligaments, and biceps-labral anchor. A large, thick-walled fluid collection with peripheral enhancement extended into the intermuscular and intramuscular planes of the rotator cuff muscles.
 
 MRI of the spine (Figures 3a and 3b) showed a syrinx extending from the cervico-medullary junction to the thoracic vertebra at T11, consistent with syringomyelia, and a wedge collapse of the vertebral body of T12 thoracic vertebra, indicating chronic vertebral involvement.

 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the left shoulder showed joint destruction, including humeral head and glenoid resorption, widened joint space, periarticular calcifications, and soft tissue inflammation. MRI of the left shoulder revealed extensive bony damage, complete tears in the rotator cuff tendons and complete destruction of the glenoid labrum, glenohumeral ligaments, and biceps-labral anchor. A large, thick-walled fluid collection with peripheral enhancement extended into the intermuscular and intramuscular planes of the rotator cuff muscles. MRI of the spine showed a syrinx extending from the cervico-medullary junction to the thoracic vertebra at T11, consistent with syringomyelia, and a wedge collapse of the vertebral body of T12 thoracic vertebra, indicating chronic vertebral involvement.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Gorham–Stout disease, Septic arthritis, Neuropathic arthropathy, Milwaukee shoulder syndrome, Osteolytic tumour
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 57-year-old diabetic man with chronic bilateral lower limb weakness.  
- Clinical: New mildly painful, firm, non-tender swelling over the left shoulder, limited movement, reduced sensation, and a scar.  
- Radiography: Joint destruction with humeral head and glenoid resorption, widened joint space, periarticular calcifications, and soft tissue inflammation.  
- MRI: Extensive bony damage, complete tears of rotator cuff tendons, destruction of glenoid labrum and glenohumeral ligaments, large thick-walled fluid collection with peripheral enhancement extending into intermuscular and intramuscular planes of rotator cuff muscles.  
- MRI spine: Syrinx from cervico-medullary junction to T11 (syringomyelia), wedge collapse of T12 vertebral body.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1–2 pros/cons:  
1) Gorham–Stout disease  
- Pros: Can cause massive osteolysis and bone resorption.  
- Cons: Usually presents with progressive osteolysis without infection signs, and typically in younger patients; no mention of vascular proliferation or lymphangiomatous changes; here the findings include joint destruction with soft tissue inflammation and fluid collections, more suggestive of infection or neuropathic changes.  

2) Septic arthritis  
- Pros: Joint destruction, soft tissue inflammation, thick-walled fluid collection with peripheral enhancement consistent with abscess or infection.  
- Cons: History of long-standing neuropathy and syringomyelia makes neuropathic joint more likely; the chronicity and joint destruction pattern may be inconsistent with typical septic arthritis presentation; the presence of a syrinx and chronic vertebral collapse also support neuropathic arthropathy.  

3) Neuropathic arthropathy  
- Pros: Classic for painless, destructive joint disease in setting of neuropathy (syringomyelia confirmed by MRI), extensive bony damage, joint destruction with resorption, fluid collections with peripheral enhancement (neuropathic joint can develop joint effusion and inflammation), lack of significant pain (consistent with neuropathy), reduced sensation on exam.  
- Cons: Could be confused with septic arthritis clinically, but clinical picture favors neuropathic origin with confirmed syrinx.  

4) Milwaukee shoulder syndrome  
- Pros: Typically involves elderly patients with massive rotator cuff tears and hydroxyapatite crystal deposition causing joint destruction.  
- Cons: Usually involves elderly women with crystalline arthropathy rather than chronic neuropathic changes; periarticular calcifications present, but presence of syrinx, widespread destruction, and neuropathy less typical.  

5) Osteolytic tumour  
- Pros: Can cause bone destruction.  
- Cons: No evidence of a mass lesion or tumor on imaging; no other tumor signs or systemic symptoms; periarticular infection/inflammation and joint instability favor neuropathic degeneration over tumor.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Neuropathic arthropathy - most consistent with syringomyelia, extensive destruction, and clinical history.  
2) Septic arthritis - secondary consideration due to fluid collection and soft tissue inflammation but less likely given neuropathy.  
3) Milwaukee shoulder syndrome - less likely due to patient demographics and presence of neuropathy.  
4) Gorham-Stout - less likely due to pattern of destruction and infection signs.  
5) Osteolytic tumour - least likely due to absence of tumor evidence.  

</analysis>  
<final>Neuropathic arthropathy</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old male presented with severe triventricular ventriculomegaly and cortical thinning at 35 weeks on prenatal ultrasound. At birth, he had an unusual phenotype, adducted thumbs and poor spontaneous mobility. Progressive ventriculomegaly required ventriculoperitoneal derivation (VPD). Currently, the patient has severe neurological involvement, flaccid paresis with paroxysms of rigidity and epilepsy.
 Imaging Findings: Transfontanelar US and postnatal MRI at 1 day and 1 week, respectively, show severe triventricular hydrocephaly and large massa intermedia (Figures 1a and 1b), aqueductal stenosis, enlarged quadrigeminal plate, corpus callosum agenesis, vermian hypoplasia, severe cortical thinning and diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d).
 
 The latest MRI, at the age of 3 years, before VPD treatment and mild improvement of ventriculomegaly, demonstrates better visualisation of the previous findings (Figure 3a), in addition to diffuse hypoplasia of cerebral white matter, polymicrogyria (Figures 3b and 3c), diencephalosynapsis (Figure 3d) with diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia in a so-called “butterfly-like pattern” (Figures 3e and 3f), and hypoplastic and poorly differentiated hippocampi.
